source.title: Runtime integration compatibility matrix and test-harness specification
source.author: Maya Chen
source.content:
Summary
-------
This document defines a standardized compatibility matrix for runtime components (kernels, sharders, kv-cache, dispatcher, and adapter plugins) and the accompanying test-harness specification used by the eng-serving-runtime team for validation before canary and GA rollouts. The goal is to reduce undetected integration regressions by codifying deterministic tests, telemetry contracts, and release gating criteria.

Why this exists
----------------
- Multiple runtime subsystems evolve independently (kernel optimizations, scheduler policy changes, third-party accelerator adapters). We need a repeatable, automated way to validate interop.
- Current ad-hoc checks miss cross-layer regressions that only surface under mixed workloads or long-lived sessions.
- This spec provides a compatibility matrix format, a test-harness architecture, canonical test suites, and a release gate checklist.

Scope and applicability
----------------------
- Applies to all changes in: kernel binaries, kernel-mapper, request-shaper, kv-cache, session-manager, plugin adapters (CUDA/ROCm/TPU), and the orchestration glue (dispatcher).
- Excludes application-layer prompts and editorial evaluation — those belong to product QA.

Compatibility Matrix (summary table)
-----------------------------------
Columns: component | interface-version | supported-kernel-flags | min-runtime-version | telemetry-contract | acceptance-status
Rows (examples):
- dispatcher | v2 | continuous-batching=true;prefetch=on | 1.18.0 | dispatch.event_v2 | ACCEPTED
- kernel-xform | v1 | tensor-core-quant=true | 1.19.3 | kernel.exec_v1 | INCOMPLETE
- kv-cache | v3 | async-prefetch=true;shard-coalesce=auto | 1.18.5 | kvcache.hit_rate_v3 | ACCEPTED

(Full matrix maintained as CI artifact: s3://redwood-ci/compatibility/matrix/{YYYYMMDD}.csv)

Matrix fields explained
----------------------
- component: logical name (e.g. kernel-xform, dispatcher, kv-cache, sharder, adapter-cuda).
- interface-version: semantic interface version for RPC and metadata (format: vN).
- supported-kernel-flags: comma-separated runtime feature flags required.
- min-runtime-version: minimum serving runtime package version compatible.
- telemetry-contract: primary metric/trace names that consumers rely on.
- acceptance-status: ACCEPTED | REVIEW_REQUIRED | INCOMPATIBLE.

Test-harness architecture (high level)
-------------------------------------
- Orchestrator (harness-coordinator): runs tests end-to-end, coordinates topology (single-shard, multi-shard, heterogeneous pool).
- Injector: generates canonical request surfaces (short gen, long-stream, chat-turns, embedding bursts).
- Shadow runner: runs a mirrored workload against candidate binaries without affecting production.
- Collector: aggregates trace/span logs, kernel traces, and resource telemetry into a canonical test result bundle (.tar.gz).
- Evaluator: rule engine that executes deterministic checks and SLO assertions, produces verdict (PASS/WARN/FAIL).

Test categories and examples
---------------------------
1) Interface contract tests (fast): ensure RPC schemas, headers and error codes remain consistent.
   - Command: ./harness/run_contract_tests --component kernel-xform --iface v1
2) Smoke functional tests (medium): single request correctness and kv-cache warmup.
   - Typical check: response shape and kv-cache hit_rate > 0.25 after 50 warmup requests
3) Mixed-workload fidelity tests (long): run a 30-minute mixed workload (50% short gen, 30% chat, 20% embeddings) to detect drift in scheduling fairness and KV decay.
   - SLOs: median latency <= 40ms for short gen; 95th percentile <= 120ms for chat-turns
4) Stress and degradation tests (chaos): simulate eviction, fragmenting memory, and sudden adapter disconnects. Monitor graceful fallback behaviors and circuit breakers.
5) Reproducibility smoke (deterministic seeds): verify path-sensitive changes produce traceable deterministic differences (seeded RNG).

Telemetry and trace requirements
------------------------------
Every test run must emit the following minimum telemetry fields (as top-level labels/fields):
- run_id (uuid)
- matrix_snapshot_tag (git tag or s3 csv path)
- topology (e.g. 2xA100-80GB / 4xA10)
- workload_profile (name)
- component_versions (comma-separated list)
- deterministic_seed (optional)

Collected metrics (examples):
- kernel.exec_time_ms.p50/p95/p99
- dispatcher.queue_depth.mean/peak
- kvcache.hit_rate
- memory.gpu.used_pct
- adapter.connect_fail_rate

Telemetry schema (YAML snippet)
-----------------------------
```yaml
telemetry_contract_version: v1
required_labels:
  - run_id
  - topology
  - component_versions
metrics:
  kernel.exec_time_ms:
    aggregates: [p50, p95, p99]
  kvcache.hit_rate:
    aggregates: [mean]
  dispatcher.queue_depth:
    aggregates: [mean, max]
```

Evaluator rule examples
-----------------------
- FAIL if kernel.exec_time_ms.p95 increases by > 15% relative to baseline for the same topology.
- WARN if kvcache.hit_rate drops by > 10% from baseline and mean memory.gpu.used_pct increases by > 5 points.
- FAIL if any adapter.connect_fail_rate > 0.5% sustained over 5 minutes.

Running the harness (CLI primer)
--------------------------------
1) Provision a test topology (local or cloud):
   - ./harness/provision_topology --profile a100-minimal --shards 2 --region us-west-2
2) Upload candidate artifacts:
   - ./harness/upload_artifact --component kernel-xform --artifact build/kernel-xform:1.20.0
3) Start an end-to-end run:
   - ./harness/run_end2end --matrix-tag 20280109-alpha --workload mixed-30m --output /tmp/run-20280109
4) Collect results and evaluate:
   - ./harness/collect_and_eval --input /tmp/run-20280109 --rules conf/evaluator-rules.yaml

CI integration and gating
------------------------
- CI produces a compatibility report for every PR that touches subsystems. Minimal gating: contract tests must PASS; smoke tests must PASS. Mixed workload and stress tests must be scheduled on merge for release branches (not necessarily required for every PR).
- Release gate (canary -> GA): to progress from canary to GA a candidate must have 3 independent successful mixed-workload runs across two topology classes (small and large) with PASS verdicts and no FAIL-level rule violations within the last 72 hours.

Onboarding a new component or adapter (step-by-step)
----------------------------------------------------
1) Create a new matrix entry: add row to compatibility CSV and set acceptance-status=REVIEW_REQUIRED.
2) Implement and document telemetry_contract for the component.
3) Author contract tests (unit-level) and place them under harness/tests/contract/{component}.
4) Run local harness with single-shard topology and produce initial artifacts.
5) Submit a PR referencing matrix row and attach initial run result (s3 path).
6) Peer review with owners listed in the matrix; once reviewers approve, run mixed-workload tests and update acceptance-status=ACCEPTED.

Failure modes and mitigation guidance
------------------------------------
- Symptom: kernel.exec_time_ms.p99 spike during mixed workload.
  - Mitigation: reproduce with isolated microbenchmark generator (./harness/microbench --shape short-gen --concurrency 200). Collect kernel traces, increase kernel debug level, and consult kernel-mapper team.
- Symptom: kv-cache hit_rate decays over long chat sessions.
  - Mitigation: enable keystore-prefetch flag, run long-incremental warmup, and verify sharder placement. If regression persists, rollback to previous kv-cache binary and open a regression ticket with run bundle.

Ownership and contact list
--------------------------
- Harness ownership: eng-serving-runtime/infrastructure (oncall: runtime-ci@redwood.internal)
- Evaluator rule ownership: eng-serving-runtime/perf (primary: Diego Morales)
- Kernel and scheduler owners: eng-serving-runtime/kernel-team (primary: Priya Kaur)

Release artifacts and retention
------------------------------
- Every test run produces a result bundle: {run_id}.tar.gz uploaded to s3://redwood-ci/harness-results/{run_id}/. The bundle contains: traces.jsonl, metrics.prom, topology.yaml, manifest.json.
- Retention: keep full bundles for 90 days, aggregated summaries for 540 days.

Appendix A — canonical workload profiles
----------------------------------------
- mixed-30m: 50% short-gen (seq 32), 30% chat-turns (avg seq 250), 20% embeddings (batch size 128).
- short-burst: 95% short-gen with microbursts (100ms inter-arrival) to stress scheduler.

Appendix B — example topology YAML
----------------------------------
```yaml
topology_name: a100-2-shard-hetero
nodes:
  - id: node-01
    gpu: a100-80gb
    shards: [0,1]
  - id: node-02
    gpu: a10-24gb
    shards: [2]
routing: tiered-fallback: true
```

Appendix C — quick checklist for a release engineer
----------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Verify matrix row exists and is up-to-date.
- [ ] Run contract and smoke tests locally.
- [ ] Schedule 3 mixed-workload runs across small & large topologies.
- [ ] Confirm evaluator produced PASS on all runs and no FAIL in last 72h.
- [ ] Upload run bundles to release ticket and tag release.

Notes and future work
---------------------
- Next planned additions: automated delta-analysis to highlight which kernel change likely caused a regression, and a synthetic trace generator that can exercise rare kernel fallback code paths.

Related reading
---------------
- runtime decision graph and observed behaviour catalog (internal)
- benchmarking and perf-suite: runtime deployment sanity checklist

Document history
----------------
- 2026-10-05: Created by Maya Chen (initial draft).
- 2027-03-10: Added evaluator rule examples and telemetry schema (review by Priya Kaur).
- 2028-01-12: Finalized release gating language and retention policy.

source.title: Slot-abort Contract and Graceful Recovery Protocol
source.author: Evelyn Cho
source.body:
SUMMARY

This document specifies the Slot-abort Contract and Graceful Recovery Protocol used by the serving runtime scheduler and kernel plugins. It formalizes the signals, invariants, telemetry, operator runbook, and verification tests required for safe slot abortion, state preservation, and deterministic recovery paths. The goal is to minimize user-visible SLO impact during kernel-level preemption, eviction, or forced termination while keeping the runtime auditable and testable.

INTENDED AUDIENCE
- Runtime kernel and scheduler engineers
- Oncall and SRE teams
- Release engineers validating scheduler behavior
- Performance and profiling teams

GOALS AND NON-GOALS
Goals:
- Define a single contract for how a running request slot may be aborted or suspended.
- Ensure deterministic state handoff for recoverable requests (KV cache, partial logits, decoding state).
- Provide telemetry and traces needed to map aborted slots to user-level tail latency/regressions.
- Standardize operator recovery steps and automated remediation.

Non-goals:
- This is not a doc for admission control or long-term placement policies (see scheduler-policy-language-declarative-profiles).
- Does not change model-serving API semantics for clients; all changes are internal to runtime.

BACKGROUND
Redwood’s serving runtime supports preemptive slot reclamation to maintain capacity and QoS. Historically, ad-hoc slot killing led to state loss, user-visible 5xxs, and noisy post-incident debugging. The Slot-abort Contract aims to normalize this behavior across kernel variants (synchronous kernels, async handoff kernels, multi-GPU shards).

TERMINOLOGY
- Slot: a runtime-execution reservation associated with a request pipeline (may span kvcache + token generation).
- Abort: an intentional termination of a slot prior to natural completion (categories below).
- Suspend: a low-latency serialization of in-flight state to persistent or shared memory for later resume.
- Handshake: the two-phase protocol between scheduler and kernel for safe slot transition.

ABORT CATEGORIES (with examples)
1) Planned Abort (managed): graceful eviction intended for maintenance or preemption windows. Example: drained node for upgrade.
2) Priority Preemptive Abort (policy): scheduler reclaims slot to satisfy a higher-priority request (e.g., enterprise dedicated pool overrides best-effort).
3) Forced Abort (emergency): kernel or host signals unrecoverable state (OOM, NVLINK fatal) and cannot complete handshake.
4) Latency-offload Abort: scheduler chooses to cut low-value tail requests to preserve p50/p95 for paying customers.

SIGNALING AND CONTRACT
- Outgoing abort request from scheduler to kernel: an RPC call AbortSlot(slot_id, reason_code, deadline_ms).
- Kernel must reply within handshake_deadline_ms with one of: ACK_SUSPEND, ACK_TERMINATE, NAK_RETRY.
- If ACK_SUSPEND: kernel emits a resume_token and writes minimal state to the shared suspension store (S3-like or in-cluster memstore).
- If ACK_TERMINATE: kernel attempts a best-effort flush of outputs (streamed partials) then perform local cleanup.
- If no reply before handshake_deadline_ms: scheduler marks slot as ForceKilled and increments ForceKill counter for telemetry.

Default timeouts (configurable):
| Config key | Default | Notes |
| ---------- | ------- | ----- |
| handshake_deadline_ms | 250 | Time allotted for kernel to respond to AbortSlot.
| suspend_write_ms | 100 | Time allowed to persist suspend state.
| resume_call_timeout_ms | 350 | Timeout for resume RPC when restoring a suspended slot.

STATE MODEL AND PERSISTENCE CONTRACT
- Minimal resume_token payload (string) must include: slot_schema_version, kernel_variant, kvcache_keys_hash, partial_decode_cursor, bytes_written_checksum.
- Suspension store contract: put(resume_token, binary_snapshot) must be idempotent and versioned. Failure modes must return explicit error codes (STORE_EWRITE, STOREFULL).
- On resume, kernel must validate token schema_version and kvcache_hash; mismatch -> FAIL_RESUME and surface as recoverable shim error.

TELEMETRY & TRACE SIGNALS (REQUIRED)
Emit standardized tracing annotations and metrics for every abort flow:
- trace/span tags: slot.id, abort.reason, abort.initiator (scheduler/kernel/host), kernel.variant, resume.token.present (true/false).
- counters: slot.abort.total, slot.abort.forcekill, slot.suspend.success, slot.resume.success, slot.resume.fail.
- histograms: suspend_write_latency_ms, resume_latency_ms, abort_handshake_latency_ms.

SLO IMPACT & ALERTING THRESHOLDS
Use these as initial operational thresholds (tunable per pool):
- If slot.abort.forcekill / slot.abort.total > 0.05 for 1h -> SEV2.
- If slot.resume.fail rate > 0.01 over 30m -> SEV2 and rollback any enabling rollout.
- If suspend_write_latency_ms p95 > 200ms -> investigate storage layer; create SEV3.
- Tail-latency correlation: if p99_request_latency increases by >15% coincident with increased slot.aborts -> open incident.

OPERATOR RUNBOOK (investigation and remediation)
1) Detect: receive alert (e.g., slot.resume.fail > 1%).
2) Gather context:
   - kubectl logs of kernel pod(s) with slot.id; retrieve trace span from tracing UI.
   - Query suspension store for resume_token existence: curl -X GET https://suspend-store/api/v1/tokens/<token>.
   - Check node GPU health: nvidia-smi -q; verify ECC/warnings.
3) Triaging steps (ordered):
   a) If ForceKill counter increased across many slots -> suspect handshake_deadline tuning or kernel blocking. Temporarily increase handshake_deadline_ms in canary to 500ms and observe.
   b) If store errors (STORE_EWRITE / STOREFULL) -> free space or fail fast to ensure kernel falls back to ACK_TERMINATE path.
   c) If resume validation fails (schema mismatch) -> mark resume_token as invalid; schedule automated metric to failback to full retry path and roll back kernel variant that introduced schema change.
4) Remediation:
   - For storage latency: route suspend writes to fallback in-cluster memstore and flag for storage team.
   - For kernel stalls: trigger runtime profiling (see PROFILE: gstack capture command below) and scale up auxiliary worker threads.
   - For node-level failures: cordon node and migrate slots via scheduler handoff policy.
5) Communication: create incident with slot.id examples, attach traces and last 50 lines of kernel logs, and tag oncall team.

EXAMPLE COMMANDS AND SNIPPETS
- AbortSlot RPC pseudo-call (go):
  client.AbortSlot(ctx, &pb.AbortRequest{SlotId: "slot-12345", Reason: pb.AbortReason_PREEMPT, DeadlineMs: 250})

- Inspect suspend store:
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $SUSPEND_STORE_TOKEN" https://suspend-store.internal/api/v1/tokens/slot-12345-token | jq '.'

- Quick local debug: capture the last 2MB of kernel logs and upload to S3:
  kubectl logs -n runtime kernel-pod-abc --tail=10000 > /tmp/kernel-logs.txt && aws s3 cp /tmp/kernel-logs.txt s3://redwood-debug/$(hostname)-kernel-logs.txt

TEST SUITE AND BENCHMARKS
We require a set of unit and integration checks for any change to scheduler or kernel:
- Unit tests: verify resume_token schema round-trip, idempotency of store operations, and handshake timeouts.
- Integration tests (CI): simulate Planned Abort, Preemptive Abort, Forced Abort under mixed load and assert percent of forcekills < 0.5% at p95 concurrency.
- Chaos test: orchestrated kernel stall injection (sleep in abort handler) combined with storage latency injection; assert system maintains p95 SLO for high-priority pool.
- Benchmark target: suspend_write_ms p50 < 20ms, p95 < 120ms on production-like suspension store.

INSTRUMENTATION GUIDANCE FOR PROFILING TEAMS
- Add a labeled trace span around AbortSlot/Handshake with sampling rate 100% for first 5 minutes of any canary rollout.
- Attach stack snapshots when handshake latency exceeds 2x median. Use the profiling pipeline:
  - capture: runtime-profiling agent collect --span=abort-handshake --limit=3
  - analyze: upload to internal profiler and mark as abort-handshake digest.

CANARY AND ROLLOUT CHECKLIST (pre-deploy gate)
- Unit and integration tests green.
- Canary rollout (10% of nodes) for 24h with 100% trace sampling on abort flows.
- Verify metrics: slot.suspend.success > 99.5%, slot.resume.success > 99.9% for canary.
- Confirm no increase in customer-facing 5xxs or p99 latency beyond 5% baseline.
- Approve by runtime lead and SRE reviewer.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES FOR KERNEL AUTHORS
- Keep suspend payload minimal; avoid serializing entire kvcache — instead persist key hashes and rely on shared kvcache invalidation/refresh on resume.
- Prefer async suspend writes: serialize-to-local-buffer then background upload; ensure background uploader is bounded and contributes to backpressure metrics.
- For multi-GPU shards: two-phase suspend where master shard coordinates a quorum ack before returning ACK_SUSPEND.

OWNERSHIP, ESCALATION AND ADDITIONAL RESOURCES
- Primary owner: eng-serving-runtime/kernel-scheduling team (SL: Jordan Lee).
- Oncall escalation: eng-infra SRE -> runtime platform -> kernel-scheduling.
- Related pages: tuning guidance for handshake_deadline (see related_pages) and suspend-store SLA page (internal link).

CHANGE HISTORY
- 2026-11-15: Draft authored (Evelyn Cho).
- 2026-12-08: Added telemetry counters and initial SLO thresholds (review by Priya Ramanathan).
- 2027-03-02: Finalized operator runbook and canary checklist; published after two-week canary validation.

APPENDIX A: RESUME TOKEN EXAMPLE (JSON-LIKE)
{
  "token": "slot-v1-abcd1234",
  "slot_schema_version": "1",
  "kernel_variant": "async-shard-3",
  "kvcache_keys_hash": "sha256:...",
  "partial_decode_cursor": "tokpos:1024",
  "crc32": "0x9af3b1"
}

APPENDIX B: QUICK-REFERENCE TABLES
- Abort reason codes:
  - PREEMPT: policy preemption
  - MAINTENANCE: planned maintenance
  - OOM: out-of-memory detected on device
  - HW_FATAL: hardware fatal (nvlink/ecc)
  - SUSPEND_TIMEOUT: kernel did not complete suspend in allowed window

- Handshake outcomes and downstream action:
  - ACK_SUSPEND -> persist state -> scheduler marks slot as Suspended -> possible resume later.
  - ACK_TERMINATE -> best-effort emit partial outputs -> mark request as Aborted.
  - NAK_RETRY -> scheduler may retry abort after backoff.
  - NO_RESPONSE -> ForceKilled; allocate replacement slot and increment alerting counters.

If there are questions about edge cases or proposals to change timing/config, open a design RFC in the kernel-scheduling team space and reference this document.

- Approve by runtime lead and SRE reviewer.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES FOR KERNEL AUTHORS
- Keep suspend payload minimal; avoid serializing entire kvcache — instead persist key hashes and rely on shared kvcache invalidation/refresh on resume.
- Prefer async suspend writes: serialize-to-local-buffer then background upload; ensure background uploader is bounded and contributes to backpressure metrics.
- For multi-GPU shards: two-phase suspend where master shard coordinates a quorum ack before returning ACK_SUSPEND.

OWNERSHIP, ESCALATION AND ADDITIONAL RESOURCES
- Primary owner: eng-serving-runtime/kernel-scheduling team (SL: Jordan Lee).
- Oncall escalation: eng-infra SRE -> runtime platform -> kernel-scheduling.
- Related pages: tuning guidance for handshake_deadline (see related_pages) and suspend-store SLA page (internal link).

CHANGE HISTORY
- 2026-11-15: Draft authored (Evelyn Cho).
- 2026-12-08: Added telemetry counters and initial SLO thresholds (review by Priya Ramanathan).
- 2027-03-02: Finalized operator runbook and canary checklist; published after two-week canary validation.

APPENDIX A: RESUME TOKEN EXAMPLE (JSON-LIKE)
{
  "token": "slot-v1-abcd1234",
  "slot_schema_version": "1",
  "kernel_variant": "async-shard-3",
  "kvcache_keys_hash": "sha256:...",
  "partial_decode_cursor": "tokpos:1024",
  "crc32": "0x9af3b1"
}

APPENDIX B: QUICK-REFERENCE TABLES
- Abort reason codes:
  - PREEMPT: policy preemption
  - MAINTENANCE: planned maintenance
  - OOM: out-of-memory detected on device
  - HW_FATAL: hardware fatal (nvlink/ecc)
  - SUSPEND_TIMEOUT: kernel did not complete suspend in allowed window

- Handshake outcomes and downstream action:
  - ACK_SUSPEND -> persist state -> scheduler marks slot as Suspended -> possible resume later.
  - ACK_TERMINATE -> best-effort emit partial outputs -> mark request as Aborted.
  - NAK_RETRY -> scheduler may retry abort after backoff.
  - NO_RESPONSE -> ForceKilled; allocate replacement slot and increment alerting counters.

If there are questions about edge cases or proposals to change timing/config, open a design RFC in the kernel-scheduling team space and reference this document.

source.title: SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Summary:

This playbook defines the SLA-aware Admission Controller and Backpressure strategy for Redwood's serving runtime. It describes the design, configuration, operational runbook, and recommended testing/benchmarking steps to ensure tenant-level SLOs are respected under mixed workloads and overload scenarios. The playbook targets runtime-admission (service), kernel-scheduler (service), and dispatcher components and is intended for on-call, runtime engineers, and SREs.

Goals and scope:

- Ensure per-tenant and per-route latency SLOs are prioritized when system capacity is constrained.
- Provide deterministic admission behavior that is auditable and testable.
- Define backpressure signals exposed to clients and internal services.
- Offer a practical operational runbook for detecting, mitigating, and rolling back admission policy changes.

Out of scope:

- Model-level quality regressions (handled by Optimize/evals).
- Long-term capacity provisioning (handled by capacity-planning team).

Background and motivation:

As traffic patterns diversify and multi-tenant traffic mixes (short-chat, long-generation, embeddings), the runtime must protect defined SLOs (p95/p99 for latency) for high-priority tenants. Simple FIFO queues allow noisy tenants to consume disproportionate kernel slots and drive SLO violations. An admission controller that incorporates SLA buckets, dynamic scoring, and token budgets prevents this while enabling graceful degradation for best-effort traffic.

Design overview (high level):

1) Components
- runtime-admission: front-line HTTP/gRPC gateway that accepts requests, validates routing, and evaluates admission score.
- kernel-scheduler: allocates kernel slots and issues low-level backpressure when GPU resources are saturated.
- dispatcher: routes admitted requests to worker shards and observes queueing metrics.

2) Signals and inputs to admission decision
- Tenant SLA tier (platinum/gold/silver/best-effort).
- Real-time queue depth and queue wait time (per-tenant).
- Recent kernel eviction/timeout rates and per-shard utilization.
- Token budget consumption (per-tenant per-window).
- Experiment/canary overrides (for rollout testing).

3) Outcome actions
- Admit immediately and assign priority metadata.
- Defer (hold in bounded waiting queue with estimated wait).
- Reject with structured payload (429 with retry-after and fallback model suggestion).
- Route to best-effort fallback pool (quantized/low-cost variant).

Admission algorithm (practical rules):

- Each tenant has a configured SLA bucket: {tier, p95_ms_target, burst_tokens_per_minute, steady_tokens_per_minute}. Example tiers: platinum (p95 120ms), gold (p95 200ms), silver (p95 500ms), best-effort (no p95 SLO).

- Score calculation (simplified):
  score = (tier_weight / (1 + queue_wait_ratio)) * available_token_ratio - penalty(kernel_eviction_rate)
  where values are normalized to [0,1]. The admission threshold is configurable per-service.

- Token budgets are refilled on a leaky-bucket; requests consume budget proportional to estimated token cost (sequence length * model_cost_factor).

Backpressure signals and API:

- kernel-scheduler exposes gRPC stream /v1/backpressure with messages: {shard_id, congestion_level, suggested_drain_ms, affected_models}.
- runtime-admission subscribes and translates congestion_level to per-tenant queue caps and admission thresholds.
- HTTP response header additions for observability and client behavior: X-Redwood-Admission: admitted|deferred|rejected, X-Redwood-Estimated-Wait: <ms>, X-Redwood-Policy-Id: <id>.

SLOs and thresholds (examples):

- Default per-tenant p95 targets (example): platinum 120ms, gold 200ms, silver 500ms.
- Maximum admission queue depth per shard: 512 requests.
- Defer-to-reject pivot: if average estimated wait > 2 * p95_target for tier AND kernel_eviction_rate > 0.05, switch to reject for best-effort.

Configuration examples (YAML):

# admission-policy.yaml
policy:
  name: sla-aware-default
  tiers:
    - name: platinum
      p95_ms: 120
      tier_weight: 1.0
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 60
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 20
    - name: gold
      p95_ms: 200
      tier_weight: 0.7
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 40
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 12
    - name: silver
      p95_ms: 500
      tier_weight: 0.4
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 20
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 6
    - name: best-effort
      p95_ms: 0
      tier_weight: 0.1
      burst_tokens_per_minute: 10
      steady_tokens_per_minute: 2

# kernel backpressure thresholds
kernel:
  congestion_levels:
    - level: 0
      util_pct: 0-70
    - level: 1
      util_pct: 71-85
    - level: 2
      util_pct: 86-95
    - level: 3
      util_pct: 96-100
  level_actions:
    0: no_action
    1: tighten_admission_by: 10%
    2: tighten_admission_by: 25%
    3: reject_non_critical: true

Operational runbook (oncall) - overload detection:

1) Detect: Alerts to pager when aggregated kernel_utilization > 92% for > 60s OR p95 per-tenant latency > configured p95_target for two consecutive 30s windows.
2) Triage: Use dashboards: Runtime -> Admission -> Per-tenant queue depth, Kernel -> shard_utilization and eviction_rate, Dispatcher -> per-route latency heatmap.
3) Short-term mitigation (fast, <5m):
   a) Increase admission strictness: curl -XPOST http://runtime-admission/internal/policy/tighten -d '{"factor":0.25}'
   b) Apply emergency routing to fallback pool: kubectl patch svc dispatcher -p '{"spec": {"selector": {"pool":"fallback"}}}' (use only for best-effort heavy traffic).
   c) If single tenant causing noise, temporarily throttle via: POST /internal/tenants/{id}/throttle with {"rate": "5 rps"}.
4) Medium-term mitigation (5-30m):
   a) Temporarily increase worker replicas for fallback pool if capacity available.
   b) Engage capacity-planning if average utilization remains > 85% for > 30m.
5) Communication: Post status on #runtime-ops with subject [Admission Overload] and include quick metrics snapshot: top 5 tenants by queue depth, kernel_eviction_rate, affected models.

Runbook: admission policy rollback and canary steps:

- Canary rollout matrix: 1% traffic for 15m, observe p95/p99 and kernel_eviction_rate. If no regression, progress to 10% for 30m, then 50% for 60m, then full.
- Rollback command: POST /internal/policy/activate with previous_policy_id or kubectl apply -f admission-policy-previous.yaml.
- Safety gates automated: If p95 for any platinum tenant degrades by >20% vs baseline or kernel_eviction_rate > 3%, automatically rollback to last stable policy.

Diagnostics and telemetry to collect (minimum):

- Per-request: tenant_id, route_id, estimated_token_cost, admission_outcome, estimated_wait_ms, kernel_shard_id.
- Aggregates: per-tenant queue depth, average wait, admitted rate, rejected rate, kernel_eviction_rate per shard.
- Traces: end-to-end trace from runtime-admission to kernel-scheduler with 5% sampling for normal, 50% for overload.

Testing and benchmarking plan:

- Synthetic scenarios in the perf harness: mix of short (<=64 tokens) chat, medium (256 tokens) generation, long (>=1024 tokens) generations, and embedding streams.
- Run 6h soak tests with 70/20/10 mix (short/medium/long) at 1.5x expected peak to validate admission fairness and SLO preservation.
- Validate canary using shadow traffic: compare admitted outcomes and latency deltas against baseline.

Example troubleshooting checklist (quick):

- If many deferred requests but low admitted latency: inspect token budgets and refill rates.
- If kernel_eviction_rate high: check large-sequence tenants and apply temporary model-variant routing.
- If single shard hot: verify shard affinity and consider rebalance (see runtime-shard-placement playbook).

Ownership and escalation:

- Primary owner: runtime-scheduling team (oncall: runtime-sched-rotations).
- Secondary: eng-sre.
- For capacity issues escalate to capacity-planning lead.

Compatibility notes / Release considerations:

- Admission changes are backward compatible for clients; runtime will continue to return structured 429 responses.
- When introducing new header fields (X-Redwood-Estimated-Wait), coordinate with SDK team and document in SDK changelog.

Related artifacts and links:
- Perf harness job: perf/sla-admission-harness (internal CI)
- Telemetry dashboard: /dashboards/runtime-admission-sla
- Kernel backpressure gRPC proto: proto/runtime/backpressure.proto

Appendix: sample 429 response payload (JSON)

{
  "error": "capacity_exceeded",
  "reason": "platinum-sla-protect",
  "retry_after_seconds": 8,
  "suggested_fallback": "gpt4-small-quantized"
}
Notes and future work:

- Research item: incorporate model-level estimated compute (measured in flops or kernel cycles) into token budget calculation.
- Explore reinforcement learning approach for dynamic admission thresholds.

Document history:
- 2027-02-14 created by Priya Natarajan
- 2027-03-01 updated: clarified backpressure API and added automatic safety rollback gate.

- Perf harness job: perf/sla-admission-harness (internal CI)
- Telemetry dashboard: /dashboards/runtime-admission-sla
- Kernel backpressure gRPC proto: proto/runtime/backpressure.proto

Appendix: sample 429 response payload (JSON)

{
  \"error\": \"capacity_exceeded\",
  \"reason\": \"platinum-sla-protect\",
  \"retry_after_seconds\": 8,
  \"suggested_fallback\": \"gpt4-small-quantized\"
}
Notes and future work:

- Research item: incorporate model-level estimated compute (measured in flops or kernel cycles) into token budget calculation.
- Explore reinforcement learning approach for dynamic admission thresholds.

- 2027-03-01 updated: clarified backpressure API and added automatic safety rollback gate.

source.title: Causal Latency Attribution and Signal Enrichment Playbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary and purpose

This playbook defines a repeatable approach for attributing causal sources of request latency in the serving runtime and enriching traces and metrics with synthesized signals that improve root-cause diagnosis. The intended audience is runtime engineers, SREs, and profiling/product teams who investigate tail-latency incidents or validate scheduler/kernel changes against real traffic.

Goals
- Produce an actionable causal hypothesis for >80% of investigated tail-latency incidents within 48 hours.
- Ensure enriched telemetry is available for post-incident analysis and automated detection rules within 6 hours of rollout.
- Keep incremental instrumentation cost increase under 12% of telemetry budget for a standard region.

Scope and non-goals
- Scope: production serving runtime (request surface -> kernel dispatch -> GPU execution -> response), associated request routing, KV cache behavior, and kernel scheduling decisions. Includes streaming, chat, and embedding request shapes.
- Non-goals: model-family quality regressions, client-side application logic, and offline evaluation harnesses. Quality regressions should be investigated using the eval-harness docs.

Signal catalogue (what to collect)
Collect the following signals for each traced request. Where possible, attach a single trace_id and request_id to correlate logs, metrics, and kernel traces.

Primary signals (always-on):
- request_id, trace_id, timestamp_start, timestamp_end
- request_shape: token_count, prompt_length, max_decode_length, batch_size, priority_class
- rpc_spans: inbound_http, auth, router, proxy-layer, runtime_api
- kernel_spans: kernel_start_ns, kernel_queue_ns, kernel_execution_ns, kernel_end_ns, kernel_name
- kvcache_hits: prefix_hits, kvcache_warmup_ms
- gpu_counters: sm_util_pct, mem_alloc_bytes, warp_stall_cycles
- host_metrics: numa_node, cpu_steal_ms, irq_drop_counts

Enrichment signals (high-cardinality; sample and attach heuristics):
- model_variant_hash, quantization_profile_tag, compiled_kernel_version
- scheduling_decision: preempted_by, time_in_priority_queue, admission_reason
- shard_topology: replica_id, numa_zone, local_gpu_id
- batching_context: batch_trigger_reason (timeout/size), constituent_request_ids

Sampling and cost strategy
1) 100% lightweight metrics: counters, duration histograms, basic request metadata. Retain for 14d.
2) 10% deterministic sampling for full tracing of production traffic (uniform across tenants). Retain traces for 30d.
3) Spike sampling: on SLO breach (p95 > configured threshold) automatically increase trace sampling to 50% for impacted routes for the next 1h.
4) High-fidelity kernel traces (GPU perf counters + nvtx) on 0.5% of requests. Escalate to 5% during incident windows.

Instrumentation implementation notes
- Use trace context propagation header X-Redwood-Trace to join router, runtime, and GPU trace collectors. Ensure header is present on internal RPCs and passthrough to private deploys only if configured.
- Kernel instrumentation should use lightweight entry/exit probes with a separate binary that collects NVTX ranges and GPU counters and attaches them by trace_id. Avoid synchronous RPCs from kernel probes; write to local ring buffer and flush asynchronously.
- KV cache metrics should be emitted at two levels: aggregate counter per shard and per-request kvcache_hit fields. Per-request kvcache fields should be added to sampled traces only (see sampling policy).

Causal analysis methodology (step-by-step)
1) Triage: Confirm SLO breach and scope impact (routes, models, customers). Pull p50/p95/p99 histograms and error rates for the last 30m, 2h, 24h.
2) Surface anomalies: Run automated anomaly detectors against enriched signals (kernel queue growth, nvlink error rates, sudden drop in prefix_hits). If detector flags present — mark candidate signals.
3) Correlate with deployment/rollout metadata: map anomalies to rollout windows, kernel rollouts, or canary changes.
4) Build causal graph: for the candidate timeframe, assemble the sequence of dependent spans (router -> admission -> kernel_queue -> kernel_exec -> fetch_kv). Enrich nodes with host and kernel signals.
5) Use counterfactual checks: replay small subset of requests on a mirrored infra (shadow) with alternative scheduler settings or kernel version to test causality.
6) Validate by targeted mitigation: e.g., revert kernel flag, route traffic to fallback kernel, or pin model_variant. Observe expected latency regression rollback.

Investigation runbook (actionable play steps)
- Step A: Gather baseline
  1. Identify impacted route(s) and window: `redwood-observability query \"latency.p95{route=\\\"/v1/generate\\\"}\" --from=-2h --to=now`
  2. Collect traces: filter traces by high-latency (>p99 threshold) and `sample_rate>0`.
  3. Export top 50 kernel_spans ordered by kernel_queue_ns.

- Step B: Quick hypothesis checks (first 30 mins)
  1. Kernel queue growth: check kernel_queue_ns histogram per replica. If > 3x baseline, inspect recent scheduling policy changes.
  2. KV cache coldness: compare prefix_hits ratio for impacted requests vs baseline. If prefix_hits dropped >25%, suspect cache churn.
  3. Host-level contention: check cpu_steal_ms and pci_bandwidth_errors.

- Step C: Escalation and mitigations
  1. If kernel_queue growth is causal, apply emergency mitigation: increase admission_caps for impacted priority_class or route traffic to a less-loaded pool. Use `redwood-admin admission adjust --route /v1/generate --add-cap 20`
  2. For KV cache issues, trigger targeted prefetch warmups for top prompt prefixes or pin sessions to warmed replicas.
  3. For GPU-level stalls, disable the suspect kernel variant and roll back to previously stable kernel: follow runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix.

Template investigation checklist (for incident postmortem)
- Incident window and scope
- Evidence of change (deployments, kernel flags, autoscaler events)
- Key enriched signals correlated with latency spike
- Counterfactual replay results and mitigation outcome
- Root cause classification (scheduling, kernel bug, resource contention, routing)
- Estimated user impact and duration
- Follow-up remediation and owners

Signal enrichment pipeline (design)
- Ingress collection: routers attach request metadata and initial trace headers.
- Short-term enrichment: runtime proxies add kernel and kvcache metrics to sampled traces.
- Asynchronous aggregator: writes enriched records to a trace-enrichment topic (Kafka). An enrichment worker joins kernel counters, host metrics, and batch composition and emits a single enriched trace payload.
- Storage and index: enriched traces are stored in the tracing backend with indexed fields for kernel_name, quantization_profile_tag, and batch_trigger_reason.

Configuration table (operational knobs)
| parameter | default | unit | purpose | owner |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
|trace_sampling.rate|0.10|fraction|Deterministic sampling rate for end-to-end traces|observability-team|
|kernel_trace.high_fidelity_rate|0.005|fraction|Proportion of requests with NVTX + perf counters|serving-runtime|
|spike_increase.multiplier|5|x|Multiplier applied to sampling when SLO breached|sre|
|enrichment.flush_interval|15|seconds|How often kernel probe flushes ring buffer|serving-runtime|
|telemetry_budget_pct|12|percent|Max extra telemetry spend from enrichment|finance|

SLOs and alert thresholds (examples)
- Route /v1/generate: p95 < 240ms, p99 < 900ms. Trigger: sustained p95 breach for 10m.
- Kernel queue growth: alert when median kernel_queue_ns increases 2.5x over baseline for 5m.
- KV cache eviction surge: alert when global prefix_miss_rate increases by +15% in 3m.

Common causal patterns and remediation matrix
- Pattern: kernel_queue growth + high batch sizes -> Remediation: lower max_batch_size for latency-sensitive priorities; enable batch_slicer.
- Pattern: sudden nvlink_errors + mem_alloc spikes -> Remediation: failover replica, reduce concurrent_session_limit, schedule maintenance to repro NIC/driver issues.
- Pattern: prefix_hits drop after rollout -> Remediation: rollback model_variant change or pre-warm KV cache using production-sourced prompt prefixes.

Verification and testing
- Always test instrumentation changes in the staging sandbox with production-synth traffic generator. Follow the runtime-scaling-experiment-protocol.
- Use deterministic replay of sampled traces in a shadow infra to validate mitigation effects before wide rollback.

Rollout checklist for enrichment changes
1. Implement feature flag gating for enrichment fields.
2. Canary to 5% of traffic for 24h; validate no tail impact.
3. Evaluate telemetry cost delta and adjust sampling rates.
4. Promote to 50% for 48h; monitor SLOs and kernel queue metrics.
5. Full rollout with staged increases to 100% over 7 days.

Ownership and contacts
- Primary owner: serving-runtime observability subteam (oncall: runtime-profiling).
- Secondary: eng-sre (oncall: runtime-sre).
- Pager escalation: follow oncall runbook and incident-bridge setup.

Appendix: example commands and traces
- Query top kernel queue contributors (CLI):
```
redwood-traces query --filter 'latency>500ms and route==\"/v1/generate\"' --fields trace_id,kernel_name,kernel_queue_ns --sort kernel_queue_ns --limit 50
```
- Minimal enriched trace JSON (sampled):
```
{
  \"trace_id\": \"abc123\",
  \"request_id\": \"r-984\",
  \"route\": \"/v1/generate\",
  \"kernel_spans\": [{\"kernel_name\":\"matmul_v2\",\"kernel_queue_ns\":4200000,\"kernel_execution_ns\":230000}],
  \"kvcache\": {\"prefix_hits\":0,\"prefix_miss_reason\":\"cold-start\"}
}
```

Related artifacts and reading
- See runtime-telemetry-sampling-cost-aware-profiling-2027 for sampling strategies.
- Refer to runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix-2026 for safe rollback steps.
- Integration notes for private deployments: ensure X-Redwood-Trace header passthrough is enabled in the VPC control plane.

Change log
- 2026-11-12 (Ava Chen): initial draft and prototype enrichment schema.
- 2027-01-18 (Ethan Park): sampling policy updates and spike sampling automation.
- 2027-02-03 (Miguel Alvarez): added example commands and verification checklist.

Document maintenance
This playbook is maintained by the serving-runtime observability subteam. Review cadence: quarterly or after any major kernel/scheduler rollout.

- Query top kernel queue contributors (CLI):
```
redwood-traces query --filter 'latency>500ms and route==\"/v1/generate\"' --fields trace_id,kernel_name,kernel_queue_ns --sort kernel_queue_ns --limit 50
```
- Minimal enriched trace JSON (sampled):
```
{
  \"trace_id\": \"abc123\",
  \"request_id\": \"r-984\",
  \"route\": \"/v1/generate\",
  \"kernel_spans\": [{\"kernel_name\":\"matmul_v2\",\"kernel_queue_ns\":4200000,\"kernel_execution_ns\":230000}],
  \"kvcache\": {\"prefix_hits\":0,\"prefix_miss_reason\":\"cold-start\"}
}
```

Related artifacts and reading
- See runtime-telemetry-sampling-cost-aware-profiling-2027 for sampling strategies.
- Refer to runtime-upgrade-validation-and-canary-matrix-2026 for safe rollback steps.
- Integration notes for private deployments: ensure X-Redwood-Trace header passthrough is enabled in the VPC control plane.

Change log
- 2026-11-12 (Ava Chen): initial draft and prototype enrichment schema.
- 2027-01-18 (Ethan Park): sampling policy updates and spike sampling automation.
- 2027-02-03 (Miguel Alvarez): added example commands and verification checklist.

Document maintenance
This playbook is maintained by the serving-runtime observability subteam. Review cadence: quarterly or after any major kernel/scheduler rollout.

source.title: Observability → Cost Signal Mapping and Ownership Roster
source.author: Hannah Chen
source.content:
Overview:
This document defines a pragmatic mapping between runtime observability signals and the cost / SLO consequences they imply. It consolidates ownership for each signal, provides concrete alert thresholds, investigative playbooks, and sample queries to accelerate incident triage and cost attribution. The goal is to make cost-impacting signals actionable for oncall, SREs, runtime engineers, and product stakeholders without repetitive context-switching.

Why this exists:
- We observe recurring incidents where telemetry is present but responsibility and cost mapping are unclear.
- Kernel-level events (slow kernels, preemption, fragmentation) generate telemetry that must be translated into cost signals (excess GPU-hours, increased tail-latency retries, failed inference units).
- This doc standardizes naming, ownership, and initial remediation steps so pager fatigue and finger-pointing decrease.

Signal taxonomy (high level):
1) Resource & capacity signals
   - gpu_util_pct: instantaneous GPU utilization per node
   - gpu_steal_pct: fraction of GPU cycles lost to eviction/preemption
   - host_mem_pressure: OS-level memory pressure on serving nodes
2) Kernel & scheduler signals
   - kernel_latency_p50/p95/p99: per-kernel execution latencies
   - kernel_queue_depth: queued kernel invocations waiting on scheduler slot
   - kernel_handoff_count: number of handoffs between kernels/plugins during a request
3) KV / model caching signals
   - kvcache_hit_rate: ratio of requests served from KV cache
   - kvcache_warmup_time: average time to populate cache after model swap or scale event
4) Request surface & fidelity signals
   - observed_brevity: fraction of responses truncated due to eviction or timeout
   - model_variant_switch_rate: rate of runtime fallback to lower-fidelity variants
5) Cost-correlated aggregates
   - token_cost_rate: modeled $/token over 1m windows (see cost-model section)
   - excess_gpu_hours: measured GPU-hours above steady-state baseline

Signal → Cost mapping (annotated table):
Note: This table is intended to be human-readable. For programmatic mapping, see the appendix sample SQL/PromQL.

| Observability Signal | Immediate Cost Impact | How to interpret | Primary Owner | Escalation owner |
|----------------------|-----------------------|------------------|---------------|------------------|
| gpu_steal_pct > 5% (5m) | Excess GPU-hours due to preemption (retries + lower throughput) | Often caused by noisy neighbor or infra reclaim | infra-fleet (eng-infra) | eng-sre-oncall |
| kernel_latency_p99 increase by > 2x (30m) | Increased tail latency -> client-side retries -> higher token_cost_rate | Could be kernel regression or scheduler congestion | serving-runtime (eng) | runtime-incident-lead |
| kvcache_hit_rate < 70% (5m) | Increased per-request compute -> higher unit cost | May indicate cache invalidation after deploy or model swap | runtime-caching (eng) | eng-platform |
| model_variant_switch_rate spike | Switches to cheaper variants may hide SLO regressions but lower revenue/quality | Intentional during degradation, but unexpected spikes indicate routing misconfig | routing-and-fallbacks (eng) | product-ops |
| host_mem_pressure sustained | Node-level OOMs, increased evictions, workload redistribution -> excess infra spend | Investigate recent deploys, container limits | node-ops (eng-infra) | eng-sre |

Ownership roster (quick reference):
- serving-runtime (primary): Hannah Chen, Arjun Patel
- infra-fleet (primary): Miguel Ortega
- runtime-caching (primary): Priya Natarajan
- routing-and-fallbacks (primary): Carlos Vega
- eng-sre (escalation/human ops): Devon Brooks
- product-ops (business impact): Maya Singh

Alert thresholds and rationale (recommended starting points):
- gpu_steal_pct (5m avg) >= 5% -> Severity: P2. Rationale: sustained steal causes throughput loss across tenants; triage within 30m.
- kernel_latency_p99 (per-model) >= baseline * 2 for 30m -> Severity: P1 (if traffic > 5% of production) or P2 otherwise.
- kvcache_hit_rate drop >= 15 percentage points for 10m -> Severity: P2.
- model_variant_switch_rate > 3x baseline and > 0.5% absolute -> Severity: P2 (investigate misrouting or rollout).

Sample triage runbook (first 30 minutes):
1) On alert, capture current window traces and the following dashboards: kernel-latency-per-model, gpu-util-and-steal, kvcache-heatmap, routing-variant-metrics.
2) Run quick attribution queries:
   - PromQL: avg_over_time(gpu_steal_pct[10m]) by (node)
   - SQL: SELECT model, count(*) as requests, avg(kernel_latency_p99) FROM traces WHERE ts >= now() - interval '10 minutes' GROUP BY model ORDER BY requests DESC LIMIT 20;
3) If kernel_latency_p99 is elevated and confined to a subset of models: tag the suspect model(s) and check recent model-rollout commits and quantization profiles.
4) If gpu_steal_pct is high across nodes: check cloud provider preemption events, node autoscaler logs, and recent infra changes.
5) If kvcache_hit_rate is low across many models: verify kvcache warmup logs, recent cache eviction policies, and any config change in cache TTLs.
6) Document findings in the incident ticket and implement temporary mitigations (scale out read cache nodes, pin model variants, shift traffic) before long-term fix.

Cost model quick reference (how signals feed $):
- token_cost_rate = base_token_cost * (1 + kernel_overhead_factor + retry_factor)
- excess_gpu_hours = sum_over_nodes(steal_pct * node_gpu_capacity * window_hours)
Example calculation (pseudocode):
  base_token_cost = $0.0000005
  kernel_overhead_factor = kernel_latency_p50 / baseline_p50 - 1
  retry_factor = observed_retries / successful_requests
  token_cost_rate = base_token_cost * (1 + kernel_overhead_factor + retry_factor)

Configuration table (what to hook up where):
| Signal Name | Collector | Typical retention | Dashboard | Alert channel |
|-------------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| gpu_steal_pct | node-exporter + agent | 30d (raw), 90d (rollups) | infra/gpu/steal | #infra-ops (PagerDuty P2) |
| kernel_latency_p99 | trace-collector (open-telemetry) | 90d (indexes), 365d (samples) | runtime/kernel/latency | #runtime-oncall (PagerDuty P1/P2) |
| kvcache_hit_rate | metrics-proxy | 60d | runtime/cache/hit-rate | #runtime-oncall |
| model_variant_switch_rate | routing-metrics | 90d | routing/variant-switches | #product-ops (notify) |

Signal enrichment examples (useful tags):
- model=*, model_version=*, kernel_name=*, node=*, tenant_id=*, request_path=*, quant_profile=*

Sample PromQL / SQL snippets (copy-paste friendly):
- PromQL: topk(10, sum by (model) (increase(kernel_exec_count[10m]) * increase(kernel_latency_sum[10m]) / increase(kernel_exec_count[10m])))
- SQL (clickhouse-like):
  SELECT model, percentileExact(0.99)(kernel_latency_ms) AS p99, count() AS requests
  FROM traces
  WHERE ts >= now() - INTERVAL 15 MINUTE
  GROUP BY model
  ORDER BY p99 DESC
  LIMIT 50;

Runbook: when to escalate to runtime-incident-lead (P1 path):
1) Any kernel_latency_p99 regression for a model responsible for >5% of token_cost_rate for >30 minutes.
2) A sudden cross-tenant spike in gpu_steal_pct causing >10% throughput degradation across multiple tenants.
3) Sustained kvcache mass-invalidations affecting >10 models and causing >20% token_cost_rate increase.

Ownership & playbook contact matrix (condensed):
- infra-fleet: node preemptions, GPU allocations, cloud autoscaler interactions. Contact: @Miguel Ortega, #infra-ops.
- serving-runtime: kernel performance, scheduler policies, per-model kernel regressions. Contact: @Hannah Chen, #runtime-oncall.
- runtime-caching: kvcache policies, TTLs, cache node scaling. Contact: @Priya Natarajan, #runtime-cache.
- routing-and-fallbacks: model variant routing, canary logic, fallback mapping. Contact: @Carlos Vega, #routing.

Rollout checklist for new kernel or scheduler change:
1) Preflight: 72h synthetic run with representative traffic (see synthetic-corpus-runbook).
2) Canary: 0.5% traffic for 2h, collect kernel_latency_p50/p95/p99, gpu_steal_pct.
3) Observe: no p99 regression > 1.3x baseline and no increase in gpu_steal_pct > 2 percentage points.
4) Gradual ramp: 5% -> 25% -> 100% with automated abort if any alert fires.
5) Post-mortem: mandatory if any alert triggered during rollout.

Appendix A — Example incident timeline (realistic condensed case):
- 10:03: Alert: kernel_latency_p99 for model X increased 2.8x (30m window). PagerDuty P1 triggered.
- 10:06: Oncall collects traces; finds kernel_handoff_count spike that correlates with a new kernel plugin rollout.
- 10:14: Mitigation: rollback new plugin for model X. p99 returns to baseline by 10:22.
- 10:45: Post-incident owner: serving-runtime. Root cause: plugin introduced unexpected lock in KV prefetch path.

Appendix B — Glossary (short):
- kernel_handoff: an executed context switch between kernel implementations or accelerator adapters during a single request.
- gpu_steal: cycles lost due to preemption/reclamation or other system-level scheduling.
- kvcache: prefix/KV cache used to avoid recomputing model context.

Notes and future work:
- Integrate a machine-readable mapping (YAML/JSON) into the alerts pipeline so that runbooks are surfaced in the alert payload.
- Build a cost-attribution job that regularly computes token_cost_rate and posts to the cost dashboard.
- Add correlation widgets in dashboards that join traces to infra events (node reboots, preemption).

Related resources:
- runtime/kernel-telemetry-annotation-guidelines (team-wiki)
- runtime-oncall-playbook (runbooks)
- synthetic-corpus-runbook (benchmarking suite)

If you find gaps in ownership or unclear signal definitions, please open a ticket with label "observability:signal-mapping" and tag @Hannah Chen for triage.

3) Sustained kvcache mass-invalidations affecting >10 models and causing >20% token_cost_rate increase.

Ownership & playbook contact matrix (condensed):
- infra-fleet: node preemptions, GPU allocations, cloud autoscaler interactions. Contact: @Miguel Ortega, #infra-ops.
- serving-runtime: kernel performance, scheduler policies, per-model kernel regressions. Contact: @Hannah Chen, #runtime-oncall.
- runtime-caching: kvcache policies, TTLs, cache node scaling. Contact: @Priya Natarajan, #runtime-cache.
- routing-and-fallbacks: model variant routing, canary logic, fallback mapping. Contact: @Carlos Vega, #routing.

Rollout checklist for new kernel or scheduler change:
1) Preflight: 72h synthetic run with representative traffic (see synthetic-corpus-runbook).
2) Canary: 0.5% traffic for 2h, collect kernel_latency_p50/p95/p99, gpu_steal_pct.
3) Observe: no p99 regression > 1.3x baseline and no increase in gpu_steal_pct > 2 percentage points.
4) Gradual ramp: 5% -> 25% -> 100% with automated abort if any alert fires.
5) Post-mortem: mandatory if any alert triggered during rollout.

Appendix A — Example incident timeline (realistic condensed case):
- 10:03: Alert: kernel_latency_p99 for model X increased 2.8x (30m window). PagerDuty P1 triggered.
- 10:06: Oncall collects traces; finds kernel_handoff_count spike that correlates with a new kernel plugin rollout.
- 10:14: Mitigation: rollback new plugin for model X. p99 returns to baseline by 10:22.
- 10:45: Post-incident owner: serving-runtime. Root cause: plugin introduced unexpected lock in KV prefetch path.

Appendix B — Glossary (short):
- kernel_handoff: an executed context switch between kernel implementations or accelerator adapters during a single request.
- gpu_steal: cycles lost due to preemption/reclamation or other system-level scheduling.
- kvcache: prefix/KV cache used to avoid recomputing model context.

Notes and future work:
- Integrate a machine-readable mapping (YAML/JSON) into the alerts pipeline so that runbooks are surfaced in the alert payload.
- Build a cost-attribution job that regularly computes token_cost_rate and posts to the cost dashboard.
- Add correlation widgets in dashboards that join traces to infra events (node reboots, preemption).

Related resources:
- runtime/kernel-telemetry-annotation-guidelines (team-wiki)
- runtime-oncall-playbook (runbooks)
- synthetic-corpus-runbook (benchmarking suite)

If you find gaps in ownership or unclear signal definitions, please open a ticket with label "observability:signal-mapping" and tag @Hannah Chen for triage.

source.title: Quantization-Induced Timing Investigation Handbook
source.author: Aisha Thompson
source.body:
Summary

This handbook codifies a standard investigation and mitigation workflow for latency regressions and high-variance tail behavior that correlate with quantized model kernels or mixed-precision execution paths. It is targeted at SREs, runtime engineers, and applied ML engineers responsible for rollout safeties and kernel scheduling.

Scope

- Applies to inference serving instances using int8/4/2 quantization paths, mixed FP16/FP32 kernels, and runtime kernel switching (auto, fallback, or staged rollout).
- Covers production incidents and reproducible lab investigations.
- Excludes model-quality hallucination investigation (see quality-guardrails space).

Symptoms that should trigger this playbook

1) Sudden increase in p99/p999 latency after a quantization rollout or kernel update.
2) Increased variance in kernel execution durations in kernel-duration histograms.
3) Elevated kernel error rates (timeouts/retries) coincident with quantized model variants.
4) Autoscaler thrash or queue buildup when quantized kernels are used.

Key telemetry to collect (minimum set)

- Per-request trace with kernel hints: kernel.name, kernel.variant, kernel.duration_ns, sequence_length, batch_size, tenant_id
- Percentile latency (p50/p95/p99/p999) for request surface and per-kernel breakdown
- Kernel-duration histogram (log-buckets, microsecond resolution)
- GPU occupancy / SM utilization per kernel (sampled)
- CUDA error counters and pending stream counts
- KV-cache hit/miss rates and cache-priming latencies
- Memory allocation / OOM events and fragmentation metrics

Recommended Prometheus alerting signals (examples)

- Alert: Quantization_Kernel_P99_Increase
  Expr: increase(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket{kernel_variant=~".*quant.*"}[5m]) > 0 and histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket{kernel_variant=~".*quant.*"}[5m])) by (le)) > 0.8

- Alert: Kernel_Error_Surge
  Expr: increase(runtime_kernel_errors_total{kernel_variant=~".*quant.*"}[1m]) / increase(runtime_requests_total[1m]) > 0.01

Investigation checklist (ordered steps)

1) Baseline verification
   - Confirm regression window and affected release / deployment (Git commit, container image, kernel package).
   - Query traffic slices: which model variants, routes, customer tiers are affected? Use time-windowed query and compare control vs. quantized slices.

2) Triage with shadowed traces
   - Enable shadow logging for a representative slice (non-prod routing or shadow channel) to collect per-kernel traces without impacting production.
   - If possible, mirror a small percentage (1–5%) of live requests to a canary pool running the quantized kernel for direct comparison.

3) Kernel-level profiling
   - Capture per-GPU kernel traces for the affected timeframe. Recommended tools and commands: 
     - Nsight Systems (batch capture): nsight-sys --trace=cuda --capture-range=pid:12345-12345 -o capture.qdrep
     - CUPTI / nvtx annotations: ensure runtime emits NVTX begin/end for kernel sections.
     - Lightweight sampler: collect 1s-5s stack-samples using perf or nsight-sys sampling to get occupancy hotspots.

4) Correlate kernel durations with sequence shape and batching
   - Use trace queries to pivot on sequence_length and aggregated batch_size. Look for skew: e.g., quantized kernel latency regresses for variable sequence segments > 512 tokens.

5) Check memory / dispatch interactions
   - Inspect memory allocation timelines for spikes that align with kernel duration climbs; quantized kernels may change workspace sizing.

6) Regression isolation using controlled replay
   - Re-run a production traffic slice through a replay harness in the lab that can toggle quantized vs. fp16 kernels deterministically. Measure per-kernel latencies and jitter.

7) If reproducible, instrument microbenchmarks
   - Create a microbenchmark (single request, controlled sequence length, controlled batch sizes) and run across kernel variants. Collect kernel duration histograms and occupancy.

8) Reach mitigation decision
   - If quantized kernel causes unfixable latency or tail variance (per SLO table below), trigger rollback policy. Otherwise, apply mitigations below and continue rollout in small cohorts.

SLO gating and decision table

| Severity | Condition | Action (owner) | Evidence required |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Sev 1 | p99 increase > 2x baseline AND errors > 0.5% | Immediate rollback to previous kernel (runtime-owner) | Pre/post histograms, trace slices, replay results |
| Sev 2 | p99 increase 1.2–2x OR p999 increase > 1.5x | Pause rollout and start triage (oncall + runtime) | Microbenchmark and kernel-level trace |
| Sev 3 | Minor p95 increase (<1.2x) but stable errors | Continue rollout with throttling and observability | Canary telemetry over 24h |

Mitigations and quick fixes

- Rollback: Revert to previous kernel / quantization profile via rollout CLI and route traffic away from canary pool.
- Kernel hinting: Pin to fallback kernel for high-seq-length cohort via routing rule (example below).
- Batching policy change: Force max-batch-size limit to reduce variation for mixed kernels.
- Prefetching: Increase async KV-cache prefetch window when memory allows.

Routing rule example (YAML fragment)

route_overrides:
  - name: pin-high-seq-to-fallback
    match:
      - field: request.sequence_length
        op: ">\""
        value: "1024"
    action:
      - set_kernel_variant: fallback-fp16

Runtime rollout CLI snippets

# List active kernel variants
redwood runtime kernels list --pool=canary
# Pin route to old kernel
redwood runtime route override apply --route-id=route-123 --kernel=fallback-fp16 --reason="rollback-quant-2026-11-05"

Controlled experiment: A/B quantized vs fallback (small cohort)

1) Select representative tenant cohort (low-volume but realistic shapes).
2) Create two canary pools: A (quantized) and B (fallback). Mirror identical traffic; ensure statistical power (minimum 5k requests per cohort).
3) Collect: per-request latencies, kernel durations, GPU utilization, and error rates.
4) Run a two-sample test on p99 and p999. Use bootstrap if distribution non-parametric.
5) If A shows significant tail degradation (p99 diff > 20% and p-value < 0.01), fail the gate.

Persistent-fix paths (when kernel is culprit)

- Kernel tune: collaborate with kernel-team to adjust tiling, loop unrolling thresholds, or quantization packing order.
- Workspace sizing: change temporary buffer allocation strategy to avoid fragmentation spikes.
- Scheduler hints: add admission control to avoid mixing large sequence requests with tight batches on quantized kernels.

Artifact and evidence collection policy

- Store all captures (nsight qdrep, trace logs) in S3 under s3://redwood-runtime-debug/{incident-id}/captures with read ACL to runtime-team and oncall.
- Include a triage.md summary with key graphs and the replay harness config.

Runbook checklist (quick actions for oncall)

1) Acknowledge alert and page runtime-owner.
2) Attach incident ID and take a short sample capture (30s) from impacted node(s).
3) Enable shadow capture for affected routes.
4) If clear regression and high customer impact: execute rollback steps.
5) If uncertain: create replay job and run microbenchmarks in the lab.

Ownership and escalation

- First responder: serving-runtime oncall (rotation: runtime-oncall@redwood.ai).
- Secondary: kernel-team lead (Sana Reddy).
- Escalate to SRE manager when rollback affects > 10% traffic or revenue-bearing tenants.

Appendix: Useful queries and artifacts

- Example trace store query (SQL-style):
  SELECT request_id, kernel_variant, histogram_quantile(0.99, kernel_duration_histogram) AS p99_kernel FROM traces WHERE timestamp BETWEEN TIMESTAMP_SUB(now(), INTERVAL 10 MINUTE) AND now() AND kernel_variant LIKE '%quant%' GROUP BY kernel_variant;

- Microbenchmark YAML example:
  bench_name: quant-510-test
  sequence_length: 510
  batch_sizes: [1,2,4,8]
  kernel_variants: [quant-int8, fallback-fp16]
  runs: 1000

- Preferred artifact naming: {incident-id}_{node-or-pool}_{capture-type}_{timestamp} e.g. INC-20261105_canary1_nsight_qdrep_20261105T0921.qdrep

Related reading

- Runtime profiling guidelines: /eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/runtime-profiling-playbook-guidelines-2025
- Quantization rollout safety: /eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/quantization-rollout-safety-and-quality-gate-playbook-2026

Contact and maintenance

- Doc owner: Aisha Thompson (aisha@redwood.ai)
- Next review cycle: 2027-06-01

End of handbook

source.title: Adaptive Telemetry Bandwidth Management and Trace Priority Playbook
source.author: Maya Gupta
source.content:
Summary:
This playbook defines the Adaptive Telemetry Bandwidth Management (ATBM) policy and Trace Priority model used by the Serving Runtime to keep observability useful under constrained telemetry budgets. It covers objectives, priority classes, runtime admission & backpressure behavior, operational controls, incident runbook, benchmark validation steps, rollout checklist, and known caveats.

Goals:
- Ensure high-value traces and metrics remain available when telemetry bandwidth is constrained.
- Keep telemetry costs bounded while preserving the signal required for production debugging and SLA forensics.
- Provide deterministic runtime behavior and simple operational levers for on-call and SRE teams.

Background:
Serving runtime produces three telemetry surfaces: kernel traces (per-kernel microtraces), request-execution traces (end-to-end per-request spans), and aggregated metrics/derived signals. Left unchecked, high request volumes or noisy customers can saturate export pipelines and storage.

Terminology:
- Telemetry budget (B_total): total egress capacity for observability (Mbps) assigned per region/cluster.
- Priority class: categorization that determines sampling and propagation policy for individual traces/spans.
- Trace score: runtime estimate (0-100) indicating the expected debugging value of a trace (hotpath errors, SLO breaches, anomalous latencies).
- Backpressure window: sliding interval (default 60s) used to smooth admission decisions.

Priority classes and default policies:
1) critical
- Description: request traces associated with production 5xx errors, asserted SLA violations, security alerts.
- Sampling rule: retain 100% of spans and full kernel trace payloads. No throttling.
- Budget reserve: 35% of B_total.

2) high
- Description: requests with tail-latency > P95 + configured delta or requests from enterprise customers flagged for enhanced observability.
- Sampling rule: adaptive 50-100% depending on available budget; full parameterized spans but kernel trace sampling at 50%.
- Budget target: 30% of remaining after critical reserve.

3) medium
- Description: standard production requests within normal latency bounds but useful for performance regression hunts.
- Sampling rule: probabilistic sampling 5-20%; span size limited to summary-only (drop heavy payloads); kernel traces sampled at 10%.
- Budget target: 20%.

4) low
- Description: debug-level traces (developer sandboxes, low-importance customers, non-prod traffic mirrored into prod path).
- Sampling rule: aggressive downsampling 0.1-2%; only metrics emitted, traces triaged to nearline storage.
- Budget target: 10%.

Adaptive allocation algorithm (high level):
- Inputs: B_total, historical per-class consumption (windowed 60s), live-inflight queue lengths, trace_score distribution.
- Step 1: allocate reserves for critical class (min_quanta) before any adaptive adjustment.
- Step 2: compute available budget B_avail = max(0, B_total - consumed_reserved).
- Step 3: for each class (high, medium, low), compute target allocation proportional to configured weights and bounded by recent consumption trends. Use exponential smoothing alpha=0.2.
- Step 4: enforce class-level throttles: if class consumption > allocation, apply the admission policy that first reduces kernel trace fidelity, then span payloads, then sampling rate.

Sample runtime config (JSON/YAML style):
- telemetry_budget_mbps: 120
- backpressure_window_s: 60
- priority_classes:
  - name: critical
    weight: 0.35
    min_reserved_mbps: 40
  - name: high
    weight: 0.30
    max_fidelity: full
  - name: medium
    weight: 0.20
    sampling_range: [0.05, 0.20]
  - name: low
    weight: 0.15
    sampling_range: [0.001, 0.02]

Operational controls and CLI examples:
- View current telemetry budget and class consumption (prometheus style):
  $ redwoodctl telemetry status --cluster us-west-2
  Output: B_total=120Mbps, used=88Mbps, critical=12Mbps, high=40Mbps, medium=30Mbps, low=6Mbps, headroom=32Mbps

- Temporarily increase B_total during incident (requires SRE approval):
  $ redwoodctl telemetry set-budget --cluster us-west-2 --mbps 200 --reason incident: high trace loss

- Force-reclassify a request for higher fidelity (debug helper):
  $ redwoodctl traces reclassify --request-id req-1234 --priority high --ttl 300

Instrumentation guidance for SDKs:
- Emit a lightweight pre-span with score hints: {error=true, latency_ms=..., user_tier=enterprise} to help runtime quickly compute trace_score without serializing full payloads.
- Attach kernel trace hints to spans to allow prioritized capture without full payload until admitted.

Runbook: telemetry-saturation-detection and mitigation
Detection (alerts):
- Alert: telemetry_egress_utilization > 85% for 3m AND telemetry_headroom < 15% for 3m
- Alert: kernel_trace_drop_rate > 1% for 5m (indicates fidelity loss)

Immediate actions (on-call):
1) Acknowledge alert and notify SRE rotation and runtime oncall via #runtime-operator.
2) Run: redwoodctl telemetry status --cluster <cluster> and capture output to incident channel.
3) If B_total < expected for current traffic, check recent config changes: redwoodctl audit tail --service telemetry --since 1h
4) If consumption spikes are attributable to a named customer/route, apply temporary route-level sampling cap:
   redwoodctl telemetry route-update --route-id route-42 --sampling 0.05 --until 2026-11-02T18:00:00Z --reason protect headroom
5) If the spike is system-wide and urgent, increase B_total conservatively (to avoid cost shock): redwoodctl telemetry set-budget --mbps +50
6) If increasing B_total is not permitted, enact degradation order: reduce low->medium->high sampling and drop kernel payloads per the adaptive policy.
7) Post-incident: file an incident ticket, run the Benchmark Validation section below, and set retrospective action items.

Benchmark validation procedures (to run in staging before rolling to prod):
- Goal: Verify adaptive allocation does not starve critical traces and maintains headroom tolerance under mixed workloads.
- Steps:
  1) Deploy telemetry agent with configured B_total and backpressure_window.
  2) Generate synthetic mixed workload: 60% medium traffic, 30% high (inject 5xx at 0.5%) and 10% low bursts. Use workload-driver: ./perf-driver --profile mixed-telemetry --rps 10k
  3) Assert metrics: critical trace retention >= 99.9%, high-class kernel trace fidelity >= 60% when B_total >= 1.25x baseline consumption, headroom >= 10%.
  4) Run cost/egress measurement for 4h and capture percentiles and p95/p99 spike events.
  5) Run edge-case test: sudden 10x low-priority traffic surge; check that critical traces are unaffected and low-class throttles to minimum sampling.

Rollout checklist (canary -> 25% -> 100%):
- Create canary cluster with 5% of traffic and enable ATBM with default weights.
- Monitor for 24 hours on these signals: telemetry_egress_utilization, kernel_trace_drop_rate, trace_sla_missed_count, customer_debug_requests.
- Run benchmark validation against canary for 1h daily during rollout.
- Collect developer feedback and adjust sampling_range and weights as needed.
- Update SDKs with trace_score hint release notes and deprecate old telemetry flags.

Known caveats and open items:
- Heavy payload suppression: dropping span payloads for medium class can impact downstream auto-instrumentation correlation. We need to ensure unique IDs survive suppression.
- Heterogeneous clusters: B_total per-cluster model assumes consistent egress capacity; split clusters by network limiting devices require per-node calibration.
- Metrics lag: smoothing window alpha impacts reactive speed — lower alpha = slower reaction but fewer oscillations. Default alpha=0.2 is conservative; experiments recommended per-cluster.
- Security: reclassification CLI commands are audited but elevated permissions are required; misuse could expose more telemetry than allowed.

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime telemetry policy owner: Maya Gupta (eng-serving-runtime)
- Primary SRE contact: Jonah Kim
- Observability platform: Ava Chen
- Slack channels: #runtime-operator, #observability-ops
- Pager escalation: see oncall roster in /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-roster

Appendix A — example Prometheus queries
- current_egress_usage_mbps:
  sum(rate(telemetry_egress_bytes_total[1m])) / 1024 / 1024 * 8
- kernel_trace_drop_rate:
  rate(kernel_trace_dropped_total[5m]) / rate(kernel_trace_emitted_total[5m])

Appendix B — simulation artifacts to attach to postmortems
- perf-driver config files used
- redwoodctl telemetry status logs
- Sampling heatmaps (per-route, per-customer) for the incident window

Revision history:
- 2026-03-15: Draft created
- 2026-05-04: Added adaptive allocation algorithm and runbook steps
- 2026-11-02: Expanded benchmark validation and rollout checklist

Related pages and references:
- Profiling sampling playbooks and cost-correlation (see related_pages)
- SDK instrumentation guidelines (internal SDK docs)

End of playbook.

title: Adaptive Telemetry Bandwidth Management and Trace Priority Playbook

Summary:
This playbook defines the Adaptive Telemetry Bandwidth Management (ATBM) policy and Trace Priority model used by the Serving Runtime to keep observability useful under constrained telemetry budgets. It covers objectives, priority classes, runtime admission & backpressure behavior, operational controls, incident runbook, benchmark validation steps, rollout checklist, and known caveats.

Goals:
- Ensure high-value traces and metrics remain available when telemetry bandwidth is constrained.
- Keep telemetry costs bounded while preserving the signal required for production debugging and SLA forensics.
- Provide deterministic runtime behavior and simple operational levers for on-call and SRE teams.

Background:
Serving runtime produces three telemetry surfaces: kernel traces (per-kernel microtraces), request-execution traces (end-to-end per-request spans), and aggregated metrics/derived signals. Left unchecked, high request volumes or noisy customers can saturate export pipelines and storage.

Terminology:
- Telemetry budget (B_total): total egress capacity for observability (Mbps) assigned per region/cluster.
- Priority class: categorization that determines sampling and propagation policy for individual traces/spans.
- Trace score: runtime estimate (0-100) indicating the expected debugging value of a trace (hotpath errors, SLO breaches, anomalous latencies).
- Backpressure window: sliding interval (default 60s) used to smooth admission decisions.

Priority classes and default policies:
1) critical
- Description: request traces associated with production 5xx errors, asserted SLA violations, security alerts.
- Sampling rule: retain 100% of spans and full kernel trace payloads. No throttling.
- Budget reserve: 35% of B_total.

2) high
- Description: requests with tail-latency > P95 + configured delta or requests from enterprise customers flagged for enhanced observability.
- Sampling rule: adaptive 50-100% depending on available budget; full parameterized spans but kernel trace sampling at 50%.
- Budget target: 30% of remaining after critical reserve.

3) medium
- Description: standard production requests within normal latency bounds but useful for performance regression hunts.
- Sampling rule: probabilistic sampling 5-20%; span size limited to summary-only (drop heavy payloads); kernel traces sampled at 10%.
- Budget target: 20%.

4) low
- Description: debug-level traces (developer sandboxes, low-importance customers, non-prod traffic mirrored into prod path).
- Sampling rule: aggressive downsampling 0.1-2%; only metrics emitted, traces triaged to nearline storage.

- backpressure_window_s: 60

    sampling_range: [0.001, 0.02]

  Output: B_total=120Mbps, used=88Mbps, critical=12Mbps, high=40Mbps, medium=30Mbps, low=6Mbps, headroom=32Mbps

- Force-reclassify a request for higher fidelity (debug helper):

Detection (alerts):
- Alert: telemetry_egress_utilization > 85% for 3m AND telemetry_headroom < 15% for 3m
- Alert: kernel_trace_drop_rate > 1% for 5m (indicates fidelity loss)

Immediate actions (on-call):
1) Acknowledge alert and notify SRE rotation and runtime oncall via #runtime-operator.
2) Run: redwoodctl telemetry status --cluster <cluster> and capture output to incident channel.
3) If B_total < expected for current traffic, check recent config changes: redwoodctl audit tail --service telemetry --since 1h
4) If consumption spikes are attributable to a named customer/route, apply temporary route-level sampling cap:
   redwoodctl telemetry route-update --route-id route-42 --sampling 0.05 --until 2026-11-02T18:00:00Z --reason protect headroom
5) If the spike is system-wide and urgent, increase B_total conservatively (to avoid cost shock): redwoodctl telemetry set-budget --mbps +50
6) If increasing B_total is not permitted, enact degradation order: reduce low->medium->high sampling and drop kernel payloads per the adaptive policy.

Benchmark validation procedures (to run in staging before rolling to prod):
- Goal: Verify adaptive allocation does not starve critical traces and maintains headroom tolerance under mixed workloads.
- Steps:
  1) Deploy telemetry agent with configured B_total and backpressure_window.
  2) Generate synthetic mixed workload: 60% medium traffic, 30% high (inject 5xx at 0.5%) and 10% low bursts. Use workload-driver: ./perf-driver --profile mixed-telemetry --rps 10k
