source.title: async-kernel-deadlock-patterns-and-mitigation-2026
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body:
Summary:

This document catalogs recurring async-kernel deadlock patterns observed in the runtime between 2024–2026, diagnostic signatures, short-term mitigations, and durable remediation paths. It is intended for oncall engineers, runtime kernel authors, scheduler owners, and SREs.

Scope and impact:
- Affects the serving runtime's asynchronous kernel handoff and cooperative preemption paths.
- Symptoms range from elevated tail latency (p99.99 spikes) to full request stalls on specific model variants, sometimes causing partial loss of capacity on a GPU node for minutes.
- Customers: observed primarily under mixed workloads (long-context chat + short embeddings) and during kernel rollouts or topology changes.
- Business impact: can exceed alerting SLOs (p95 latency > 2x target) and trigger failover cascades in the orchestrator.

High-level patterns (observed signatures):
1) Handoff-starvation deadlock
   - Trigger: a kernel A holding a per-shard mutex waits for a KV cache warmup callback that is scheduled on the same worker thread which is blocked on A’s completion.
   - Telemetry: kernel_handoff_wait_ms for shard X increases; kernel_active_threads==0 while gpu_dispatch_queue_depth>0; repeated trace annotations: "handoff:await-kv-warmup".

2) Interleaved-batch lock inversion
   - Trigger: two concurrent request cohorts (cohort-long and cohort-short) acquire locks in opposite orders during batch assembly and commit; when a preemptible kernel tries to preempt a non-cooperative kernel, both wait for slot credits.
   - Telemetry: increasing lock_contention_count, per-kernel credit_backoff_ms, and a correlated rise in per-request queue_time.

3) Async-callback-livelock from external IO
   - Trigger: a plugin or sidecar performing model-variant lookup issues synchronous filesystem or network calls within an async callback that is expected to be non-blocking; the callback holds scheduler-internal state and prevents kernel completion.
   - Telemetry: long-running callback traces labeled "sidecar-sync-io"; blocked thread stack traces show user call into sidecar library.

Detection rules and useful queries:
- Metrics to monitor (recommended):
  - kernel_handoff_wait_ms (histogram)
  - kernel_dispatch_backpressure_ratio (gauge)
  - lock_contention_count (counter) broken down by lock name
  - gpu_dispatch_queue_depth (gauge)
  - kernel_active_threads (gauge)
  - kernel_stall_retries (counter)

- Example alert condition (immediate page):
  - WHEN: 5m avg(kernel_handoff_wait_ms{p99} > 500ms) AND gpu_dispatch_queue_depth > 4
  - WHY: indicates persistent handoff slippage with backpressure building on dispatch queue
  - ACTION: page runtime-oncall, include node id and recent trace ids

- Useful log search patterns:
  - "handoff:await-kv-warmup" OR "sidecar-sync-io" OR "lock_acquire_timeout"
  - Example logQL: {app="runtime"} |= "handoff:await-kv-warmup" | json | line_format "{{.shard}} {{.kernel_id}} {{.duration_ms}}"

Repro steps (minimal, non-customer data sandbox):
1) On the dev cluster, deploy the synthetic-deadlock-replayer service (see repo infra/synthetic-deadlock).
2) Configuration snippet (YAML) for reproducer:
   - concurrent_long_cohorts: 6
   - short_cohort_rate: 200/s
   - enable_sidecar_sync_io: true
   - kv_warmup_latency_ms: 200

3) Run: `./replayer --target-node=node-7 --duration=600s --profile=handoff-starvation`
4) Observe metrics: p99 kernel_handoff_wait_ms > 500ms and kernel_active_threads drop to near 0 while gpu_dispatch_queue_depth rises.

Live investigation playbook (oncall):
1) Triage and scope
   - Identify affected nodes (metric: gpu_dispatch_queue_depth > 0 for > 60s).
   - Gather recent traces (trace IDs indicated by trace_sampler for the node).
2) Quick mitigations (fast, reversible)
   - If pattern is handoff-starvation: disable KV-prefetch warmup (runtime flag: --kv-warmup=false) for the node or toggle to low-concurrency mode.
   - If pattern is plugin IO: flip plugin sandboxing mode to async (flag: --plugin-sync-to-async=true) or unload the plugin (if safe).
   - If lock inversion suspected: enable priority-boost (flag: --sched-priority-boost=on) to break inversion while preserving safety.
3) Evacuate traffic (if mitigations insufficient)
   - Evacuate the node from the pool using orchestrator: drain node with graceful timeout 120s, failing fast if operator needs to reclaim capacity.
4) Capture artifacts
   - Collect full core dumps (gcore), runtime thread stacks, and kernel dispatcher traces and attach to incident. Use `collect-runtime-artifacts --node node-7 --since 2026-10-03T10:00:00Z`.

Long-term remediations and design recommendations:
- Contract-level changes
  - Enforce async-safety contracts: any callback executed in the runtime must be annotated and rejected at load-time if it performs sync IO or acquires runtime locks. Introduce linker-time or load-time checks for common blocking syscalls.
  - Introduce cooperative timeout windows for long kernels: kernels must periodically yield within a configured window (default 50ms) or be preempted at a safe checkpoint.

- Scheduler improvements
  - Deterministic lock ordering: define and enforce a global lock ordering table for the most frequently contended locks (kv-warmup-lock, batch-assemble-lock, checkpoint-lock). See proposed table below:

  +----------------------+----------------------------+
  | Lock name            | Ordering priority (lower=first) |
  +----------------------+----------------------------+
  | scheduler-global     | 1                          |
  | kv-warmup-lock       | 2                          |
  | batch-assemble-lock  | 3                          |
  | kernel-state-lock    | 4                          |

  - Kernel handoff grace protocol: when a kernel attempts handoff, a small rendezvous token is acquired; if rendezvous cannot be satisfied inside 100ms, the originator will release non-critical locks and retry to avoid circular waits.

- Observability and testing
  - Add a dedicated deadlock detector stage in the nightly microbenchmark suite that runs the synthetic-deadlock-replayer across combinatorial cohort ratios and kernel variants.
  - Add deterministic unit tests for priority-boost and lock ordering with a simulated scheduler.

Safe rollout guidance for fixes:
- Canary algorithm: start with 1% canary traffic on patched scheduler + kernel (min 3 nodes). Validate metrics for 6 hours. Gate criteria: no p99.9 kernel_handoff_wait_ms regression, lock_contention_count reduced by >= 50% on canary.
- Fallback: automatic rollback if canary sees kernel_active_threads drop by > 30% or if dispatcher error rate increases > 0.5% above baseline.

SOP snippets (commands):
- Disable kv warmup on a single node (useful temporary mitigant):
  kubectl -n infra exec node-agent--node-7 -- sh -c 'svcctl set-flag runtime --kv-warmup=false && svcctl restart runtime'

- Collect kernel dispatcher traces:
  curl -s "http://node-7.internal:9090/debug/dispatcher/traces?since=10m" > dispatcher-traces-node-7.json

- Minimal gdb backtrace capture (if the node is unresponsive):
  sudo gcore $(pgrep runtime) && sudo gdb --batch --pid $(pgrep runtime) -ex 'thread apply all bt' -ex 'quit' > bt-node-7.txt

Owner and escalation:
- Primary owner: runtime-kernel-team (oncall alias: runtime-kernel-oncall)
- Secondary: eng-sre (alias: eng-sre-oncall)
- For customer-impacting incidents, page customer-success-sre and the PM for the affected model tier.

Related pages and artifacts:
- See synthetic-deadlock test harness: git://repo/infra/synthetic-deadlock (internal)
- Trace examples attached to this page: trace-2026-06-11-node-7 (internal)

Appendix: example stack snapshot (sanitized) showing common deadlock layout:
- Thread 1: runtime::kernel::kv_warmup_wait() -> runtime::kv::lock() -> waiting for checkpoint-lock
- Thread 2: runtime::scheduler::batch_commit() -> runtime::kernel::lock() -> waiting for kv_warmup-lock
- Thread 3: plugin::variant_lookup() -> sync DNS resolve -> holds scheduler-global context

Notes and caveats:
- Many deadlock signatures are transient and only appear with a precise mix of cohort shapes; synthetic replayer helps reproduce but may not capture third-party plugin-induced deadlocks.
- Changes that add preemption points must be verified against model fidelity experiments to ensure they do not materially regress latency stability for long-context workloads.

Change history:
- 2026-05-12: Document created (Maya Chen)
- 2026-07-01: Added quick mitigation flags and example commands (Ethan Park)
- 2026-10-03: Expanded detection queries and added canary gating thresholds (Rina Patel)

End of document.

title: async-kernel-deadlock-patterns-and-mitigation-2026
preview:
Summary:

This document catalogs recurring async-kernel deadlock patterns observed in the runtime between 2024–2026, diagnostic signatures, short-term mitigations, and durable remediation paths. It is intended for oncall engineers, runtime kernel authors, scheduler owners, and SREs.

Scope and impact:
- Affects the serving runtime's asynchronous kernel handoff and cooperative preemption paths.
- Symptoms range from elevated tail latency (p99.99 spikes) to full request stalls on specific model variants, sometimes causing partial loss of capacity on a GPU node for minutes.
- Customers: observed primarily under mixed workloads (long-context chat + short embeddings) and during kernel rollouts or topology changes.
- Business impact: can exceed alerting SLOs (p95 latency > 2x target) and trigger failover cascades in the orchestrator.

High-level patterns (observed signatures):
1) Handoff-starvation deadlock
   - Trigger: a kernel A holding a per-shard mutex waits for a KV cache warmup callback that is scheduled on the same worker thread which is blocked on A’s completion.
   - Telemetry: kernel_handoff_wait_ms for shard X increases; kernel_active_threads==0 while gpu_dispatch_queue_depth>0; repeated trace annotations: "handoff:await-kv-warmup".

2) Interleaved-batch lock inversion
   - Trigger: two concurrent request cohorts (cohort-long and cohort-short) acquire locks in opposite orders during batch assembly and commit; when a preemptible kernel tries to preempt a non-cooperative kernel, both wait for slot credits.
   - Telemetry: increasing lock_contention_count, per-kernel credit_backoff_ms, and a correlated rise in per-request queue_time.

3) Async-callback-livelock from external IO
   - Trigger: a plugin or sidecar performing model-variant lookup issues synchronous filesystem or network calls within an async callback that is expected to be non-blocking; the callback holds scheduler-internal state and prevents kernel completion.
   - Telemetry: long-running callback traces labeled "sidecar-sync-io"; blocked thread stack traces show user call into sidecar library.

Detection rules and useful queries:
- Metrics to monitor (recommended):
  - kernel_handoff_wait_ms (histogram)
  - kernel_dispatch_backpressure_ratio (gauge)
  - lock_contention_count (counter) broken down by lock name
  - gpu_dispatch_queue_depth (gauge)
  - kernel_active_threads (gauge)
  - kernel_stall_retries (counter)

- Example alert condition (immediate page):
  - WHEN: 5m avg(kernel_handoff_wait_ms{p99} > 500ms) AND gpu_dispatch_queue_depth > 4
  - WHY: indicates persistent handoff slippage with backpressure building on dispatch queue
  - ACTION: page runtime-oncall, include node id and recent trace ids

- Useful log search patterns:
  - "handoff:await-kv-warmup" OR "sidecar-sync-io" OR "lock_acquire_timeout"
  - Example logQL: {app="runtime"} |= "handoff:await-kv-warmup" | json | line_format "{{.shard}} {{.kernel_id}} {{.duration_ms}}"

Repro steps (minimal, non-customer data sandbox):
1) On the dev cluster, deploy the synthetic-deadlock-replayer service (see repo infra/synthetic-deadlock).
2) Configuration snippet (YAML) for reproducer:
   - concurrent_long_cohorts: 6
   - short_cohort_rate: 200/s
   - enable_sidecar_sync_io: true
   - kv_warmup_latency_ms: 200

3) Run: `./replayer --target-node=node-7 --duration=600s --profile=handoff-starvation`
4) Observe metrics: p99 kernel_handoff_wait_ms > 500ms and kernel_active_threads drop to near 0 while gpu_dispatch_queue_depth rises.

Live investigation playbook (oncall):
1) Triage and scope
   - Identify affected nodes (metric: gpu_dispatch_queue_depth > 0 for > 60s).
   - Gather recent traces (trace IDs indicated by trace_sampler for the node).
2) Quick mitigations (fast, reversible)
   - If pattern is handoff-starvation: disable KV-prefetch warmup (runtime flag: --kv-warmup=false) for the node or toggle to low-concurrency mode.
   - If pattern is plugin IO: flip plugin sandboxing mode to async (flag: --plugin-sync-to-async=true) or unload the plugin (if safe).
   - If lock inversion suspected: enable priority-boost (flag: --sched-priority-boost=on) to break inversion while preserving safety.
3) Evacuate traffic (if mitigations insufficient)
   - Evacuate the node from the pool using orchestrator: drain node with graceful timeout 120s, failing fast if operator needs to reclaim capacity.
4) Capture artifacts
   - Collect full core dumps (gcore), runtime thread stacks, and kernel dispatcher traces and attach to incident. Use `collect-runtime-artifacts --node node-7 --since 2026-10-03T10:00:00Z`.

Long-term remediations and design recommendations:
- Contract-level changes
  - Enforce async-safety contracts: any callback executed in the runtime must be annotated and rejected at load-time if it performs sync IO or acquires runtime locks. Introduce linker-time or load-time checks for common blocking syscalls.
  - Introduce cooperative timeout windows for long kernels: kernels must periodically yield within a configured window (default 50ms) or be preempted at a safe checkpoint.

- Scheduler improvements
  - Deterministic lock ordering: define and enforce a global lock ordering table for the most frequently contended locks (kv-warmup-lock, batch-assemble-lock, checkpoint-lock). See proposed table below:

  +----------------------+----------------------------+
  | Lock name            | Ordering priority (lower=first) |
  +----------------------+----------------------------+
  | scheduler-global     | 1                          |
  | kv-warmup-lock       | 2                          |
  | batch-assemble-lock  | 3                          |
  | kernel-state-lock    | 4                          |

  - Kernel handoff grace protocol: when a kernel attempts handoff, a small rendezvous token is acquired; if rendezvous cannot be satisfied inside 100ms, the originator will release non-critical locks and retry to avoid circular waits.

- Observability and testing
  - Add a dedicated deadlock detector stage in the nightly microbenchmark suite that runs the synthetic-deadlock-replayer across combinatorial cohort ratios and kernel variants.
  - Add deterministic unit tests for priority-boost and lock ordering with a simulated scheduler.

Safe rollout guidance for fixes:
- Canary algorithm: start with 1% canary traffic on patched scheduler + kernel (min 3 nodes). Validate metrics for 6 hours. Gate criteria: no p99.9 kernel_handoff_wait_ms regression, lock_contention_count reduced by >= 50% on canary.
- Fallback: automatic rollback if canary sees kernel_active_threads drop by > 30% or if dispatcher error rate increases > 0.5% above baseline.

SOP snippets (commands):
- Disable kv warmup on a single node (useful temporary mitigant):
  kubectl -n infra exec node-agent--node-7 -- sh -c 'svcctl set-flag runtime --kv-warmup=false && svcctl restart runtime'

- Collect kernel dispatcher traces:
  curl -s "http://node-7.internal:9090/debug/dispatcher/traces?since=10m" > dispatcher-traces-node-7.json

- Minimal gdb backtrace capture (if the node is unresponsive):
  sudo gcore $(pgrep runtime) && sudo gdb --batch --pid $(pgrep runtime) -ex 'thread apply all bt' -ex 'quit' > bt-node-7.txt

Owner and escalation:
- Primary owner: runtime-kernel-team (oncall alias: runtime-kernel-oncall)
- Secondary: eng-sre (alias: eng-sre-oncall)
- For customer-impacting incidents, page customer-success-sre and the PM for the affected model tier.

Related pages and artifacts:
- See synthetic-deadlock test harness: git://repo/infra/synthetic-deadlock (internal)
- Trace examples attached to this page: trace-2026-06-11-node-7 (internal)

Appendix: example stack snapshot (sanitized) showing common deadlock layout:
- Thread 1: runtime::kernel::kv_warmup_wait() -> runtime::kv::lock() -> waiting for checkpoint-lock
- Thread 2: runtime::scheduler::batch_commit() -> runtime::kernel::lock() -> waiting for kv_warmup-lock
- Thread 3: plugin::variant_lookup() -> sync DNS resolve -> holds scheduler-global context

Notes and caveats:
- Many deadlock signatures are transient and only appear with a precise mix of cohort shapes; synthetic replayer helps reproduce but may not capture third-party plugin-induced deadlocks.
- Changes that add preemption points must be verified against model fidelity experiments to ensure they do not materially regress latency stability for long-context workloads.

Change history:
- 2026-05-12: Document created (Maya Chen)
- 2026-07-01: Added quick mitigation flags and example commands (Ethan Park)
- 2026-10-03: Expanded detection queries and added canary gating thresholds (Rina Patel)

End of document.

source.title: Degradation Probing and Load‑Shed Orchestration Manual
source.author: Priya Nandakumar
source.content:
Overview
========
This document describes the design, operational contracts, and runbook for Redwood's runtime-level degradation probing and load‑shed orchestration subsystem (the Shedder + Probe Controller). It explains how the system detects overload or fidelity regressions, safely degrades throughput/quality, probes for capacity recovery, and executes fallbacks to preserve customer‑facing SLOs. Intended audience: SREs, runtime engineers, oncall responders, and anyone running canary/rollout experiments against the serving runtime.

Scope and goals
----------------
- Scope: host runtime components that make admission and fidelity decisions (shedder, probe controller, router policies, kernel scheduler hooks). Includes metrics, alerting, config knobs, and incident procedures. Does NOT cover dedicated customer‑side capacity contracts or API client behavior.
- Goals:
  - Keep p99 tail latency below configured SLOs during partial failure modes.
  - Minimize error amplification from naive hard‑shutdowns.
  - Provide deterministic probing detection windows (so automated rollbacks/canaries behave reliably).
  - Offer clear observability signals for triage and postmortem.

Ownership
---------
- Primary owner: eng-serving-runtime (runtime-sre squad)
- Secondary owners: eng-platform (routing), eng-infra (autoscaler)
- Pager: runtime-sre oncall

Key terminology
---------------
- Shedder: admission component that rejects or defers incoming requests based on runtime signals and configured policies.
- Probe: a controlled low‑volume request used to test component health/fidelity during or after a degraded state.
- Degradation window: period during which the runtime accepts reduced throughput or fidelity (e.g., lower token budget, quantized model variant) to remain within resource bounds.
- Fidelity fallback: deliberate swap to a cheaper/compact model variant to preserve latency at the expense of some output quality.

High‑level architecture (text diagram)
-------------------------------------
Ingress -> Per‑route Router -> Admission Layer (Shedder) -> Scheduler/Kernels -> Execution
                              ^                                      |
                              |                                      v
                        Probe Controller <--- Health Aggregator <-- Kernel Telemetry

Design principles
-----------------
1. Graceful and measurable degradation: prefer smooth fidelity reductions (batch size limits, prefix caching, model variant swap) over abrupt connection resets.
2. Explorable recovery: when shedding, emit probes at controlled cadence to test recovery; probes must be tagged and isolated for observability.
3. Multi‑signal decisions: admission uses a weighted decision of kernel latency, GPU queue length, KV cache miss ratio, and model‑swap health. No single metric triggers an irreversible fallback.
4. Safety first: all automated fallback actions are rate‑limited and subject to automatic rollback if probe failure rate exceeds threshold.

Runtime components and contracts
--------------------------------
- Shedder (runtime component):
  - Inputs: per‑GPU queue depth, p99 kernel latency (1s window), slot exhaustion events, memory pressure flag.
  - Outputs: admit / queue / reject decisions + shed_reason tag.
  - Contract: decision latency <= 2ms; decisions annotated with trace_id and shed_reason.

- Probe Controller:
  - Inputs: health signals from telemetry, manual override from console, experiment flags.
  - Behavior: when degradation begins, schedule periodic probes (default cadence 1 probe/10s per cohort) that exercise the full model path including KV cache warmups.
  - Contract: probes must not exceed 0.5% of normal traffic and must be isolated from user billing metrics.

- Fidelity Orchestrator (model variant switcher):
  - Inputs: probe success ratio, runtime pressure score.
  - Outputs: model variant assignment per route/session.
  - Contract: variant switch must be atomic at the session token boundary and revertible within 30s when probes pass recovery thresholds.

Observability contract (metrics and logs)
-----------------------------------------
The following metrics must be emitted for every runtime shard and aggregated per cluster. Use these exact metric names for alerts and dashboards.

Metrics table (CSV style)
metric_name,description,unit,cardinality
runtime.shedder.admit_rate,Requests admitted after admission logic,req/s,route+shard
runtime.shedder.reject_rate,Requests rejected due to shedding,req/s,route+shard
runtime.shedder.queue_depth,Admission queue length,requests,shard
runtime.kernel.p99_latency,Kernel p99 latency over 1s window,ms,shard
runtime.kernel.queue_wait,Time spent waiting for kernel slot,ms,shard
runtime.kvcache.miss_ratio,KV cache miss ratio,percent,route
runtime.probe.success_ratio,Fraction of probes that returned expected status,percent,cohort
runtime.fidelity.variant,Active model variant id,string,route+session

Alerting thresholds (recommended)
- Sev 1: runtime.kernel.p99_latency > 1200ms for > 2m AND runtime.shedder.reject_rate > 0.1req/s aggregated across cluster.
- Sev 2: runtime.probe.success_ratio < 80% over a 5m window.
- Sev 3: runtime.kvcache.miss_ratio > 30% for a route over 10m.

Runbook: detection -> containment -> probe -> recovery (step‑by‑step)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Detection:
   - Pager triggers on Sev 1 alert. Open incident channel and claim.
   - Confirm p99 latency, drop rate, and any kernel OOM/slot errors in the trace stream.
2) Containment:
   - If admit decisions are saturating kernels, temporarily raise shedder aggressiveness to target 20% reject until further analysis. Command:
     - rctl shedder set --route <route> --aggressiveness 0.2 --reason "temporary‑containment"
   - Document the change in the incident timeline and tag affected customers in the console.
3) Probe:
   - Initiate manual probe run to validate execution path:
     - curl -H "X-Redwood-Probe: true" -X POST https://runtime-probe.local/healthcheck --data '{"route":"<route>","model":"<variant>"}'
   - Inspect runtime.probe.success_ratio and kernel traces for failed probes.
4) Recovery:
   - If probe success_ratio >= 95% for 2 consecutive 1m windows: gradually rollback shedder aggressiveness by 10% every 30s.
   - If probe failure persists, trigger model variant swap to a lower‑cost variant (see fidelity table below) with a canary cohort (1% traffic) for 5m before wide rollout.

CLI and diagnostic commands
---------------------------
- List shedding state per route: rctl shedder list --output table\- Set aggressiveness (0.0–1.0): rctl shedder set --route <route> --aggressiveness 0.3\- Trigger a manual probe: rctl probe run --route <route> --variant <variant> --count 10\- Dump last 5 kernel traces for a shard: rctl trace tail --shard <id> --limit 5\- Temporarily pin model variant: rctl model pin --route <route> --variant <id> --ttl 10m

Sample PromQL alerts
--------------------
- High p99 latency with shedding:
  sum by(cluster) (rate(runtime.kernel.p99_latency_sum[1m])) / sum by(cluster) (rate(runtime.kernel.p99_latency_count[1m])) > 1200
- Probe failure rate:
  1 - (sum(runtime.probe.success_ratio) by(cohort) / count(runtime.probe.success_ratio) by(cohort)) > 0.2

Configuration knobs and recommended defaults (table)
---------------------------------------------------
knob,default,description
shedder.window_size,1000ms,Time window for smoothing decision signals
shedder.min_admit_rate,10req/s,Minimum guaranteed admission per route (soft)
shedder.max_probe_fraction,0.005,Max fraction of traffic that can be probes
probe.cadence,10s,Minimum interval between probes for same cohort
variant.canary_fraction,0.01,Fraction of traffic used to canary a variant swap
fallback.max_duration,15m,Maximum continuous duration for an automatic fallback before human review

Safety & rollout constraints
---------------------------
- Any automated model variant swap must pass the probe gate for the canary cohort for at least three consecutive probe windows.
- Fallbacks that persist longer than fallback.max_duration must open a ticket in the runtime backlog and notify the release owner.
- All shedder aggressiveness changes performed by automation are rate limited to 10% change per minute to avoid oscillation.

Testing and validation matrix
----------------------------
- Unit tests: shedder decision logic and probe tagging (CI enforced).
- Integration tests: synthetic load profiles that simulate cache coldstart, kernel saturation, and degraded interconnect (run by perf CI nightly).
- Chaos tests: injected kernel hang and NVLink degradation scenarios with controlled shedding to validate graceful degradation behavior (game day quarterly).

Known limitations and tradeoffs
------------------------------
- Probes are small by design and can pass even when larger normal traffic fails due to queue ordering differences; use probe traces as one signal among many.
- Model variant swaps reduce latency but may cause downstream semantic regressions; every variant includes a quality delta estimate that must be considered for enterprise customers.
- The shedder cannot (currently) account for cross‑shard KV cache warming heuristics perfectly; this can lead to transient higher miss_ratio after recovery.

Post‑incident checklist
----------------------
- Export probe traces and kernel traces for the incident window.
- Compute probe success_ratio and compare to probe baseline.
- If fallback triggered, run fidelity regression suite for affected prompt sets.
- File a follow‑up action in the runtime backlog with owner and target date.

Appendix A: Example YAML policy fragment
---------------------------------------
# admission policy for high throughput text-generation route
admission_policy:
  route: "/v1/generate"
  shedder:
    aggressiveness: 0.15
    window_size_ms: 1000
    min_admit_rate: 10
  probe:
    cadence_s: 10
    max_fraction: 0.005
  fidelity_fallback:
    canary_fraction: 0.01
    variants:["gptx-8b-q4","gptx-8b-q2a"]

Appendix B: Quick reference for oncall responders
--------------------------------------------------
- First 5 minutes: confirm alerts, set temporary containment to 20% reject if kernels saturated.
- 5–20 minutes: run manual probes and escalate to runtime-sre if probe fail rate > 20%.
- >20 minutes: if automated rollback does not stabilize, trigger manual canary variant swap and route pin.

Document history and changes
--------------------------
- 2026-02-10: created (Priya Nandakumar)
- 2026-04-18: tightened probe cadence and added canary constraints (reviewers: Ethan Park)
- 2026-09-15: updated alert thresholds and added PromQL examples (reviewers: Marcus Li, Sofia Alvarez)

Related docs and links
----------------------
- Runtime decision graph: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Backpressure playbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/sla-aware-admission-controller-and-backpressure-playbook-2027
- Kernel telemetry contract: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/team-wiki/kernel-telemetry-annotation-guidelines-2025

title: Degradation Probing and Load‑Shed Orchestration Manual
preview:
Overview
========
This document describes the design, operational contracts, and runbook for Redwood's runtime-level degradation probing and load‑shed orchestration subsystem (the Shedder + Probe Controller). It explains how the system detects overload or fidelity regressions, safely degrades throughput/quality, probes for capacity recovery, and executes fallbacks to preserve customer‑facing SLOs. Intended audience: SREs, runtime engineers, oncall responders, and anyone running canary/rollout experiments against the serving runtime.

Scope and goals
----------------
- Scope: host runtime components that make admission and fidelity decisions (shedder, probe controller, router policies, kernel scheduler hooks). Includes metrics, alerting, config knobs, and incident procedures. Does NOT cover dedicated customer‑side capacity contracts or API client behavior.
- Goals:
  - Keep p99 tail latency below configured SLOs during partial failure modes.
  - Minimize error amplification from naive hard‑shutdowns.
  - Provide deterministic probing detection windows (so automated rollbacks/canaries behave reliably).
  - Offer clear observability signals for triage and postmortem.

Ownership
---------
- Primary owner: eng-serving-runtime (runtime-sre squad)
- Secondary owners: eng-platform (routing), eng-infra (autoscaler)
- Pager: runtime-sre oncall

Key terminology
---------------
- Shedder: admission component that rejects or defers incoming requests based on runtime signals and configured policies.
- Probe: a controlled low‑volume request used to test component health/fidelity during or after a degraded state.
- Degradation window: period during which the runtime accepts reduced throughput or fidelity (e.g., lower token budget, quantized model variant) to remain within resource bounds.
- Fidelity fallback: deliberate swap to a cheaper/compact model variant to preserve latency at the expense of some output quality.

High‑level architecture (text diagram)
-------------------------------------
Ingress -> Per‑route Router -> Admission Layer (Shedder) -> Scheduler/Kernels -> Execution
                              ^                                      |
                              |                                      v
                        Probe Controller <--- Health Aggregator <-- Kernel Telemetry

Design principles
-----------------
1. Graceful and measurable degradation: prefer smooth fidelity reductions (batch size limits, prefix caching, model variant swap) over abrupt connection resets.
2. Explorable recovery: when shedding, emit probes at controlled cadence to test recovery; probes must be tagged and isolated for observability.
3. Multi‑signal decisions: admission uses a weighted decision of kernel latency, GPU queue length, KV cache miss ratio, and model‑swap health. No single metric triggers an irreversible fallback.
4. Safety first: all automated fallback actions are rate‑limited and subject to automatic rollback if probe failure rate exceeds threshold.

Runtime components and contracts
--------------------------------
- Shedder (runtime component):
  - Inputs: per‑GPU queue depth, p99 kernel latency (1s window), slot exhaustion events, memory pressure flag.
  - Outputs: admit / queue / reject decisions + shed_reason tag.
  - Contract: decision latency <= 2ms; decisions annotated with trace_id and shed_reason.

- Probe Controller:
  - Inputs: health signals from telemetry, manual override from console, experiment flags.
  - Behavior: when degradation begins, schedule periodic probes (default cadence 1 probe/10s per cohort) that exercise the full model path including KV cache warmups.
  - Contract: probes must not exceed 0.5% of normal traffic and must be isolated from user billing metrics.

- Fidelity Orchestrator (model variant switcher):
  - Inputs: probe success ratio, runtime pressure score.
  - Outputs: model variant assignment per route/session.
  - Contract: variant switch must be atomic at the session token boundary and revertible within 30s when probes pass recovery thresholds.

Observability contract (metrics and logs)
-----------------------------------------
The following metrics must be emitted for every runtime shard and aggregated per cluster. Use these exact metric names for alerts and dashboards.

Metrics table (CSV style)
metric_name,description,unit,cardinality
runtime.shedder.admit_rate,Requests admitted after admission logic,req/s,route+shard
runtime.shedder.reject_rate,Requests rejected due to shedding,req/s,route+shard
runtime.shedder.queue_depth,Admission queue length,requests,shard
runtime.kernel.p99_latency,Kernel p99 latency over 1s window,ms,shard
runtime.kernel.queue_wait,Time spent waiting for kernel slot,ms,shard
runtime.kvcache.miss_ratio,KV cache miss ratio,percent,route
runtime.probe.success_ratio,Fraction of probes that returned expected status,percent,cohort
runtime.fidelity.variant,Active model variant id,string,route+session

Alerting thresholds (recommended)
- Sev 1: runtime.kernel.p99_latency > 1200ms for > 2m AND runtime.shedder.reject_rate > 0.1req/s aggregated across cluster.
- Sev 2: runtime.probe.success_ratio < 80% over a 5m window.
- Sev 3: runtime.kvcache.miss_ratio > 30% for a route over 10m.

Runbook: detection -> containment -> probe -> recovery (step‑by‑step)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
1) Detection:
   - Pager triggers on Sev 1 alert. Open incident channel and claim.
   - Confirm p99 latency, drop rate, and any kernel OOM/slot errors in the trace stream.
2) Containment:
   - If admit decisions are saturating kernels, temporarily raise shedder aggressiveness to target 20% reject until further analysis. Command:
     - rctl shedder set --route <route> --aggressiveness 0.2 --reason "temporary‑containment"
   - Document the change in the incident timeline and tag affected customers in the console.
3) Probe:
   - Initiate manual probe run to validate execution path:
     - curl -H "X-Redwood-Probe: true" -X POST https://runtime-probe.local/healthcheck --data '{"route":"<route>","model":"<variant>"}'
   - Inspect runtime.probe.success_ratio and kernel traces for failed probes.
4) Recovery:
   - If probe success_ratio >= 95% for 2 consecutive 1m windows: gradually rollback shedder aggressiveness by 10% every 30s.
   - If probe failure persists, trigger model variant swap to a lower‑cost variant (see fidelity table below) with a canary cohort (1% traffic) for 5m before wide rollout.

CLI and diagnostic commands
---------------------------
- List shedding state per route: rctl shedder list --output table\- Set aggressiveness (0.0–1.0): rctl shedder set --route <route> --aggressiveness 0.3\- Trigger a manual probe: rctl probe run --route <route> --variant <variant> --count 10\- Dump last 5 kernel traces for a shard: rctl trace tail --shard <id> --limit 5\- Temporarily pin model variant: rctl model pin --route <route> --variant <id> --ttl 10m

Sample PromQL alerts
--------------------
- High p99 latency with shedding:
  sum by(cluster) (rate(runtime.kernel.p99_latency_sum[1m])) / sum by(cluster) (rate(runtime.kernel.p99_latency_count[1m])) > 1200
- Probe failure rate:
  1 - (sum(runtime.probe.success_ratio) by(cohort) / count(runtime.probe.success_ratio) by(cohort)) > 0.2

Configuration knobs and recommended defaults (table)
---------------------------------------------------
knob,default,description
shedder.window_size,1000ms,Time window for smoothing decision signals
shedder.min_admit_rate,10req/s,Minimum guaranteed admission per route (soft)
shedder.max_probe_fraction,0.005,Max fraction of traffic that can be probes
probe.cadence,10s,Minimum interval between probes for same cohort
variant.canary_fraction,0.01,Fraction of traffic used to canary a variant swap
fallback.max_duration,15m,Maximum continuous duration for an automatic fallback before human review

Safety & rollout constraints
---------------------------
- Any automated model variant swap must pass the probe gate for the canary cohort for at least three consecutive probe windows.
- Fallbacks that persist longer than fallback.max_duration must open a ticket in the runtime backlog and notify the release owner.
- All shedder aggressiveness changes performed by automation are rate limited to 10% change per minute to avoid oscillation.

Testing and validation matrix
----------------------------
- Unit tests: shedder decision logic and probe tagging (CI enforced).
- Integration tests: synthetic load profiles that simulate cache coldstart, kernel saturation, and degraded interconnect (run by perf CI nightly).
- Chaos tests: injected kernel hang and NVLink degradation scenarios with controlled shedding to validate graceful degradation behavior (game day quarterly).

Known limitations and tradeoffs
------------------------------
- Probes are small by design and can pass even when larger normal traffic fails due to queue ordering differences; use probe traces as one signal among many.
- Model variant swaps reduce latency but may cause downstream semantic regressions; every variant includes a quality delta estimate that must be considered for enterprise customers.
- The shedder cannot (currently) account for cross‑shard KV cache warming heuristics perfectly; this can lead to transient higher miss_ratio after recovery.

Post‑incident checklist
----------------------
- Export probe traces and kernel traces for the incident window.
- Compute probe success_ratio and compare to probe baseline.
- If fallback triggered, run fidelity regression suite for affected prompt sets.
- File a follow‑up action in the runtime backlog with owner and target date.

Appendix A: Example YAML policy fragment
---------------------------------------
# admission policy for high throughput text-generation route
admission_policy:
  route: "/v1/generate"
  shedder:
    aggressiveness: 0.15
    window_size_ms: 1000
    min_admit_rate: 10
  probe:
    cadence_s: 10
    max_fraction: 0.005
  fidelity_fallback:
    canary_fraction: 0.01
    variants:["gptx-8b-q4","gptx-8b-q2a"]

Appendix B: Quick reference for oncall responders
--------------------------------------------------
- First 5 minutes: confirm alerts, set temporary containment to 20% reject if kernels saturated.
- 5–20 minutes: run manual probes and escalate to runtime-sre if probe fail rate > 20%.
- >20 minutes: if automated rollback does not stabilize, trigger manual canary variant swap and route pin.

Document history and changes
--------------------------
- 2026-02-10: created (Priya Nandakumar)
- 2026-04-18: tightened probe cadence and added canary constraints (reviewers: Ethan Park)
- 2026-09-15: updated alert thresholds and added PromQL examples (reviewers: Marcus Li, Sofia Alvarez)

Related docs and links
----------------------
- Runtime decision graph: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Backpressure playbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/sla-aware-admission-controller-and-backpressure-playbook-2027
- Kernel telemetry contract: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/team-wiki/kernel-telemetry-annotation-guidelines-2025

source.title: Kernel ABI & Quantization Kernel Compatibility Guidelines
source.author: Mateo Alvarez
source.content:
Summary

This document describes the compatibility matrix and operational guidelines for kernel ABIs and quantization-optimized kernels used by the Redwood serving runtime. It covers version mapping, supported GPU microarchitectures, recommended fallback strategies, preflight tests, safe rollout and rollback procedures, and required telemetry for forensic analysis. The goal is to make kernel selection predictable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments and to reduce upgrade risk for private (VPC / on-prem) customers.

Scope

- Kernel ABIs exposed by the runtime (cuda/cudnn/cuBLAS, ROCm, oneAPI-compatible kernels)
- Quantization-aware kernels (int8, int4, dynamic-float16 paths) and heuristics that select them
- Compatibility across accelerator generations (NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Ada; AMD CDNA2/CDNA3; Intel Gaudi/ARC where applicable)
- Runtime-level rollback and canary test procedures for private deployments

Audience

This is intended for: runtime engineers, kernel maintainers, private-deploy field operators, SREs, and solutions engineers who run customer rollouts.

1) High-level compatibility principles

- ABI-first: prefer stable runtime ABI targets (e.g., CUDA 11.x vs 12.x). Kernel optimizations should be feature-gated, not ABI-breaking.
- Architecture-aware selection: kernels must declare supported architectures (e.g., sm_80, sm_90, gfx110x) and a minimum driver/firmware requirement.
- Graceful fallback: for any quantized kernel that fails validation, the runtime must fall back to the next-safe kernel (e.g., int8 -> fp16 -> fp32) while preserving request semantics.
- Telemetry and evidence preservation: every kernel switch must emit structured telemetry (see "Telemetry requirements" section) to support post-incident forensics.

2) Kernel compatibility table (summary)

| Kernel type | Typical ABI / Driver req | Supported HW (examples) | Quant modes | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|-------------|-------|
| high-throughput-mha-optim (CUDA) | CUDA 12.1+, driver 535+ | NVIDIA Ada (RTX 40x0/ GH200 variants), Hopper (H100) | fp16, bf16 | Uses Tensor Cores; requires cuBLAS/cuDNN GPU kernels; prefers workspace memory >= 8 MB |
| int8-mha-fastpath | CUDA 12.1+, driver 540+ | NVIDIA Hopper (H100) with int8 tensor cores | int8 | Only for models validated to <1% quality delta; must be feature gated per-customer |
| rocm-mha-optimized | ROCm 5.6+ | AMD CDNA2 (Instinct MI100), CDNA3 | fp16, bf16 | Requires ROCm kernel packages; falls back to CPU-hosted fp32 path if missing |
| oneapi-bf16-path | oneAPI 2023.4+ | Intel Gaudi/ARC experimental | bf16 | API experimental; enabled only in internal QA and by explicit customer opt-in |

(Full machine-readable matrix is stored in the kernel registry: /internal/registries/kernel-compat-2026.csv)

3) Preflight checks (private deployments)

Before enabling a new kernel set on a private deployment, run the checklist below. These steps must be automated and recorded in the release ticket.

Preflight checklist (ordered):
1. Inventory: collect accelerator model, driver version, firmware revision and OS kernel (uname -a) from every node.
2. Sizing: ensure ephemeral workspace and pinned GPU memory meet kernel requirements (example: 8 MB workspace recommended for mha-optim).
3. Driver validation: verify drivers meet minimum versions listed in the compatibility table.
4. Artifact attestation: verify kernel artifacts signatures using the internal signing key (see CLI snippet).
5. Unit kernel smoke: run the kernel smoke harness (tools/kernel-smoke-runner) on a representative node.
6. Regression eval: run a small, deterministic prompt set against the canary cluster (N=1k tokens) and check quality regressions (absolute delta thresholds per model).
7. Telemetry gating: ensure telemetry hooks are deployed and forwarding to the evidence sink.

Example CLI snippets:

# retrieve GPU inventory (Linux)
$ sudo /opt/redwood/bin/gpu-inventory --output json > gpu-inventory.json

# verify kernel artifact signature
$ /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-verify --artifact /opt/kernels/mha-optim-v2.tar.gz --trusted-keys /etc/redwood/trusted-keys.pem

4) Canary rollout plan (safe pattern for private/VPC deployments)

Principles: small blast radius, automatic quality gates, rollback hooks, and customer notification where required.

Step-by-step canary plan:
1. Create a canary pool: reserve 2–4 nodes (must be isolated from production tenant traffic).
2. Apply kernel bundle to canary nodes and restart runtime components using the operator's safe-restart hook.
3. Run functional smoke and deterministic evals. Must pass model-level quality gates:
   - Top-level service SLOs (p95 latency, error rate) must remain within 10% of baseline.
   - Quality regression per-model must be <= configured delta (default 0.8% absolute token-level difference).
4. Run load-validation: replay recorded production traces at 1x and 2x QPS for 30 minutes. Monitor CPU/GPU utilization and latency tails.
5. If all gates pass, promote kernel bundle to a staged subset (10% of production nodes) and run the same checks for 2 hours.
6. Gradually increase to 50% and then 100% using automated health checks.

If any gate fails, follow the rollback procedure immediately. Notify the customer and create a postmortem if customer-affecting.

5) Rollback procedure (operator-safe)

Goals: restore previous stable kernel, preserve forensic evidence, avoid data loss.

Immediate rollback steps:
1. Abort ongoing promotions using the operator: /opt/redwood/bin/operator promote --abort <bundle-id>
2. Mark the canary nodes read-only for new model swaps: /opt/redwood/bin/node-ralm --set read-only
3. Revert kernel artifacts to the last-known-good bundle: /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-apply --bundle-id <last-good>
4. Restart runtime components with --preserve-kv to avoid KV cache loss when possible.
5. Run validation smoke and replay a 10-minute production trace.
6. Collect forensic evidence: kernel logs, telemetry spans, driver stack traces. Package them with the rollback ticket.

Operator CLI example for rollback:

# revert to last known good bundle
$ /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-apply --bundle-id rwbundle-2026-09-12 --force && sudo systemctl restart redwood-runtime --preserve-kv

6) Forensic telemetry and required traces

Minimum telemetry emitted on kernel switch events (every value must be present):
- event_type: KERNEL_SWITCH
- timestamp_utc
- node_id
- bundle_id
- previous_kernel_id
- new_kernel_id
- driver_version
- firmware_revision
- validation_signatures (artifact sig id)
- smoke_results: pass/fail and short hash of the test output
- quality_eval_summary: per-model numeric deltas

Retention: for private deployments, preserve full forensic packages for 90 days (customer contract permitting). Evidence bundles must be stored in the customer-specific evidence bucket with immutability flags when requested by compliance.

7) Quality gating configuration (suggested defaults)

- latency_p95_increase_threshold: 10%
- error_rate_absolute_threshold: 0.5%
- per_model_quality_delta: 0.8% absolute token-level difference
- smoke_harness_timeout: 300s

8) Known incompatibility patterns & mitigation recipes

Pattern: int8 kernel yields subtle hallucination drift on long prompts.
Mitigation: restrict int8 to short-sequence inference or enable per-model calibration. If a customer requires long-context fidelity, prefer bf16 or fp16 kernels.

Pattern: ROCm kernel fails on nodes with older firmware microcode.
Mitigation: require firmware >= vendor-specified minimum; if not possible, mark nodes as non-candidate for this bundle.

9) Customer-facing configuration knobs (private deploys)

Operators can expose the following flags to customers during onboarding (document in the customer-runbook template):
- enable_experimental_kernels: boolean (default false)
- per_model_quantization_profile: URL to customer-approved profile
- max_latency_slo: integer (ms) to allow tuning fallback aggressiveness

10) Post-incident checklist and postmortem template

1. Preserve evidence bundle and copy to secure forensic store.
2. Populate postmortem with timeline, root cause hypotheses, and customer impact.
3. Run replays using isolated sandbox and test candidate fixes.
4. Share corrective action plan with stakeholders and schedule follow-up.

Appendix A — Kernel artifact signing and verification

- All kernel bundles must be signed by the release pipeline using the Redwood release key. Artifacts without a valid signature are rejected by the operator.
- Signing command (CI):
$ gpg --batch --yes --armor --sign --detach-sign --local-user redwood-release-key artifacts/mha-optim-v2.tar.gz

- Verification (operator):
$ /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-verify --artifact artifacts/mha-optim-v2.tar.gz --trusted-keys /etc/redwood/trusted-keys.pem

Appendix B — Minimal test matrix (smoke harness samples)

| Test name | Purpose | Expected result | Run time |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|----------|
| kernel-basic-launch | Validate kernel loads and exports expected symbols | success, no segfault | 30s |
| inkernel-inference | Basic generate for short prompt (N=3 tokens) | exact output hash match | 45s |
| stress-multi-stream | 8 concurrent streams for 60s | p95 latency < configured threshold | 60s |

Appendix C — Ownership and escalation

- Primary owner: kernels team (eng-serving-runtime) — Mateo Alvarez
- Secondary: SRE on-call for serving runtime
- Escalation: if rollback required and customer impact > 10% of SLO, page Director of Platform (on-call list) and notify Customer Success lead for the affected account.

Related documents and artifacts

- Kernel compatibility registry: /internal/registries/kernel-compat-2026.csv
- Kernel smoke harness: git@internal:redwood/tools/kernel-smoke-runner.git
- Artifact signing key rotation policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/crypto-agile-key-rotation-2026

Change log

- 2026-02-15: Created (initial draft)
- 2026-06-01: Added canary rollout timings and telemetry fields (Priya Rangan)
- 2026-08-12: Clarified rollback commands and preserved-kv restart semantics (Janelle Park)

title: Kernel ABI & Quantization Kernel Compatibility Guidelines
preview:
Summary

This document describes the compatibility matrix and operational guidelines for kernel ABIs and quantization-optimized kernels used by the Redwood serving runtime. It covers version mapping, supported GPU microarchitectures, recommended fallback strategies, preflight tests, safe rollout and rollback procedures, and required telemetry for forensic analysis. The goal is to make kernel selection predictable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments and to reduce upgrade risk for private (VPC / on-prem) customers.

Scope

- Kernel ABIs exposed by the runtime (cuda/cudnn/cuBLAS, ROCm, oneAPI-compatible kernels)
- Quantization-aware kernels (int8, int4, dynamic-float16 paths) and heuristics that select them
- Compatibility across accelerator generations (NVIDIA Ampere, Hopper, Ada; AMD CDNA2/CDNA3; Intel Gaudi/ARC where applicable)
- Runtime-level rollback and canary test procedures for private deployments

Audience

This is intended for: runtime engineers, kernel maintainers, private-deploy field operators, SREs, and solutions engineers who run customer rollouts.

1) High-level compatibility principles

- ABI-first: prefer stable runtime ABI targets (e.g., CUDA 11.x vs 12.x). Kernel optimizations should be feature-gated, not ABI-breaking.
- Architecture-aware selection: kernels must declare supported architectures (e.g., sm_80, sm_90, gfx110x) and a minimum driver/firmware requirement.
- Graceful fallback: for any quantized kernel that fails validation, the runtime must fall back to the next-safe kernel (e.g., int8 -> fp16 -> fp32) while preserving request semantics.
- Telemetry and evidence preservation: every kernel switch must emit structured telemetry (see "Telemetry requirements" section) to support post-incident forensics.

2) Kernel compatibility table (summary)

| Kernel type | Typical ABI / Driver req | Supported HW (examples) | Quant modes | Notes |
|-------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|-------------|-------|
| high-throughput-mha-optim (CUDA) | CUDA 12.1+, driver 535+ | NVIDIA Ada (RTX 40x0/ GH200 variants), Hopper (H100) | fp16, bf16 | Uses Tensor Cores; requires cuBLAS/cuDNN GPU kernels; prefers workspace memory >= 8 MB |
| int8-mha-fastpath | CUDA 12.1+, driver 540+ | NVIDIA Hopper (H100) with int8 tensor cores | int8 | Only for models validated to <1% quality delta; must be feature gated per-customer |
| rocm-mha-optimized | ROCm 5.6+ | AMD CDNA2 (Instinct MI100), CDNA3 | fp16, bf16 | Requires ROCm kernel packages; falls back to CPU-hosted fp32 path if missing |
| oneapi-bf16-path | oneAPI 2023.4+ | Intel Gaudi/ARC experimental | bf16 | API experimental; enabled only in internal QA and by explicit customer opt-in |

(Full machine-readable matrix is stored in the kernel registry: /internal/registries/kernel-compat-2026.csv)

3) Preflight checks (private deployments)

Before enabling a new kernel set on a private deployment, run the checklist below. These steps must be automated and recorded in the release ticket.

Preflight checklist (ordered):
1. Inventory: collect accelerator model, driver version, firmware revision and OS kernel (uname -a) from every node.
2. Sizing: ensure ephemeral workspace and pinned GPU memory meet kernel requirements (example: 8 MB workspace recommended for mha-optim).
3. Driver validation: verify drivers meet minimum versions listed in the compatibility table.
4. Artifact attestation: verify kernel artifacts signatures using the internal signing key (see CLI snippet).
5. Unit kernel smoke: run the kernel smoke harness (tools/kernel-smoke-runner) on a representative node.
6. Regression eval: run a small, deterministic prompt set against the canary cluster (N=1k tokens) and check quality regressions (absolute delta thresholds per model).
7. Telemetry gating: ensure telemetry hooks are deployed and forwarding to the evidence sink.

Example CLI snippets:

# retrieve GPU inventory (Linux)
$ sudo /opt/redwood/bin/gpu-inventory --output json > gpu-inventory.json

# verify kernel artifact signature
$ /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-verify --artifact /opt/kernels/mha-optim-v2.tar.gz --trusted-keys /etc/redwood/trusted-keys.pem

4) Canary rollout plan (safe pattern for private/VPC deployments)

Principles: small blast radius, automatic quality gates, rollback hooks, and customer notification where required.

Step-by-step canary plan:
1. Create a canary pool: reserve 2–4 nodes (must be isolated from production tenant traffic).
2. Apply kernel bundle to canary nodes and restart runtime components using the operator's safe-restart hook.
3. Run functional smoke and deterministic evals. Must pass model-level quality gates:
   - Top-level service SLOs (p95 latency, error rate) must remain within 10% of baseline.
   - Quality regression per-model must be <= configured delta (default 0.8% absolute token-level difference).
4. Run load-validation: replay recorded production traces at 1x and 2x QPS for 30 minutes. Monitor CPU/GPU utilization and latency tails.
5. If all gates pass, promote kernel bundle to a staged subset (10% of production nodes) and run the same checks for 2 hours.
6. Gradually increase to 50% and then 100% using automated health checks.

If any gate fails, follow the rollback procedure immediately. Notify the customer and create a postmortem if customer-affecting.

5) Rollback procedure (operator-safe)

Goals: restore previous stable kernel, preserve forensic evidence, avoid data loss.

Immediate rollback steps:
1. Abort ongoing promotions using the operator: /opt/redwood/bin/operator promote --abort <bundle-id>
2. Mark the canary nodes read-only for new model swaps: /opt/redwood/bin/node-ralm --set read-only
3. Revert kernel artifacts to the last-known-good bundle: /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-apply --bundle-id <last-good>
4. Restart runtime components with --preserve-kv to avoid KV cache loss when possible.
5. Run validation smoke and replay a 10-minute production trace.
6. Collect forensic evidence: kernel logs, telemetry spans, driver stack traces. Package them with the rollback ticket.

Operator CLI example for rollback:

# revert to last known good bundle
$ /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-apply --bundle-id rwbundle-2026-09-12 --force && sudo systemctl restart redwood-runtime --preserve-kv

6) Forensic telemetry and required traces

Minimum telemetry emitted on kernel switch events (every value must be present):
- event_type: KERNEL_SWITCH
- timestamp_utc
- node_id
- bundle_id
- previous_kernel_id
- new_kernel_id
- driver_version
- firmware_revision
- validation_signatures (artifact sig id)
- smoke_results: pass/fail and short hash of the test output
- quality_eval_summary: per-model numeric deltas

Retention: for private deployments, preserve full forensic packages for 90 days (customer contract permitting). Evidence bundles must be stored in the customer-specific evidence bucket with immutability flags when requested by compliance.

7) Quality gating configuration (suggested defaults)

- latency_p95_increase_threshold: 10%
- error_rate_absolute_threshold: 0.5%
- per_model_quality_delta: 0.8% absolute token-level difference
- smoke_harness_timeout: 300s

8) Known incompatibility patterns & mitigation recipes

Pattern: int8 kernel yields subtle hallucination drift on long prompts.
Mitigation: restrict int8 to short-sequence inference or enable per-model calibration. If a customer requires long-context fidelity, prefer bf16 or fp16 kernels.

Pattern: ROCm kernel fails on nodes with older firmware microcode.
Mitigation: require firmware >= vendor-specified minimum; if not possible, mark nodes as non-candidate for this bundle.

9) Customer-facing configuration knobs (private deploys)

Operators can expose the following flags to customers during onboarding (document in the customer-runbook template):
- enable_experimental_kernels: boolean (default false)
- per_model_quantization_profile: URL to customer-approved profile
- max_latency_slo: integer (ms) to allow tuning fallback aggressiveness

10) Post-incident checklist and postmortem template

1. Preserve evidence bundle and copy to secure forensic store.
2. Populate postmortem with timeline, root cause hypotheses, and customer impact.
3. Run replays using isolated sandbox and test candidate fixes.
4. Share corrective action plan with stakeholders and schedule follow-up.

Appendix A — Kernel artifact signing and verification

- All kernel bundles must be signed by the release pipeline using the Redwood release key. Artifacts without a valid signature are rejected by the operator.
- Signing command (CI):
$ gpg --batch --yes --armor --sign --detach-sign --local-user redwood-release-key artifacts/mha-optim-v2.tar.gz

- Verification (operator):
$ /opt/redwood/bin/artifact-verify --artifact artifacts/mha-optim-v2.tar.gz --trusted-keys /etc/redwood/trusted-keys.pem

Appendix B — Minimal test matrix (smoke harness samples)

| Test name | Purpose | Expected result | Run time |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|----------|
| kernel-basic-launch | Validate kernel loads and exports expected symbols | success, no segfault | 30s |
| inkernel-inference | Basic generate for short prompt (N=3 tokens) | exact output hash match | 45s |
| stress-multi-stream | 8 concurrent streams for 60s | p95 latency < configured threshold | 60s |

Appendix C — Ownership and escalation

- Primary owner: kernels team (eng-serving-runtime) — Mateo Alvarez
- Secondary: SRE on-call for serving runtime
- Escalation: if rollback required and customer impact > 10% of SLO, page Director of Platform (on-call list) and notify Customer Success lead for the affected account.

Related documents and artifacts

- Kernel compatibility registry: /internal/registries/kernel-compat-2026.csv
- Kernel smoke harness: git@internal:redwood/tools/kernel-smoke-runner.git
- Artifact signing key rotation policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/crypto-agile-key-rotation-2026

Change log

- 2026-02-15: Created (initial draft)
- 2026-06-01: Added canary rollout timings and telemetry fields (Priya Rangan)
- 2026-08-12: Clarified rollback commands and preserved-kv restart semantics (Janelle Park)
