source.title: Eviction Rewind and State Reconstruction Playbook
source.author: Sophie Chen
source.body:
Overview
========
This playbook documents the procedure to recover from kvcache eviction events that cause request-level state divergence or incomplete KV reconstruction during model swaps, preemption, or kernel handoffs. It covers triage, automated rewind and state reconstruction action sequences, verification checks, rollback criteria, and safety tests. The intended audience is oncall SREs, serving-runtime engineers, and incident responders.

Why this matters
-----------------
- Eviction-induced state gaps can lead to incorrect completions, user-facing hallucinations, or repeated model fallbacks.
- Fast, deterministic reconstruction preserves SLOs and avoids large-scale reroutes to lower-fidelity fallback models.
- This playbook provides a safe, repeatable sequence to reconstruct per-session KV state and safely resume traffic.

When to run this playbook
-------------------------
Run this playbook when one or more of the following are observed:
- Sudden spike in kvcache_reconstruction_failures (> 10/min sustained for 5 minutes)
- Increase in tail latency correlated with eviction_rate or kernel_handoff_rate
- Model swap incidents where > 0.5% of sessions show token regression or repeated left-pad artifacts
- Automated canary signals mark warmup_failure=true during rollout

Impact and SLOs
---------------
- Target: recover affected sessions and restore p95 inference latency within 10 minutes of start of remediation.
- Acceptable interim error budget consumption: up to 2x normal error budget for the affected service for a single incident.
- Escalate to incident commander if reconstruction extends beyond 30 minutes or if reconstruction failures exceed 1% of total affected sessions.

Ownership and permissions
-------------------------
- Primary owner: Serving Runtime (runtime-sre)
- Secondary owner: Kernel Scheduling Team (kernel-scheduler)
- Required permissions: ability to trigger kvcache reconstruction jobs, pause model-swap-agent rollouts, and modify scheduler admission controls.
- Contacts: runtime-oncall@redwood.ai, kernel-scheduler@redwood.ai, runtime-release@redwood.ai

Pre-incident checklist (quick)
-----------------------------
- Confirm current SLA window and active deployments.
- Check if any maintenance or known rollouts are in progress (model-swap-agent, kernel rollouts).
- Ensure a recent snapshot of kvcache index exists (last 24h).
- Open a dedicated incident channel and note affected clusters/regions.

Immediate triage (first 10 minutes)
-----------------------------------
1) Tag and isolate: Identify affected shard sets and tag them in the incident channel. Use the runtime incident tool:  `rtctl shards list --filter=\"eviction_rate>0.1\"`
2) Confirm metric correlations: Dashboard: Runtime / KVCACHE / Reconstruction. Check metrics: eviction_rate, kvcache_reconstruction_failures, kvcache_warmup_seconds, kernel_handoff_rate, p95_latency.
3) Stop compounding actions: Pause any model-swap-agent rollouts for affected model-versions: `rtctl rollout pause --model vX --clusters shard-a,shard-b`.
4) Reduce admission pressure: Temporarily tighten admission control to reduce new sessions to affected shards: `schedulerctl admission set --target 60% --scope shard-a`

Automated reconstruction flow (recommended)
------------------------------------------
Use the automated reconstruction pipeline (recon-runner) to attempt fast rewind and state rebuild before manual steps. Steps it performs:
- Fetch latest durable prefix snapshots from persistent-store (S3 / object-store).
- Rehydrate session KV caches using checkpointed KV deltas.
- Replay last N tokens from request logs (N default 32) into warmed kernel contexts to re-establish KV validity.
- Validate by running synthetic trace assertions.

Command to start automated run on a shard set:
- `recon-runner start --shards shard-a,shard-b --max-parallel 4 --replay-window 32 --dry-run=false`

Manual reconstruction procedure (if automated runner fails)
---------------------------------------------------------
1) Stop accepting new traffic to affected shards: `schedulerctl drain --shard shard-a --mode immediate`
2) Snapshot live kernel state (if available): `rtctl kernel snapshot --shard shard-a --out /tmp/shard-a-snap.tgz`
3) Extract last-request logs for affected sessions: `logql query \"{service=\"runtime-gpu-proxy\", shard=\"shard-a\"} | json | last(5000)\" > /tmp/shard-a-requests.ndjson`
4) Run replay tool in isolated reconstruction cluster (no external traffic):
   a) `replay-tool --input /tmp/shard-a-requests.ndjson --model-model-path gs://models/vX --kv-checkpoint gs://kvcache/snapshots/shard-a/latest --replay-window 64 --out /tmp/shard-a-replay-results.json`
   b) Inspect replay failures: `jq '.failures | length' /tmp/shard-a-replay-results.json`
5) Apply KV deltas to in-memory cache with controlled rate-limiter: `kvcachectl apply-deltas --shard shard-a --source /tmp/deltas --rate 1000/s`
6) Re-enable traffic with traffic shaping: `schedulerctl admission set --target 30% --ramp 10m --scope shard-a`

Verification checks (post-reconstruction)
----------------------------------------
- Metric convergence: kvcache_reconstruction_failures -> 0/min; kvcache_hit_rate >= previous baseline - 2% within 15 minutes.
- Synthetic assertion: run 100 representative prompts (from canary prompt-set) against reconstructed shards and assert token-by-token parity >= 99.5% (or within acceptable fidelity reg thresholds).
- Latency: p95 within 10% of baseline for reconstructed shards.
- Error logs: no new kernel panics or repeated model-fallback events for 30 minutes.

Rollback and fallback policy
---------------------------
If reconstruction fails or fidelity is unacceptable:
- Immediately revert model swap (if the swap caused the state divergence) using: `rtctl rollout revert --model vX --to vX-previous --scope shard-a`
- If revert is not possible, route affected traffic to a hardened fallback pool (fallback-tier-2) with conservative admission limits.
- Mark incident as "stalled reconstruction" and escalate to runtime product and kernel-scheduler leads.

Post-incident actions (within 72 hours)
--------------------------------------
- Run a formal postmortem with causal timeline, including kernel handoff signals and eviction bursts.
- Bake-in new canary checks if missing: add warmup_failure assertion to canary pipeline.
- Create a blocker ticket for any persistent root cause (e.g., persistent NVMe contention, faulty eviction heuristics).

Testing and canary protocol for recon changes
--------------------------------------------
1) Unit tests for recon-runner with synthetic KV deltas and deterministic replay.
2) Integration test: deploy recon-runner in canary A (single shard) with traffic mirror of 2% for 2 hours. Assert: warmup_success=true, reconstruction_latency < 300s.
3) Rollout: gradual - 5% clusters -> 25% -> 100% with automated rollback on warmup failure.

Telemetry and dashboards to consult
----------------------------------
- Runtime / KVCACHE / Eviction Rate (eviction_rate)
- Runtime / KVCACHE / Reconstruction Failures (kvcache_reconstruction_failures)
- Runtime / KVCACHE / Warmup Duration (kvcache_warmup_seconds)
- Runtime / Kernel / Handoff Rate (kernel_handoff_rate)
- APIGW / Inference / p95 latency (p95_inference_latency)
- Runtime / Synthetic / Canary Fidelity (canary_token_parity)

Diagnostic commands and snippets
-------------------------------
- List active recon jobs: `reconctl list --status active`
- Tail kernel logs for shard: `kubectl -n runtime logs -l shard=shard-a -c kernel --since=20m | grep -i eviction`
- Inspect last kvcache checkpoint manifest: `gsutil cat gs://kvcache/snapshots/shard-a/manifest.json | jq`

Config table (important params)
------------------------------
Parameter | Default | Purpose / Impact
--------- | ------- | ------------------
replay_window | 32 | Number of tokens replayed to reconstruct KV. Increasing improves fidelity but increases time.
max_parallel_recon | 4 | Maximum parallel reconstruction jobs per region. Higher value speeds recovery but raises load.
apply_delta_rate | 1000/s | Rate at which KV deltas are applied to in-memory kvcache to avoid memory spikes.
snapshot_ttl | 24h | How long persistent snapshots are kept. Longer TTL increases recovery surface but costs storage.
warmup_assertion_threshold | 0.995 | Minimum token parity to consider reconstruction successful.

Example reconstruction config (YAML)
-----------------------------------
reconstruction:
  shards: ["shard-a", "shard-b"]
  replay_window: 64
  max_parallel_recon: 2
  apply_delta_rate: 750/s
  synthetic_canary_set: "canary-recon-v2"
  notify_on: ["success","failure"]

Safety notes and caveats
------------------------
- Never apply deltas across model-version boundaries unless the model architecture and tokenizer are compatible. Cross-version KV application will produce corruption.
- If kernel snapshot feature is unavailable for a shard, prefer routing traffic away rather than performing blind reconstruction.
- Reconstruction may surface latent bugs in quantization path; watch for sudden increase in soft-errors from kernel telemetry.

Appendix: quick checklist (one-pager)
------------------------------------
1) Pause rollouts and tag shards.
2) Run recon-runner (dry-run first if time permits).
3) If recon-runner fails, drain shard and run replay-tool in isolated cluster.
4) Apply KV deltas with rate-limiter and re-enable traffic with ramp.
5) Verify fidelity with canary prompt-set and metrics.
6) Revert or fallback if fidelity/latency unacceptable.

Related links and artifacts
--------------------------
- Kernel signal handbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/eviction-coordinate-protocol-and-kernel-signal-handbook-2026
- Reconstruction automation repo: git@github.com:redwood-inference/recon-runner
- Canary prompt sets: gs://runtime-canaries/canary-recon-v2/prompts.ndjson

Document history
----------------
- 2026-01-15: Created by Sophie Chen (initial draft).
- 2026-04-08: Added automated recon-runner section and YAML config (Marco Alvarez).
- 2026-07-02: Updated verification thresholds and telemetry lists (Dmitri Sokolov).

title: Eviction Rewind and State Reconstruction Playbook
preview:
Overview
========
This playbook documents the procedure to recover from kvcache eviction events that cause request-level state divergence or incomplete KV reconstruction during model swaps, preemption, or kernel handoffs. It covers triage, automated rewind and state reconstruction action sequences, verification checks, rollback criteria, and safety tests. The intended audience is oncall SREs, serving-runtime engineers, and incident responders.

Why this matters
-----------------
- Eviction-induced state gaps can lead to incorrect completions, user-facing hallucinations, or repeated model fallbacks.
- Fast, deterministic reconstruction preserves SLOs and avoids large-scale reroutes to lower-fidelity fallback models.
- This playbook provides a safe, repeatable sequence to reconstruct per-session KV state and safely resume traffic.

When to run this playbook
-------------------------
Run this playbook when one or more of the following are observed:
- Sudden spike in kvcache_reconstruction_failures (> 10/min sustained for 5 minutes)
- Increase in tail latency correlated with eviction_rate or kernel_handoff_rate
- Model swap incidents where > 0.5% of sessions show token regression or repeated left-pad artifacts
- Automated canary signals mark warmup_failure=true during rollout

Impact and SLOs
---------------
- Target: recover affected sessions and restore p95 inference latency within 10 minutes of start of remediation.
- Acceptable interim error budget consumption: up to 2x normal error budget for the affected service for a single incident.
- Escalate to incident commander if reconstruction extends beyond 30 minutes or if reconstruction failures exceed 1% of total affected sessions.

Ownership and permissions
-------------------------
- Primary owner: Serving Runtime (runtime-sre)
- Secondary owner: Kernel Scheduling Team (kernel-scheduler)
- Required permissions: ability to trigger kvcache reconstruction jobs, pause model-swap-agent rollouts, and modify scheduler admission controls.
- Contacts: runtime-oncall@redwood.ai, kernel-scheduler@redwood.ai, runtime-release@redwood.ai

Pre-incident checklist (quick)
-----------------------------
- Confirm current SLA window and active deployments.
- Check if any maintenance or known rollouts are in progress (model-swap-agent, kernel rollouts).
- Ensure a recent snapshot of kvcache index exists (last 24h).
- Open a dedicated incident channel and note affected clusters/regions.

Immediate triage (first 10 minutes)
-----------------------------------
1) Tag and isolate: Identify affected shard sets and tag them in the incident channel. Use the runtime incident tool:  `rtctl shards list --filter=\"eviction_rate>0.1\"`
2) Confirm metric correlations: Dashboard: Runtime / KVCACHE / Reconstruction. Check metrics: eviction_rate, kvcache_reconstruction_failures, kvcache_warmup_seconds, kernel_handoff_rate, p95_latency.
3) Stop compounding actions: Pause any model-swap-agent rollouts for affected model-versions: `rtctl rollout pause --model vX --clusters shard-a,shard-b`.
4) Reduce admission pressure: Temporarily tighten admission control to reduce new sessions to affected shards: `schedulerctl admission set --target 60% --scope shard-a`

Automated reconstruction flow (recommended)
------------------------------------------
Use the automated reconstruction pipeline (recon-runner) to attempt fast rewind and state rebuild before manual steps. Steps it performs:
- Fetch latest durable prefix snapshots from persistent-store (S3 / object-store).
- Rehydrate session KV caches using checkpointed KV deltas.
- Replay last N tokens from request logs (N default 32) into warmed kernel contexts to re-establish KV validity.
- Validate by running synthetic trace assertions.

Command to start automated run on a shard set:
- `recon-runner start --shards shard-a,shard-b --max-parallel 4 --replay-window 32 --dry-run=false`

Manual reconstruction procedure (if automated runner fails)
---------------------------------------------------------
1) Stop accepting new traffic to affected shards: `schedulerctl drain --shard shard-a --mode immediate`
2) Snapshot live kernel state (if available): `rtctl kernel snapshot --shard shard-a --out /tmp/shard-a-snap.tgz`
3) Extract last-request logs for affected sessions: `logql query \"{service=\"runtime-gpu-proxy\", shard=\"shard-a\"} | json | last(5000)\" > /tmp/shard-a-requests.ndjson`
4) Run replay tool in isolated reconstruction cluster (no external traffic):
   a) `replay-tool --input /tmp/shard-a-requests.ndjson --model-model-path gs://models/vX --kv-checkpoint gs://kvcache/snapshots/shard-a/latest --replay-window 64 --out /tmp/shard-a-replay-results.json`
   b) Inspect replay failures: `jq '.failures | length' /tmp/shard-a-replay-results.json`
5) Apply KV deltas to in-memory cache with controlled rate-limiter: `kvcachectl apply-deltas --shard shard-a --source /tmp/deltas --rate 1000/s`
6) Re-enable traffic with traffic shaping: `schedulerctl admission set --target 30% --ramp 10m --scope shard-a`

Verification checks (post-reconstruction)
----------------------------------------
- Metric convergence: kvcache_reconstruction_failures -> 0/min; kvcache_hit_rate >= previous baseline - 2% within 15 minutes.
- Synthetic assertion: run 100 representative prompts (from canary prompt-set) against reconstructed shards and assert token-by-token parity >= 99.5% (or within acceptable fidelity reg thresholds).
- Latency: p95 within 10% of baseline for reconstructed shards.
- Error logs: no new kernel panics or repeated model-fallback events for 30 minutes.

Rollback and fallback policy
---------------------------
If reconstruction fails or fidelity is unacceptable:
- Immediately revert model swap (if the swap caused the state divergence) using: `rtctl rollout revert --model vX --to vX-previous --scope shard-a`
- If revert is not possible, route affected traffic to a hardened fallback pool (fallback-tier-2) with conservative admission limits.
- Mark incident as "stalled reconstruction" and escalate to runtime product and kernel-scheduler leads.

Post-incident actions (within 72 hours)
--------------------------------------
- Run a formal postmortem with causal timeline, including kernel handoff signals and eviction bursts.
- Bake-in new canary checks if missing: add warmup_failure assertion to canary pipeline.
- Create a blocker ticket for any persistent root cause (e.g., persistent NVMe contention, faulty eviction heuristics).

Testing and canary protocol for recon changes
--------------------------------------------
1) Unit tests for recon-runner with synthetic KV deltas and deterministic replay.
2) Integration test: deploy recon-runner in canary A (single shard) with traffic mirror of 2% for 2 hours. Assert: warmup_success=true, reconstruction_latency < 300s.
3) Rollout: gradual - 5% clusters -> 25% -> 100% with automated rollback on warmup failure.

Telemetry and dashboards to consult
----------------------------------
- Runtime / KVCACHE / Eviction Rate (eviction_rate)
- Runtime / KVCACHE / Reconstruction Failures (kvcache_reconstruction_failures)
- Runtime / KVCACHE / Warmup Duration (kvcache_warmup_seconds)
- Runtime / Kernel / Handoff Rate (kernel_handoff_rate)
- APIGW / Inference / p95 latency (p95_inference_latency)
- Runtime / Synthetic / Canary Fidelity (canary_token_parity)

Diagnostic commands and snippets
-------------------------------
- List active recon jobs: `reconctl list --status active`
- Tail kernel logs for shard: `kubectl -n runtime logs -l shard=shard-a -c kernel --since=20m | grep -i eviction`
- Inspect last kvcache checkpoint manifest: `gsutil cat gs://kvcache/snapshots/shard-a/manifest.json | jq`

Config table (important params)
------------------------------
Parameter | Default | Purpose / Impact
--------- | ------- | ------------------
replay_window | 32 | Number of tokens replayed to reconstruct KV. Increasing improves fidelity but increases time.
max_parallel_recon | 4 | Maximum parallel reconstruction jobs per region. Higher value speeds recovery but raises load.
apply_delta_rate | 1000/s | Rate at which KV deltas are applied to in-memory kvcache to avoid memory spikes.
snapshot_ttl | 24h | How long persistent snapshots are kept. Longer TTL increases recovery surface but costs storage.
warmup_assertion_threshold | 0.995 | Minimum token parity to consider reconstruction successful.

Example reconstruction config (YAML)
-----------------------------------
reconstruction:
  shards: ["shard-a", "shard-b"]
  replay_window: 64
  max_parallel_recon: 2
  apply_delta_rate: 750/s
  synthetic_canary_set: "canary-recon-v2"
  notify_on: ["success","failure"]

Safety notes and caveats
------------------------
- Never apply deltas across model-version boundaries unless the model architecture and tokenizer are compatible. Cross-version KV application will produce corruption.
- If kernel snapshot feature is unavailable for a shard, prefer routing traffic away rather than performing blind reconstruction.
- Reconstruction may surface latent bugs in quantization path; watch for sudden increase in soft-errors from kernel telemetry.

Appendix: quick checklist (one-pager)
------------------------------------
1) Pause rollouts and tag shards.
2) Run recon-runner (dry-run first if time permits).
3) If recon-runner fails, drain shard and run replay-tool in isolated cluster.
4) Apply KV deltas with rate-limiter and re-enable traffic with ramp.
5) Verify fidelity with canary prompt-set and metrics.
6) Revert or fallback if fidelity/latency unacceptable.

Related links and artifacts
--------------------------
- Kernel signal handbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/kernel-and-scheduling/eviction-coordinate-protocol-and-kernel-signal-handbook-2026
- Reconstruction automation repo: git@github.com:redwood-inference/recon-runner
- Canary prompt sets: gs://runtime-canaries/canary-recon-v2/prompts.ndjson

Document history
----------------
- 2026-01-15: Created by Sophie Chen (initial draft).
- 2026-04-08: Added automated recon-runner section and YAML config (Marco Alvarez).
- 2026-07-02: Updated verification thresholds and telemetry lists (Dmitri Sokolov).

source.title: Fallback validation and chaos test plan for graceful runtime fallbacks
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Purpose
-------
This document defines a repeatable validation and chaos-testing plan for the runtime's graceful fallback paths. It is intended to: verify correctness and SLA-preservation when primary kernels, model variants, or regions become unavailable; exercise fallthrough policies and automated rollback; and validate telemetry/alert fidelity used for automated mitigation. The goal is to catch correctness, performance regressions, and observability gaps before customer impact.

Scope
-----
- Components: kernel selector, request router, model-variant manager, KV cache coordination, shard handoff logic, admission controller, and routing fallbacks (region/model/tier).
- Test goals: functional correctness (response validity), latency SLO preservation (p50/p95/p99 targets), cost/capacity boundary behavior, and signal hygiene (trace/span/metric availability).
- Exclusions: hardware-level power failures and long-lived data-plane networking partitions (these are handled by infra-chaos).

Success Criteria (pass/fail)
--------------------------
1) Functional correctness: 100% of golden prompt set must return syntactically valid responses for the active fidelity target, with no malformed tokens or KV desync artifacts.
2) Latency preservation: during fallback exercises, p95 must not exceed configured fallback-SLO-multiplier x baseline p95 (default multiplier = 2.5) for more than 60s continuously.
3) Error budget preservation: fallback exercises must not consume >1% of monthly error budget for the affected tenant pool.
4) Observability: every fallback action must emit a correlated trace/span and a metric tag (fallback_type + reason) within 2s of policy firing.
5) Rollback/Recovery: automatic rollback to previous stable variant must succeed within 5 minutes for canary rollouts that experience degraded quality.

Test Plan Overview
------------------
1) Canary/Unit tests (CI): validate logic paths with deterministic mocks. Run for every kernel-scheduler or fallback-policy change.
2) Staging validation (integration): run the full plan against a staging cluster that mirrors production routing and telemetry collection.
3) Controlled chaos in canary prod (low-traffic window): short, scheduled experiments that validate production signals and playbook correctness.
4) Escalation rehearsals: practice runbook steps with oncall and SRE to measure human response and timing.

Test Types and Procedures
-------------------------
A. Kernel Unavailability Simulation
- Purpose: ensure the kernel mapper and admission controller fall back to compatible kernels or model variants without corrupting KV cache or client sessions.
- Pre-conditions: baseline throughput at 20% of steady-state; golden prompt set loaded and pinned.
- Steps:
  1. Inject kernel SIGSTOP via test harness on a subset of worker processes (3 nodes) to simulate a hung kernel.
  2. Observe: request router should divert new requests away from affected kernels after 3 failed scheduling attempts.
  3. Verify: no KV cache corruption events in logs; sessions tied to affected kernels are migrated or retried.
  4. Metrics to monitor: kernel_failure_count, kernel_schedule_retries, kvcache_migration_events, request_reroute_ratio.
- Pass/Fail:
  - Pass if golden prompts succeed within retry policy and no cache mismatches are observed.

B. Model Variant Fallback and Quality Validation
- Purpose: validate that downgrading fidelity (e.g., 16k->8k context, quantized variant) preserves semantic correctness and that the quality monitoring pipeline will detect regressions.
- Steps:
  1. Trigger variant swap for a small subset (1% traffic) using the deployment controller to a quantized variant.
  2. Feed continuous golden prompt set and synthetic adversarial prompts aimed at edge-cases.
  3. Capture outputs and run automatic evaluation (embedding-similarity + token-level diff + custom assertions).
  4. Assert alerts are fired by the quality monitor if quality drop > configured threshold (default 6% drop in embedding similarity).
- Tools: rollout-cli, quality-eval-harness, embeddings-service, diff-checker script.

C. Regional Failover and Routing Policy Exercise
- Purpose: verify smart routing and cost-aware fallback preserves SLOs when a region is slowed or unreachable.
- Steps:
  1. Simulate increased regional latency by adding artificial delay on the regional egress (tc/netem) in staging.
  2. Confirm tiered routing policy triggers (region_fallback_count metric increments) and traffic is rebalanced to alternate regions according to configured policy.
  3. Validate that per-tenant token cost does not spike beyond policy limits and that route trace contains fallback annotations.

D. KV Cache Warmup and Eviction Race Scenario
- Purpose: confirm that when primary shard evicts and fallback uses cold cache, responses are still correct and warmup telemetry is produced for downstream autotune.
- Steps:
  1. Force eviction on a KV shard by lowering memory watermark through the admin API.
  2. Route live sessions and verify fallback returns coherent responses while autoscaler warms new copies.
  3. Confirm warmup_latency, kvcache_miss_ratio, and kvcache_prefetch_hints metrics are emitted.

E. Admission Backpressure and Queue-Starvation
- Purpose: ensure admission controller rejects or queues work as designed and that graceful degradation path (reduced tokens, partial response) is triggered for overload.
- Steps:
  1. Generate synthetic burst (10x normal p50 throughput) to the cluster.
  2. Verify admission decisions, queue lengths, and that the partial-result fallback path returns truncated but valid outputs with a clear header "X-Redwood-Fallback-Reason: partial-response".

Observability & Telemetry Requirements
--------------------------------------
Every experiment must validate the following signals:
- Traces: fallback.<type>.decision (contains request_id, policy_version, reason_code, target_variant)
- Metrics (per-tenant and per-route): fallback_count, fallback_latency_ms, fallback_success_ratio, fallback_recovery_time_s
- Logs: structured JSON logs containing fallback_type, node_id, kernel_id, kv_shard_id, and experiment_tag
- Dashboards: a temporary experiment dashboard must exist for the run including p50/p95/p99, error_rates, fallback_count, and quality_eval_score

Experiment Tagging Convention
-----------------------------
Use the tag: "chaos-fallback-{YYYYMMDD}-{short-owner}" in all telemetry, e.g., chaos-fallback-20260615-priya. This ensures artifacts are searchable and excluded from production SLO calculations where appropriate.

Automated Validation Scripts (examples)
--------------------------------------
- Verify fallback emitted trace:
  curl -s "${TRACE_API}/search?tag=chaos-fallback-20260615-priya&event=fallback.decision" | jq '.hits | length'

- Run embedding similarity check:
  python3 tools/quality_eval.py --gold staples/golden_prompts.jsonl --candidate outputs/experiment_run.jsonl --threshold 0.94

Run Schedule and Roles
----------------------
- Preflight (SRE + Runtime owner): 1 week before run - verify staging mirrors production and golden sets are recent.
- Dry run (Runtime + Observability): 48 hours before - run full plan in staging.
- Production canary window (SRE lead, Runtime lead, Oncall): scheduled low-traffic window (UTC 02:00 - 05:00).
- Postmortem / Debrief: within 48 hours, include action items and telemetry gaps.

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Runtime Owner: Priya Natarajan (runtime-owner@redwood.example.com)
- SRE Lead: Ethan Morales (sre-lead@redwood.example.com)
- Observability: Lina Zhang (obs@redwood.example.com)
- Quality/Applied ML: Carlos Rivera (quality@redwood.example.com)

Post-experiment Runbook (if failure observed)
--------------------------------------------
1. Immediate containment: revert to previous stable routing policy or pause the rollout (rollout-cli rollback --name <rollout> --reason "chaos-fallback-failure").
2. Triage: collect traces, kernel logs, kvcache snapshots, and raw request/response pairs for a 30-minute window around the failure.
3. Hotfix: if the issue is a missing trace/metric, re-enable telemetry flags and re-run short smoke test. If the issue is correctness (tokenization, alignment), pause rollout and open a quality incident.
4. Communication: notify customer-facing teams if user-visible SLOs were affected and update status page if >5% of tenants impacted.
5. Retrospective: schedule a 1-hour postmortem within 72 hours and assign action owners.

Artifacts to Produce
--------------------
- Experiment run artifact bundle: traces, metrics snapshots (prometheus dump), logs, and evaluation outputs (embedding similarity scores and diffs).
- Short executive summary (1 page) with pass/fail and recommended actions.
- Open ticket list with ownership and SLAs for remediation.

Appendix: Sample configuration matrix
------------------------------------
| Experiment | Traffic fraction | Baseline p95 multiplier | Max duration | Owner |
| Kernel hang | 2% | 2.0 | 15m | Runtime |
| Variant swap | 1% | 1.5 | 30m | Quality |
| Region degrade | 5% | 2.5 | 10m | SRE |
| KV eviction | 3% | 2.0 | 20m | Runtime |
| Admission overload | 10% | 3.0 | 5m | SRE |
Notes and best practices
------------------------
- Always run against a pin-provisioned canary tenant to avoid accidental large-scale customer impact.
- Keep golden prompt sets versioned and representative (include adversarial edge-cases).
- Ensure experiment tags are excluded from billing and SLO retrospectives via query filters.
- Prefer short, iterative experiments over long-running undifferentiated chaos.

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-03-10: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2026-05-12: Added embedding similarity thresholds and automated validation scripts (reviewer: Lina Zhang).
- 2026-06-30: Expanded observability requirements and post-experiment runbook (reviewer: Ethan Morales).

Related pages
-------------
See also runtime decision graphs, fallback playbooks, and canary rollout guidelines: the related_pages list at the top contains the primary references.

End of document

title: Fallback validation and chaos test plan for graceful runtime fallbacks
preview:
Purpose
-------
This document defines a repeatable validation and chaos-testing plan for the runtime's graceful fallback paths. It is intended to: verify correctness and SLA-preservation when primary kernels, model variants, or regions become unavailable; exercise fallthrough policies and automated rollback; and validate telemetry/alert fidelity used for automated mitigation. The goal is to catch correctness, performance regressions, and observability gaps before customer impact.

Scope
-----
- Components: kernel selector, request router, model-variant manager, KV cache coordination, shard handoff logic, admission controller, and routing fallbacks (region/model/tier).
- Test goals: functional correctness (response validity), latency SLO preservation (p50/p95/p99 targets), cost/capacity boundary behavior, and signal hygiene (trace/span/metric availability).
- Exclusions: hardware-level power failures and long-lived data-plane networking partitions (these are handled by infra-chaos).

Success Criteria (pass/fail)
--------------------------
1) Functional correctness: 100% of golden prompt set must return syntactically valid responses for the active fidelity target, with no malformed tokens or KV desync artifacts.
2) Latency preservation: during fallback exercises, p95 must not exceed configured fallback-SLO-multiplier x baseline p95 (default multiplier = 2.5) for more than 60s continuously.
3) Error budget preservation: fallback exercises must not consume >1% of monthly error budget for the affected tenant pool.
4) Observability: every fallback action must emit a correlated trace/span and a metric tag (fallback_type + reason) within 2s of policy firing.
5) Rollback/Recovery: automatic rollback to previous stable variant must succeed within 5 minutes for canary rollouts that experience degraded quality.

Test Plan Overview
------------------
1) Canary/Unit tests (CI): validate logic paths with deterministic mocks. Run for every kernel-scheduler or fallback-policy change.
2) Staging validation (integration): run the full plan against a staging cluster that mirrors production routing and telemetry collection.
3) Controlled chaos in canary prod (low-traffic window): short, scheduled experiments that validate production signals and playbook correctness.
4) Escalation rehearsals: practice runbook steps with oncall and SRE to measure human response and timing.

Test Types and Procedures
-------------------------
A. Kernel Unavailability Simulation
- Purpose: ensure the kernel mapper and admission controller fall back to compatible kernels or model variants without corrupting KV cache or client sessions.
- Pre-conditions: baseline throughput at 20% of steady-state; golden prompt set loaded and pinned.
- Steps:
  1. Inject kernel SIGSTOP via test harness on a subset of worker processes (3 nodes) to simulate a hung kernel.
  2. Observe: request router should divert new requests away from affected kernels after 3 failed scheduling attempts.
  3. Verify: no KV cache corruption events in logs; sessions tied to affected kernels are migrated or retried.
  4. Metrics to monitor: kernel_failure_count, kernel_schedule_retries, kvcache_migration_events, request_reroute_ratio.
- Pass/Fail:
  - Pass if golden prompts succeed within retry policy and no cache mismatches are observed.

B. Model Variant Fallback and Quality Validation
- Purpose: validate that downgrading fidelity (e.g., 16k->8k context, quantized variant) preserves semantic correctness and that the quality monitoring pipeline will detect regressions.
- Steps:
  1. Trigger variant swap for a small subset (1% traffic) using the deployment controller to a quantized variant.
  2. Feed continuous golden prompt set and synthetic adversarial prompts aimed at edge-cases.
  3. Capture outputs and run automatic evaluation (embedding-similarity + token-level diff + custom assertions).
  4. Assert alerts are fired by the quality monitor if quality drop > configured threshold (default 6% drop in embedding similarity).
- Tools: rollout-cli, quality-eval-harness, embeddings-service, diff-checker script.

C. Regional Failover and Routing Policy Exercise
- Purpose: verify smart routing and cost-aware fallback preserves SLOs when a region is slowed or unreachable.
- Steps:
  1. Simulate increased regional latency by adding artificial delay on the regional egress (tc/netem) in staging.
  2. Confirm tiered routing policy triggers (region_fallback_count metric increments) and traffic is rebalanced to alternate regions according to configured policy.
  3. Validate that per-tenant token cost does not spike beyond policy limits and that route trace contains fallback annotations.

D. KV Cache Warmup and Eviction Race Scenario
- Purpose: confirm that when primary shard evicts and fallback uses cold cache, responses are still correct and warmup telemetry is produced for downstream autotune.
- Steps:
  1. Force eviction on a KV shard by lowering memory watermark through the admin API.
  2. Route live sessions and verify fallback returns coherent responses while autoscaler warms new copies.
  3. Confirm warmup_latency, kvcache_miss_ratio, and kvcache_prefetch_hints metrics are emitted.

E. Admission Backpressure and Queue-Starvation
- Purpose: ensure admission controller rejects or queues work as designed and that graceful degradation path (reduced tokens, partial response) is triggered for overload.
- Steps:
  1. Generate synthetic burst (10x normal p50 throughput) to the cluster.
  2. Verify admission decisions, queue lengths, and that the partial-result fallback path returns truncated but valid outputs with a clear header "X-Redwood-Fallback-Reason: partial-response".

Observability & Telemetry Requirements
--------------------------------------
Every experiment must validate the following signals:
- Traces: fallback.<type>.decision (contains request_id, policy_version, reason_code, target_variant)
- Metrics (per-tenant and per-route): fallback_count, fallback_latency_ms, fallback_success_ratio, fallback_recovery_time_s
- Logs: structured JSON logs containing fallback_type, node_id, kernel_id, kv_shard_id, and experiment_tag
- Dashboards: a temporary experiment dashboard must exist for the run including p50/p95/p99, error_rates, fallback_count, and quality_eval_score

Experiment Tagging Convention
-----------------------------
Use the tag: "chaos-fallback-{YYYYMMDD}-{short-owner}" in all telemetry, e.g., chaos-fallback-20260615-priya. This ensures artifacts are searchable and excluded from production SLO calculations where appropriate.

Automated Validation Scripts (examples)
--------------------------------------
- Verify fallback emitted trace:
  curl -s "${TRACE_API}/search?tag=chaos-fallback-20260615-priya&event=fallback.decision" | jq '.hits | length'

- Run embedding similarity check:
  python3 tools/quality_eval.py --gold staples/golden_prompts.jsonl --candidate outputs/experiment_run.jsonl --threshold 0.94

Run Schedule and Roles
----------------------
- Preflight (SRE + Runtime owner): 1 week before run - verify staging mirrors production and golden sets are recent.
- Dry run (Runtime + Observability): 48 hours before - run full plan in staging.
- Production canary window (SRE lead, Runtime lead, Oncall): scheduled low-traffic window (UTC 02:00 - 05:00).
- Postmortem / Debrief: within 48 hours, include action items and telemetry gaps.

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Runtime Owner: Priya Natarajan (runtime-owner@redwood.example.com)
- SRE Lead: Ethan Morales (sre-lead@redwood.example.com)
- Observability: Lina Zhang (obs@redwood.example.com)
- Quality/Applied ML: Carlos Rivera (quality@redwood.example.com)

Post-experiment Runbook (if failure observed)
--------------------------------------------
1. Immediate containment: revert to previous stable routing policy or pause the rollout (rollout-cli rollback --name <rollout> --reason "chaos-fallback-failure").
2. Triage: collect traces, kernel logs, kvcache snapshots, and raw request/response pairs for a 30-minute window around the failure.
3. Hotfix: if the issue is a missing trace/metric, re-enable telemetry flags and re-run short smoke test. If the issue is correctness (tokenization, alignment), pause rollout and open a quality incident.
4. Communication: notify customer-facing teams if user-visible SLOs were affected and update status page if >5% of tenants impacted.
5. Retrospective: schedule a 1-hour postmortem within 72 hours and assign action owners.

Artifacts to Produce
--------------------
- Experiment run artifact bundle: traces, metrics snapshots (prometheus dump), logs, and evaluation outputs (embedding similarity scores and diffs).
- Short executive summary (1 page) with pass/fail and recommended actions.
- Open ticket list with ownership and SLAs for remediation.

Appendix: Sample configuration matrix
------------------------------------
| Experiment | Traffic fraction | Baseline p95 multiplier | Max duration | Owner |
| Kernel hang | 2% | 2.0 | 15m | Runtime |
| Variant swap | 1% | 1.5 | 30m | Quality |
| Region degrade | 5% | 2.5 | 10m | SRE |
| KV eviction | 3% | 2.0 | 20m | Runtime |
| Admission overload | 10% | 3.0 | 5m | SRE |
Notes and best practices
------------------------
- Always run against a pin-provisioned canary tenant to avoid accidental large-scale customer impact.
- Keep golden prompt sets versioned and representative (include adversarial edge-cases).
- Ensure experiment tags are excluded from billing and SLO retrospectives via query filters.
- Prefer short, iterative experiments over long-running undifferentiated chaos.

Revision history
----------------
- 2026-03-10: Created by Priya Natarajan (initial draft).
- 2026-05-12: Added embedding similarity thresholds and automated validation scripts (reviewer: Lina Zhang).
- 2026-06-30: Expanded observability requirements and post-experiment runbook (reviewer: Ethan Morales).

Related pages
-------------
See also runtime decision graphs, fallback playbooks, and canary rollout guidelines: the related_pages list at the top contains the primary references.

End of document

source.title: Runtime 1.22 Release Notes: Workload-aware batching defaults (Fast / Standard / Cost-efficient tiers)
source.author: Marcus Reed
source.body:
## Overview
Runtime **v1.22** introduces **workload-aware batching defaults**: three customer-facing batching profiles aligned to latency SLO tiers (**fast**, **standard**, **cost-efficient**). The runtime now selects and enforces tier-appropriate scheduler knobs using workload signals (prompt-length buckets, streaming vs non-streaming, and concurrency) to improve throughput and $/token while preserving predictable tail latency.

This release includes:
- New **tiered default profiles** and a **selection/override hierarchy**
- **Tail-latency protections** for fast tier and guardrails for cost-efficient tier
- **Telemetry + reason codes** to explain “why this profile”
- **Tier-labeled metrics** with **cardinality controls**
- Rollout controls: **feature flag**, staged enablement, and kill switch

## What’s new
### 1) Three batching tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
The runtime now supports the following tiers:
- **Fast**: minimizes tail latency via tighter wait budgets, stricter admission control, and anti-starvation rules.
- **Standard**: balanced defaults for typical product traffic; moderate batching with stable streaming performance.
- **Cost-efficient**: maximizes throughput via more aggressive batching, with safeguards to prevent queue blowups during bursts.

Tier defaults are defined per model family and (when required) hardware class. If a model/hardware combination cannot safely support a tier’s default knob set, the runtime will fall back to a compatible profile and emit a reason code.

### 2) Workload-aware selection signals
Scheduler heuristics now incorporate:
- **Prompt length bucket** (prefill cost proxy)
- **Streaming flag** (streaming-first latency constraints)
- **Observed concurrency / queue pressure** (to prevent overload instability)

These signals influence batch formation and admission/backpressure behavior while respecting the chosen tier.

### 3) Safety mechanisms
- **Fast tier tail-latency protection**:
  - Tighter max-wait budget
  - Admission control under queue growth
  - Anti-starvation for long-prefill requests in mixed workloads
- **Cost-efficient guardrails**:
  - Max batch token caps
  - Queue depth clamps + early backpressure
  - “Overload mode” behavior standardized (see Known limitations)

### 4) Tier selection reason codes
The runtime emits structured reason codes for effective profile selection, including:
- `tier_default_applied`
- `route_policy_override`
- `customer_override_applied`
- `model_constraint_fallback`
- `hardware_constraint_fallback`
- `overload_mode_adjustment`
- `kill_switch_disabled`

These reason codes are intended to support Console explanations and to accelerate regression triage.

## Configuration changes
### New config surface
Runtime 1.22 adds a tier-aware schema for batching defaults.

**Primary configuration locations** (naming shown as currently implemented in control plane configs):
- Route/model policy: `batching.tier = fast|standard|cost_efficient`
- Cluster/tenant override (Dedicated): `overrides.batching.tier` and/or knob-level overrides

**Override precedence** (highest wins):
1. **Customer override** (Dedicated tenant/cluster)
2. **Route policy** (API route/model policy)
3. **Tier default** (runtime-recommended profile)
4. **Runtime safe fallback** (constraints / overload)

### Example: route policy
```yaml
# Example: Hosted API route policy
route: /v1/chat/completions
model: redwood/llama-3.1-70b
batching:
  tier: fast
```

### Example: Dedicated override
```yaml
# Example: Dedicated tenant override
tenant: acme-prod
overrides:
  batching:
    tier: standard
    # (optional) knob overrides remain supported for enterprise cases
    max_wait_ms: 1.5
```

### Backwards compatibility
- Existing knob-level configurations (manual batching settings) continue to be accepted.
- If both **tier** and manual knobs are set, the system follows the documented precedence (customer overrides first; within an override scope, explicit knobs take precedence over tier defaults).

## Migration notes
### Who needs to do something?
- **Hosted API customers**: no action required. Tier defaults are rolled out progressively behind a flag.
- **Dedicated customers**:
  - If you currently rely on manual knob tuning, you can keep it.
  - If you want the new defaults, opt in by setting `batching.tier` at the route policy or tenant override level.
- **Private (VPC/on-prem)**:
  - Defaults ship with runtime 1.22, but are disabled by default unless your deployment config enables the tiered defaults flag.

### Recommended migration path
1. Start with **standard** tier on representative routes.
2. Move latency-critical streaming endpoints to **fast**.
3. Move high-throughput async workloads (non-streaming) to **cost-efficient**.
4. Validate using tier dashboards (p95/p99, queue depth, batch tokens) and reason-code distribution.

### Deprecations
No deprecations in 1.22. (We will propose a deprecation plan for legacy-only knob presets after tier defaults are stable across top model families.)

## Observability & metrics added
### New dimensions and signals
Runtime 1.22 adds tier awareness to key latency/cost telemetry:
- Tier label (effective tier) on latency histograms and cost attribution
- Batch size distribution (tokens and requests)
- Queue depth and time-in-queue
- Prefill vs decode timing breakdown
- Reason code counters for profile selection

### Cardinality controls (important)
To avoid metric-cardinality blow-ups:
- Tier is emitted as a **bounded label** (`fast|standard|cost_efficient|fallback`)
- Model identifiers are emitted as a **stable model family key** where possible (full model name available via tracing/log event sampling)
- High-cardinality combinations are sampled for detailed traces rather than always-on metrics

If you see unexpected telemetry volume increases, verify that you are on the latest 1.22 patch with cardinality controls enabled and that custom tags are not reintroducing unbounded labels.

### Suggested dashboards to validate
See the dashboard spec for panel definitions; at minimum, validate:
- p95/p99 latency by tier (and by streaming flag)
- Queue depth p95 + sustained queue growth
- Batch tokens distribution (watch for “stuck at max”)
- Reason code distribution (ensure expected overrides are taking effect)

## Rollout controls
Tiered batching defaults are gated behind:
- Feature flag: `runtime.tiered_batching_defaults.enabled`
- Kill switch: `runtime.tiered_batching_defaults.kill_switch`

Staged rollout recommendation:
1. Internal canary routes
2. Hosted API subset (per-region)
3. Dedicated opt-in
4. Gradual broader default enablement

## Known limitations / behavior notes
### 1) Overload behavior differs by tier
Under sustained overload, the scheduler prioritizes predictable degradation:
- **Fast**: earlier admission control/backpressure to protect tail latency.
- **Cost-efficient**: will attempt larger batches within guardrails, but will enter backpressure sooner if queue depth grows beyond clamp.

If you depend on “best effort” behavior under overload (accepting higher tail latency instead of shedding), coordinate with Runtime/SRE before enabling fast tier broadly.

### 2) Mixed prompt length workloads
Although anti-starvation improvements landed in 1.22, heavily mixed workloads (very short + very long prompts) may still produce:
- Short-request latency wins with occasional long-request slowdowns in **cost-efficient**
- Fast-tier p99 sensitivity on select model families with large prefill variance

Use the mixed-workload perf scenarios in the perf suite and monitor reason codes indicating overload adjustments.

### 3) Streaming nuances
Tiering is designed to keep streaming “time-to-first-token” stable, but very small streaming chunk sizes combined with high concurrency can reduce batching opportunities. Expect **smaller throughput gains** for streaming-heavy traffic versus non-streaming.

### 4) Model/hardware constraints
Some quantized variants or specific kernels may restrict maximum effective batch tokens for stability. When constraints apply, the runtime will select a safe fallback and emit `model_constraint_fallback` or `hardware_constraint_fallback`.

## Operational notes (SRE/on-call)
- New alerts are intended to page only on **fast-tier p99 regressions** that exceed error-budget thresholds; other tier regressions should generally ticket unless correlated with elevated errors.
- When investigating regressions, start with:
  1) Confirm effective tier + reason code
  2) Check queue depth growth and overload mode triggers
  3) Compare prefill/decode timing shifts
  4) Validate that metric cardinality controls are in place

## Validation status (as of 2026-05-03)
- Internal canary: complete; no correctness issues observed.
- Hosted API: staged enablement in progress in primary NA/EU regions.
- Dedicated: opt-in available; ensure override precedence expectations are communicated to customers.

## Support / escalation
- Runtime on-call: #eng-runtime
- SRE escalation: #eng-sre
- Customer-facing guidance (docs/PM): #product-hosted-api, #docs

Include in escalation:
- Route/model, region, streaming flag
- Effective tier + top reason codes
- p95/p99 deltas and queue depth graphs
- Any custom overrides (tenant/route) currently applied

title: Runtime 1.22 Release Notes: Workload-aware batching defaults (Fast / Standard / Cost-efficient tiers)
preview:
## Overview
Runtime **v1.22** introduces **workload-aware batching defaults**: three customer-facing batching profiles aligned to latency SLO tiers (**fast**, **standard**, **cost-efficient**). The runtime now selects and enforces tier-appropriate scheduler knobs using workload signals (prompt-length buckets, streaming vs non-streaming, and concurrency) to improve throughput and $/token while preserving predictable tail latency.

This release includes:
- New **tiered default profiles** and a **selection/override hierarchy**
- **Tail-latency protections** for fast tier and guardrails for cost-efficient tier
- **Telemetry + reason codes** to explain “why this profile”
- **Tier-labeled metrics** with **cardinality controls**
- Rollout controls: **feature flag**, staged enablement, and kill switch

## What’s new
### 1) Three batching tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
The runtime now supports the following tiers:
- **Fast**: minimizes tail latency via tighter wait budgets, stricter admission control, and anti-starvation rules.
- **Standard**: balanced defaults for typical product traffic; moderate batching with stable streaming performance.
- **Cost-efficient**: maximizes throughput via more aggressive batching, with safeguards to prevent queue blowups during bursts.

Tier defaults are defined per model family and (when required) hardware class. If a model/hardware combination cannot safely support a tier’s default knob set, the runtime will fall back to a compatible profile and emit a reason code.

### 2) Workload-aware selection signals
Scheduler heuristics now incorporate:
- **Prompt length bucket** (prefill cost proxy)
- **Streaming flag** (streaming-first latency constraints)
- **Observed concurrency / queue pressure** (to prevent overload instability)

These signals influence batch formation and admission/backpressure behavior while respecting the chosen tier.

### 3) Safety mechanisms
- **Fast tier tail-latency protection**:
  - Tighter max-wait budget
  - Admission control under queue growth
  - Anti-starvation for long-prefill requests in mixed workloads
- **Cost-efficient guardrails**:
  - Max batch token caps
  - Queue depth clamps + early backpressure
  - “Overload mode” behavior standardized (see Known limitations)

### 4) Tier selection reason codes
The runtime emits structured reason codes for effective profile selection, including:
- `tier_default_applied`
- `route_policy_override`
- `customer_override_applied`
- `model_constraint_fallback`
- `hardware_constraint_fallback`
- `overload_mode_adjustment`
- `kill_switch_disabled`

These reason codes are intended to support Console explanations and to accelerate regression triage.

## Configuration changes
### New config surface
Runtime 1.22 adds a tier-aware schema for batching defaults.

**Primary configuration locations** (naming shown as currently implemented in control plane configs):
- Route/model policy: `batching.tier = fast|standard|cost_efficient`
- Cluster/tenant override (Dedicated): `overrides.batching.tier` and/or knob-level overrides

**Override precedence** (highest wins):
1. **Customer override** (Dedicated tenant/cluster)
2. **Route policy** (API route/model policy)
3. **Tier default** (runtime-recommended profile)
4. **Runtime safe fallback** (constraints / overload)

### Example: route policy
```yaml
# Example: Hosted API route policy
route: /v1/chat/completions
model: redwood/llama-3.1-70b
batching:
  tier: fast
```

### Example: Dedicated override
```yaml
# Example: Dedicated tenant override
tenant: acme-prod
overrides:
  batching:
    tier: standard
    # (optional) knob overrides remain supported for enterprise cases
    max_wait_ms: 1.5
```

### Backwards compatibility
- Existing knob-level configurations (manual batching settings) continue to be accepted.
- If both **tier** and manual knobs are set, the system follows the documented precedence (customer overrides first; within an override scope, explicit knobs take precedence over tier defaults).

## Migration notes
### Who needs to do something?
- **Hosted API customers**: no action required. Tier defaults are rolled out progressively behind a flag.
- **Dedicated customers**:
  - If you currently rely on manual knob tuning, you can keep it.
  - If you want the new defaults, opt in by setting `batching.tier` at the route policy or tenant override level.
- **Private (VPC/on-prem)**:
  - Defaults ship with runtime 1.22, but are disabled by default unless your deployment config enables the tiered defaults flag.

### Recommended migration path
1. Start with **standard** tier on representative routes.
2. Move latency-critical streaming endpoints to **fast**.
3. Move high-throughput async workloads (non-streaming) to **cost-efficient**.
4. Validate using tier dashboards (p95/p99, queue depth, batch tokens) and reason-code distribution.

### Deprecations
No deprecations in 1.22. (We will propose a deprecation plan for legacy-only knob presets after tier defaults are stable across top model families.)

## Observability & metrics added
### New dimensions and signals
Runtime 1.22 adds tier awareness to key latency/cost telemetry:
- Tier label (effective tier) on latency histograms and cost attribution
- Batch size distribution (tokens and requests)
- Queue depth and time-in-queue
- Prefill vs decode timing breakdown
- Reason code counters for profile selection

### Cardinality controls (important)
To avoid metric-cardinality blow-ups:
- Tier is emitted as a **bounded label** (`fast|standard|cost_efficient|fallback`)
- Model identifiers are emitted as a **stable model family key** where possible (full model name available via tracing/log event sampling)
- High-cardinality combinations are sampled for detailed traces rather than always-on metrics

If you see unexpected telemetry volume increases, verify that you are on the latest 1.22 patch with cardinality controls enabled and that custom tags are not reintroducing unbounded labels.

### Suggested dashboards to validate
See the dashboard spec for panel definitions; at minimum, validate:
- p95/p99 latency by tier (and by streaming flag)
- Queue depth p95 + sustained queue growth
- Batch tokens distribution (watch for “stuck at max”)
- Reason code distribution (ensure expected overrides are taking effect)

## Rollout controls
Tiered batching defaults are gated behind:
- Feature flag: `runtime.tiered_batching_defaults.enabled`
- Kill switch: `runtime.tiered_batching_defaults.kill_switch`

Staged rollout recommendation:
1. Internal canary routes
2. Hosted API subset (per-region)
3. Dedicated opt-in
4. Gradual broader default enablement

## Known limitations / behavior notes
### 1) Overload behavior differs by tier
Under sustained overload, the scheduler prioritizes predictable degradation:
- **Fast**: earlier admission control/backpressure to protect tail latency.
- **Cost-efficient**: will attempt larger batches within guardrails, but will enter backpressure sooner if queue depth grows beyond clamp.

If you depend on “best effort” behavior under overload (accepting higher tail latency instead of shedding), coordinate with Runtime/SRE before enabling fast tier broadly.

### 2) Mixed prompt length workloads
Although anti-starvation improvements landed in 1.22, heavily mixed workloads (very short + very long prompts) may still produce:
- Short-request latency wins with occasional long-request slowdowns in **cost-efficient**
- Fast-tier p99 sensitivity on select model families with large prefill variance

Use the mixed-workload perf scenarios in the perf suite and monitor reason codes indicating overload adjustments.

### 3) Streaming nuances
Tiering is designed to keep streaming “time-to-first-token” stable, but very small streaming chunk sizes combined with high concurrency can reduce batching opportunities. Expect **smaller throughput gains** for streaming-heavy traffic versus non-streaming.

### 4) Model/hardware constraints
Some quantized variants or specific kernels may restrict maximum effective batch tokens for stability. When constraints apply, the runtime will select a safe fallback and emit `model_constraint_fallback` or `hardware_constraint_fallback`.

## Operational notes (SRE/on-call)
- New alerts are intended to page only on **fast-tier p99 regressions** that exceed error-budget thresholds; other tier regressions should generally ticket unless correlated with elevated errors.
- When investigating regressions, start with:
  1) Confirm effective tier + reason code
  2) Check queue depth growth and overload mode triggers
  3) Compare prefill/decode timing shifts
  4) Validate that metric cardinality controls are in place

## Validation status (as of 2026-05-03)
- Internal canary: complete; no correctness issues observed.
- Hosted API: staged enablement in progress in primary NA/EU regions.
- Dedicated: opt-in available; ensure override precedence expectations are communicated to customers.

## Support / escalation
- Runtime on-call: #eng-runtime
- SRE escalation: #eng-sre
- Customer-facing guidance (docs/PM): #product-hosted-api, #docs

Include in escalation:
- Route/model, region, streaming flag
- Effective tier + top reason codes
- p95/p99 deltas and queue depth graphs
- Any custom overrides (tenant/route) currently applied

source.title: Cross-layer Experiment Matrix and Validation Checklist
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
Summary:
This document is a consolidated experiment matrix and validation checklist for cross-layer runtime experiments that touch kernel scheduling, KV-cache behavior, profiling instrumentation, and benchmark acceptance. It's intended for engineiners running controlled experiments that may affect SLOs and cost. Use this as the canonical checklist and runbook for planning, executing, and validating experiments that modify scheduler policies, kernel variants, or observation sampling rates.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: experiments affecting serving runtime execution path, kernel selection, batching, KV cache prefetch, and telemetry sampling.
- Audience: runtime engineers, SREs, performance researchers, release engineers.

High-level experiment categories (matrix):
1) Scheduling policy changes
  - Example: priority-driven crediting -> opportunistic slot coalescing
  - Impact areas: tail latency, fairness, kernel queue depth, eviction rate
2) Kernel variant rollouts
  - Example: fast-attention-v2 vs safe-attention-v1 (reduced memory, different numeric stability)
  - Impact: per-token CPU/GPU time, p99 latency, EPS (errors per second)
3) KV-cache behavior and prefetch tuning
  - Example: asynchronous prefetch enabled/disabled, prefetch window size
  - Impact: cold-starts, token generation latency, cache warmup time
4) Telemetry and sampling changes
  - Example: full traces -> sampled traces with adaptive enrichment
  - Impact: signal fidelity, profiling cost, root-cause analysis windows
5) Quantization/Kernel-precision changes
  - Example: fp16 -> int8 with calibration profile B
  - Impact: model quality regressions, kernel numerical edgecases

Experiment planning checklist (pre-run):
- Define hypothesis in a single sentence. Example: "Opportunistic slot coalescing reduces average batch overhead by >= 8% without increasing p99 latency by > 5%."
- Identify owners and reviewer: owner (runtime-team), reviewer (profiling-team lead).
- Identify target canary cohort: (e.g., internal-dev, 5% routed to experimental pool).
- Required approvals: SLO-risk review (eng-sre), data-privacy signoff (if traces contain customer content).
- Safety gates: circuit-breaker thresholds (see table).

Safety gate thresholds (example):
- p50 latency: no increase > 15%
- p95 latency: no increase > 10%
- p99 latency: no increase > 5%
- Error rate: no absolute increase > 0.05% or > 2x baseline (whichever smaller)
- Observability signal: successful trace capture >= 60% of warm requests

Validation matrix (what to measure):
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 measured at token-level and end-to-end request-level
- Throughput: tokens/sec per GPU, requests/sec per slot
- Cost: per-token GPU time, energy proxy (W*ms) if available
- Correctness: model output diffs measured with eval prompt set (small fidelity harness)
- Resource signals: GPU SM util, memory alloc/peak, NVLink error rate
- Scheduler signals: queue depth, kernel startup time, preemption occurrences
- KV cache signals: hit rate, eviction churn, warmup time

Profiling playbook (short form):
1) Baseline capture: run 30-minute stable baseline with mirrored production trace. Collect: kernel traces, CPU/GPU counters, KV-cache logs, end-to-end latencies.
2) Change capture: enable experimental flag for 5% traffic with traffic mirror. Capture same signals and tag traces with experiment id.
3) Sampling strategy: use stratified sampling across sequence-length buckets: 0-64, 65-256, 257-1024, >1024. Target at least 1000 traces per bucket.
4) Trace enrichment: attach kernel-id, scheduler-decision, kvcache-cohort, slot-id, and model-variant tags to each trace.
5) Differential analysis: compute delta per metric per bucket and generate a hypothesis test (bootstrap CI).

Benchmarking protocol (operational):
- Use the canonical workload generator (redwood-bench 1.4) with the following profile file for acceptance tests. Always run bench in shadow-mode first (no live routing).

Sample bench invocation:
redwood-bench run --profile profiles/mixed-dialogue-2026.yaml --duration 30m --target-gpus 4 --mirror=true --tag exp-crosslayer-{{experiment_id}}

Example profile snippet (profiles/mixed-dialogue-2026.yaml):
---
name: mixed-dialogue-2026
sequence-distribution:
  short: 0.55
  medium: 0.30
  long: 0.15
concurrency: 120
  # simulated clients sending variable-length turn-taking dialogues

Acceptance criteria (bench):
- Stable p95 throughput within -5% / +6% of baseline for the same cost tier
- p99 latency no worse than baseline + 4%
- No numerical exceptions or OOMs across 1,000 long traces

Kernel scheduling guidance (operational rules):
- Prefer per-tenant credit buckets for Dedicated pools where business SLAs exist.
- Use opportunistic slot coalescing only if average sequence length > 128 in target workload.
- Avoid naive preemption on kernels marked as non-interruptible. Instead, use cooperative-yield signals.
- When testing new kernel variants, run the kernel under a synthetic microbench of 1-10 token sequences and 512-token sequences to surface divergences.

Runbook: experimental canary rollout (step-by-step):
1) Preflight (local): run bench shadow run and confirm metrics stable.
2) Canary 1 (internal-dev): route 0% -> 1% -> 5% over 30-minute windows. Monitor safety gates.
3) Canary 2 (internal-paid): route 10% for 2 hours. If metrics exceed thresholds, roll back to 0% and capture traces.
4) Controlled ramp: 10% -> 25% -> 50% over 24h windows with periodic smoke checks.
5) Full rollout: schedule during low-traffic maintenance window. Keep 30% rollback capacity for first 24h.
6) Post-mortem checklist: collect diffs, update experiment registry entry, and schedule follow-up regression tests if any signal exceeded warning thresholds.

Escalation and ownership:
- First responder: experiment owner (runtime team engineer).
- SRE on-call: Pager duty group "runtime-sre".
- If p99 latency threshold breached for > 15 minutes: escalate to on-call manager + product impact channel.
- If numerical regressions observed (BLEU/Rouge/embedding drift) above tolerance: involve applied-ml team and freeze rollout.

Appendix A — sample runtime feature flag YAML (deploy-time):
---
feature_flag:
  name: opportunistic_slot_coalesce
  enabled: false  # set true in canary
  rollout:
    strategy: gradual
    steps: [1,5,10,25,50,100]
  metrics_watch: [latency_p99, error_rate, kvcache_hit_rate]

Appendix B — common troubleshooting commands:
- Collect live traces for a slot: redwood-cli traces fetch --slot-id <slot> --from <time> --to <time> --format ndjson
- Dump scheduler queue state: kubectl exec -n runtime svc/scheduler -- schedulerctl queue-dump --output json | jq '.top10'
- Revert feature flag: redwood-cli flags set opportunistic_slot_coalesce --enabled=false --reason "slo-rollback"

Appendix C — minimal regression gating tests (CI):
- integration/e2e/fidelity-check: run 200 eval prompts generating at least 95% semantic similarity according to eval-harness v2.1
- perf/pipeline/smoke: run 30s microbench verifying kernel warmup time < 15ms on target hardware
- telemetry/sampling-check: confirm trace coverage >= configured target for each seq-length bucket

Notes and rationale:
- We prioritize conservative latency SLOs during canaries; cost improvements that cause small tail regressions should be debated with product if they affect user experience.
- The matrix enforces that any experiment that touches both kernel selection and KV-cache must include both microbench and end-to-end corpus fidelity tests.

Change log:
- 2026-02-11: initial draft (Priya N.)
- 2026-03-16: added acceptance criteria and bench profile examples (Marcus P.)
- 2026-05-03: expanded profiling sampling guidance and escalation steps (Leah Z.)

Related references:
- See runtime-decision-delegation-and-observer-patterns-2026 for observer tagging conventions.
- See causal-latency-attribution playbook for detailed statistical test recipes.

If you plan to run an experiment, create an entry in the experiments registry and assign the fields: hypothesis, owner, expected-safety-gates, mirror-run-id, and bench-tag. Attach the mirroring trace dataset to the registry entry before starting the canary.

- Prefer per-tenant credit buckets for Dedicated pools where business SLAs exist.
- Use opportunistic slot coalescing only if average sequence length > 128 in target workload.
- Avoid naive preemption on kernels marked as non-interruptible. Instead, use cooperative-yield signals.
- When testing new kernel variants, run the kernel under a synthetic microbench of 1-10 token sequences and 512-token sequences to surface divergences.

Runbook: experimental canary rollout (step-by-step):
1) Preflight (local): run bench shadow run and confirm metrics stable.
2) Canary 1 (internal-dev): route 0% -> 1% -> 5% over 30-minute windows. Monitor safety gates.
3) Canary 2 (internal-paid): route 10% for 2 hours. If metrics exceed thresholds, roll back to 0% and capture traces.
4) Controlled ramp: 10% -> 25% -> 50% over 24h windows with periodic smoke checks.
5) Full rollout: schedule during low-traffic maintenance window. Keep 30% rollback capacity for first 24h.
6) Post-mortem checklist: collect diffs, update experiment registry entry, and schedule follow-up regression tests if any signal exceeded warning thresholds.

Escalation and ownership:
- First responder: experiment owner (runtime team engineer).
- SRE on-call: Pager duty group "runtime-sre".
- If p99 latency threshold breached for > 15 minutes: escalate to on-call manager + product impact channel.
- If numerical regressions observed (BLEU/Rouge/embedding drift) above tolerance: involve applied-ml team and freeze rollout.

Appendix A — sample runtime feature flag YAML (deploy-time):
---
feature_flag:
  name: opportunistic_slot_coalesce
  enabled: false  # set true in canary
  rollout:
    strategy: gradual
    steps: [1,5,10,25,50,100]
  metrics_watch: [latency_p99, error_rate, kvcache_hit_rate]

Appendix B — common troubleshooting commands:
- Collect live traces for a slot: redwood-cli traces fetch --slot-id <slot> --from <time> --to <time> --format ndjson
- Dump scheduler queue state: kubectl exec -n runtime svc/scheduler -- schedulerctl queue-dump --output json | jq '.top10'
- Revert feature flag: redwood-cli flags set opportunistic_slot_coalesce --enabled=false --reason "slo-rollback"

Appendix C — minimal regression gating tests (CI):
- integration/e2e/fidelity-check: run 200 eval prompts generating at least 95% semantic similarity according to eval-harness v2.1
- perf/pipeline/smoke: run 30s microbench verifying kernel warmup time < 15ms on target hardware
- telemetry/sampling-check: confirm trace coverage >= configured target for each seq-length bucket

Notes and rationale:
- We prioritize conservative latency SLOs during canaries; cost improvements that cause small tail regressions should be debated with product if they affect user experience.
- The matrix enforces that any experiment that touches both kernel selection and KV-cache must include both microbench and end-to-end corpus fidelity tests.

Change log:
- 2026-02-11: initial draft (Priya N.)
- 2026-03-16: added acceptance criteria and bench profile examples (Marcus P.)
- 2026-05-03: expanded profiling sampling guidance and escalation steps (Leah Z.)

Related references:
- See runtime-decision-delegation-and-observer-patterns-2026 for observer tagging conventions.
- See causal-latency-attribution playbook for detailed statistical test recipes.

If you plan to run an experiment, create an entry in the experiments registry and assign the fields: hypothesis, owner, expected-safety-gates, mirror-run-id, and bench-tag. Attach the mirroring trace dataset to the registry entry before starting the canary.
text:
Summary:
This document is a consolidated experiment matrix and validation checklist for cross-layer runtime experiments that touch kernel scheduling, KV-cache behavior, profiling instrumentation, and benchmark acceptance. It's intended for engineiners running controlled experiments that may affect SLOs and cost. Use this as the canonical checklist and runbook for planning, executing, and validating experiments that modify scheduler policies, kernel variants, or observation sampling rates.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: experiments affecting serving runtime execution path, kernel selection, batching, KV cache prefetch, and telemetry sampling.
- Audience: runtime engineers, SREs, performance researchers, release engineers.

High-level experiment categories (matrix):
1) Scheduling policy changes
  - Example: priority-driven crediting -> opportunistic slot coalescing
  - Impact areas: tail latency, fairness, kernel queue depth, eviction rate
2) Kernel variant rollouts
  - Example: fast-attention-v2 vs safe-attention-v1 (reduced memory, different numeric stability)
  - Impact: per-token CPU/GPU time, p99 latency, EPS (errors per second)
3) KV-cache behavior and prefetch tuning
  - Example: asynchronous prefetch enabled/disabled, prefetch window size
  - Impact: cold-starts, token generation latency, cache warmup time
4) Telemetry and sampling changes
  - Example: full traces -> sampled traces with adaptive enrichment
  - Impact: signal fidelity, profiling cost, root-cause analysis windows
5) Quantization/Kernel-precision changes
  - Example: fp16 -> int8 with calibration profile B
  - Impact: model quality regressions, kernel numerical edgecases

Experiment planning checklist (pre-run):
- Define hypothesis in a single sentence. Example: "Opportunistic slot coalescing reduces average batch overhead by >= 8% without increasing p99 latency by > 5%."
- Identify owners and reviewer: owner (runtime-team), reviewer (profiling-team lead).
- Identify target canary cohort: (e.g., internal-dev, 5% routed to experimental pool).
- Required approvals: SLO-risk review (eng-sre), data-privacy signoff (if traces contain customer content).
- Safety gates: circuit-breaker thresholds (see table).

Safety gate thresholds (example):
- p50 latency: no increase > 15%
- p95 latency: no increase > 10%
- p99 latency: no increase > 5%
- Error rate: no absolute increase > 0.05% or > 2x baseline (whichever smaller)
- Observability signal: successful trace capture >= 60% of warm requests

Validation matrix (what to measure):
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 measured at token-level and end-to-end request-level
- Throughput: tokens/sec per GPU, requests/sec per slot
- Cost: per-token GPU time, energy proxy (W*ms) if available
- Correctness: model output diffs measured with eval prompt set (small fidelity harness)
- Resource signals: GPU SM util, memory alloc/peak, NVLink error rate
- Scheduler signals: queue depth, kernel startup time, preemption occurrences
- KV cache signals: hit rate, eviction churn, warmup time

Profiling playbook (short form):
1) Baseline capture: run 30-minute stable baseline with mirrored production trace. Collect: kernel traces, CPU/GPU counters, KV-cache logs, end-to-end latencies.
2) Change capture: enable experimental flag for 5% traffic with traffic mirror. Capture same signals and tag traces with experiment id.
3) Sampling strategy: use stratified sampling across sequence-length buckets: 0-64, 65-256, 257-1024, >1024. Target at least 1000 traces per bucket.
4) Trace enrichment: attach kernel-id, scheduler-decision, kvcache-cohort, slot-id, and model-variant tags to each trace.
5) Differential analysis: compute delta per metric per bucket and generate a hypothesis test (bootstrap CI).

Benchmarking protocol (operational):
- Use the canonical workload generator (redwood-bench 1.4) with the following profile file for acceptance tests. Always run bench in shadow-mode first (no live routing).

Sample bench invocation:
redwood-bench run --profile profiles/mixed-dialogue-2026.yaml --duration 30m --target-gpus 4 --mirror=true --tag exp-crosslayer-{{experiment_id}}

Example profile snippet (profiles/mixed-dialogue-2026.yaml):
---
name: mixed-dialogue-2026
sequence-distribution:
  short: 0.55
  medium: 0.30
  long: 0.15
concurrency: 120
  # simulated clients sending variable-length turn-taking dialogues

Acceptance criteria (bench):
- Stable p95 throughput within -5% / +6% of baseline for the same cost tier
- p99 latency no worse than baseline + 4%
- No numerical exceptions or OOMs across 1,000 long traces

Kernel scheduling guidance (operational rules):
- Prefer per-tenant credit buckets for Dedicated pools where business SLAs exist.
- Use opportunistic slot coalescing only if average sequence length > 128 in target workload.
- Avoid naive preemption on kernels marked as non-interruptible. Instead, use cooperative-yield signals.
- When testing new kernel variants, run the kernel under a synthetic microbench of 1-10 token sequences and 512-token sequences to surface divergences.

Runbook: experimental canary rollout (step-by-step):
1) Preflight (local): run bench shadow run and confirm metrics stable.
2) Canary 1 (internal-dev): route 0% -> 1% -> 5% over 30-minute windows. Monitor safety gates.
3) Canary 2 (internal-paid): route 10% for 2 hours. If metrics exceed thresholds, roll back to 0% and capture traces.
4) Controlled ramp: 10% -> 25% -> 50% over 24h windows with periodic smoke checks.
5) Full rollout: schedule during low-traffic maintenance window. Keep 30% rollback capacity for first 24h.
6) Post-mortem checklist: collect diffs, update experiment registry entry, and schedule follow-up regression tests if any signal exceeded warning thresholds.

Escalation and ownership:
- First responder: experiment owner (runtime team engineer).
- SRE on-call: Pager duty group "runtime-sre".
- If p99 latency threshold breached for > 15 minutes: escalate to on-call manager + product impact channel.
- If numerical regressions observed (BLEU/Rouge/embedding drift) above tolerance: involve applied-ml team and freeze rollout.

Appendix A — sample runtime feature flag YAML (deploy-time):
---
feature_flag:
  name: opportunistic_slot_coalesce
  enabled: false  # set true in canary
  rollout:
    strategy: gradual
    steps: [1,5,10,25,50,100]
  metrics_watch: [latency_p99, error_rate, kvcache_hit_rate]

Appendix B — common troubleshooting commands:
- Collect live traces for a slot: redwood-cli traces fetch --slot-id <slot> --from <time> --to <time> --format ndjson
- Dump scheduler queue state: kubectl exec -n runtime svc/scheduler -- schedulerctl queue-dump --output json | jq '.top10'
- Revert feature flag: redwood-cli flags set opportunistic_slot_coalesce --enabled=false --reason "slo-rollback"

Appendix C — minimal regression gating tests (CI):
- integration/e2e/fidelity-check: run 200 eval prompts generating at least 95% semantic similarity according to eval-harness v2.1
- perf/pipeline/smoke: run 30s microbench verifying kernel warmup time < 15ms on target hardware
- telemetry/sampling-check: confirm trace coverage >= configured target for each seq-length bucket

Notes and rationale:
- We prioritize conservative latency SLOs during canaries; cost improvements that cause small tail regressions should be debated with product if they affect user experience.
- The matrix enforces that any experiment that touches both kernel selection and KV-cache must include both microbench and end-to-end corpus fidelity tests.

Change log:
- 2026-02-11: initial draft (Priya N.)
- 2026-03-16: added acceptance criteria and bench profile examples (Marcus P.)
- 2026-05-03: expanded profiling sampling guidance and escalation steps (Leah Z.)

Related references:
- See runtime-decision-delegation-and-observer-patterns-2026 for observer tagging conventions.
- See causal-latency-attribution playbook for detailed statistical test recipes.
