  latency: "0.75"
  cost: "0.20"
  isolation: "0.05"
  affinity: {pools: [hot, warm], protect_until: "2026-04-01"}

Appendix B — decision scoring example (csv):
input,weight,score,contrib
latency_priority,0.75,0.82,0.615
cost_priority,0.20,0.34,0.068
isolation_priority,0.05,0.9,0.045
final_score,, ,0.728

Appendix C — escalation contact matrix:
- Pager 1: eng-sre (primary)
- Pager 2: runtime-architecture (Priya Natarajan)
- Email: runtime-discuss@redwood.ai

Related pages and next steps:
- Add kernel hint adoption metrics to runtime-consolidation dashboard and alert if adoption < 80% across a pool.
- Prototype ML-driven fallback scorer (Q2 2026) to replace hand-tuned weighting.

If you are implementing or operating TFREA and see any unexpected behaviors, file a ticket in the runtime backlog (component: runtime-fallback) and include DecisionTrace IDs for at least 5 example requests.
text:
Overview:

This document describes the Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) used by Redwood’s serving runtime to meet multi-dimensional SLOs (latency percentiles, cost budget, and availability) while degrading predictably during capacity pressure or downstream failure modes. TFREA unifies routing policies, candidate model variants, eviction heuristics, and runtime-informed kernel scheduling hints into a single, observable control loop.

Goals:
- Maintain 99th-percentile latency SLOs per route under normal load.
- Bound hosted per-token cost within configured customer cost tiers.
- Provide deterministic, monotonic quality degradation (higher-quality -> lower-quality) with automated rollback when pressure subsides.
- Make fallback decisions explainable and traceable per request for post-incident analysis.

Scope:
- Applies to hosted and Dedicated modes; Private deployments can enable TFREA via configuration flag.
- Covers routing layer, model-variant catalog, eviction/placement heuristics, kernel scheduling hints, and observability integration.

Key concepts and components:
1) Routing Policy Engine (RPE)
- Policy primitives: latency-priority, cost-priority, isolation-priority. Each route has a profile: e.g., {latency: 0.7, cost: 0.2, isolation: 0.1}.
- Decision inputs: request metadata (route, token budget, customer plan), node telemetry (utilization, queue depth), model health (regression alerts), and global capacity signals.
- Outputs: selected pool (hot, warm, cold), model variant (full, quantized, distilled), and optional dual-write fallback (emit to both selected and fallback model asynchronously for A/B quality logging).

2) Model Variant Catalog and Contracts
- Each canonical model version registers 1..N variants with contract metadata:
  - quality_score (0.0-1.0) derived from eval harness
  - compute_cost_factor (relative tokens/second)
  - warmup_time_seconds
  - compatible_kernels (list of kernels / tensor layouts)
- Example registration YAML snippet: 
  id: llama-3-20b:v2026-02
  variants:
    - id: llama-3-20b_fp16
      quality_score: "0.98"
      cost_factor: "1.0"
      warmup: "18"
      kernels: [matmul-fused, alpa-attn-optimized]
    - id: llama-3-20b_8bitq
      quality_score: "0.92"
      cost_factor: "0.55"
      warmup: "6"
      kernels: [quant-8q-conv]

3) Tiered Pools (hot / warm / cold)
- Hot: low-latency nodes, prewarmed KV caches, continuous batching source; used for latency-priority traffic. SLO target: p99 < 120ms for short requests (<=128 tokens).
- Warm: on-demand nodes with partial KV warmup and batched scheduling; SLO target: p99 < 260ms.
- Cold: preemptible or burst capacity; best-effort latency with strong cost savings. SLO target: p99 < 1s (best-effort).

4) Eviction and Placement Heuristics
- Eviction signals: node memory pressure, KV cold-start ratio > 0.4, kernel queue growth rate > 20% over 30s, and per-tenant cost throttle events.
- Heuristic ordering when evicting: least-recently-used variant replicas weighted by (quality_score * route_priority) to avoid evicting high-quality routes.
- Soft-eviction step: move from hot->warm; Hard-eviction: terminate replica (cold) and optionally nominate a lower-cost variant.

5) Kernel Scheduling Hints and Interaction
- RPE emits kernel hints: preferred_kernel, concurrency_target, slice_budget. These are advisory fields consumed by kernel-and-scheduling layer to optimize batch slicing and preemption.
- Example hint: {preferred_kernel: "matmul-fused", concurrency_target: "6", slice_budget_ms: "40"}.

6) Observability and Decision Tracing
- Every routing decision logs a DecisionTrace ID attached to request spans. Trace contains inputs, scoring vector, selected_variant, and fallback_candidates. Stored in the decision-trace index for 30 days by default.
- Key dashboards: Per-route fallback rate, per-variant quality delta (A/B logs), pool occupancy and eviction rate, kernel hint adoption rate.

SLOs and thresholds (operative values):
- Route-level p50/p95/p99 SLOs are configured per route. Default intent for conversational routes: p50 < 30ms, p95 < 95ms, p99 < 250ms.
- Cost guardrails: per-customer monthly token-cost target with soft watermarks at 50% and 80% where TFREA may progressively bias cost-priority.
- Fallback budget: No route should experience fallback > 10% of requests sustained over 5 minutes without triggering operator notification.

Operational procedures and runbooks:
1) Gradual rollout checklist (owner: runtime-release)
1. Create canary pool with 5% traffic weight.
2. Enable decision-trace sampling at 5% for canary.
3. Monitor: fallback-rate, p99 latency, kernel-queue-depth for 15 minutes.
4. If fallback_rate > 1% or p99 regresses > 15% over baseline, abort and roll back.
5. If stable, ramp 5% every 10 minutes to target.

2) Incident triage playbook: Unexpected fallback surge
- Pager triggers at fallback_rate > 10% sustained for 2 minutes.
- Triage steps:
  1. Query decision-trace for last 5 minutes (filter by route). CLI: redwoodctl traces query --route <r> --since 5m --filter fallback=true
  2. Check pool telemetry: node_util, kv_hit_rate, kernel_queue_growth.
  3. If KV cold-start > 50%: trigger warmup job for top-5 variants: redwoodctl runtime warmup --variants <ids> --target-pool hot
  4. If node OOMs or watchdog hits: cordon nodes and spin up replacement nodes (see gpu-fleet-and-capacity runbook).
  5. Temporarily set route policy to latency-priority and pin to hot pool for critical tenants while investigating.

3) Performance regression investigation (owner: runtime-profiling)
- Collect sample logs and kernel traces with sampler strategy: 1:1000 baseline, 1:50 on regression window.
- Use profiling commands: profiler collect --trace-id <trace> --duration 30s --output s3://runtime-profiles/<id>.tar.gz
- Correlate with batch-slice histograms and kernel latency percentiles.

Benchmarking and validation suite:
- The TFREA validation harness is a sub-suite in the performance suite: includes mixed-workload load generator, KV-warmup simulator, and variant quality regression tests.
- Key scenarios:
  - Steady-state with mixed short/long sequences (60/30/10 split by request length).
  - Rapid burst (2x sustained) with concurrent model-variant promotion/demotion.
  - Engineered wholesale kernel regression: inject a slow kernel variant to ensure sentinel metrics and fallbacks work.
- Release gate: TFREA must pass canary e2e smoke and two automated burst simulations without p99 SLO breaches before canary ramp.

Release notes summary (2026-02-15):
- Introduced Tiered Fallback Routing and Eviction Architecture (TFREA) to reduce tail latency and control cost during capacity pressure.
- Added DecisionTrace indexing and trace-based runbook utilities.
- New model-variant contract fields (quality_score, warmup_time_seconds) and kernel hint API.

Known issues and workarounds (as of 2026-03-02):
- Issue: Under certain mixed-tenant workloads, eviction ranking can bias too heavily toward routes with frequent short requests, causing long-tail quality regressions for background batch routes.
  - Workaround: Set explicit route affinity in route config to protect background batch routes, e.g., affinity: {protect_until: "2026-06-01"}.
- Issue: Kernel hint adoption sometimes lags on heterogeneous pools where the kernel mapper is updating; mismatch can cause suboptimal batch slicing.
  - Workaround: Restart kernel-mapper after variant promotion: systemctl restart kernel-mapper (one node at a time) and allow 60s for re-registration.

Implementation notes and APIs:
- New API: /v1/runtime/route-evaluate (internal) returns DecisionTrace and selected_variant. Schema: {route, inputs, scoring, selected, fallbacks, hints}.
- redwoodctl examples:
  - Query fallback stats: redwoodctl runtime stats fallback --route <route> --window 10m
  - Force promote variant to hot pool: redwoodctl runtime promote --variant <id> --pool hot --reason "emergency-fix"

Ownership and SLAs:
- Owners: runtime-architecture (Priya Natarajan), runtime-scheduling (Marcus Liu), runtime-profiling (Zara Ortega).
- Support: eng-sre oncall for 24/7 pager coverage.
- Documentation and training: rollout workshops scheduled for 2026-03-10 and 2026-03-17.

Appendix A — route policy example (YAML):
route: "conversations/v1/generate"
policy:
  latency: "0.75"
  cost: "0.20"
  isolation: "0.05"
  affinity: {pools: [hot, warm], protect_until: "2026-04-01"}

Appendix B — decision scoring example (csv):
input,weight,score,contrib
latency_priority,0.75,0.82,0.615
cost_priority,0.20,0.34,0.068
isolation_priority,0.05,0.9,0.045
final_score,, ,0.728

Appendix C — escalation contact matrix:
- Pager 1: eng-sre (primary)
- Pager 2: runtime-architecture (Priya Natarajan)
- Email: runtime-discuss@redwood.ai

Related pages and next steps:
- Add kernel hint adoption metrics to runtime-consolidation dashboard and alert if adoption < 80% across a pool.
- Prototype ML-driven fallback scorer (Q2 2026) to replace hand-tuned weighting.

If you are implementing or operating TFREA and see any unexpected behaviors, file a ticket in the runtime backlog (component: runtime-fallback) and include DecisionTrace IDs for at least 5 example requests.

source.title: Graceful Restart Latency Contracts and Snapshot Synthesis
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview

This document defines latency contracts (SLOs/SLO targets) and the operational protocol for graceful restarts of runtime workers, kernel modules, and model shards. It also specifies the snapshot synthesis format we use for resumable execution: what state is captured, how it is validated, and how resume/rollback works. The goal is to provide a concrete, testable, and observable procedure that keeps customer-facing latency and correctness within predictable bounds during maintenance, upgrades, and transient failures.

Audience

- Runtime engineers implementing restart semantics and kernel handoff.
- SRE and oncall responsible for incident response when restarts impact SLOs.
- Release engineers and QA writing canary tests and fidelity benchmarks.

Definitions

- graceful restart: a coordinated shutdown and resume of a worker (or kernel) that preserves sufficient execution state so in-flight requests can be resumed or rerouted without correctness regressions.
- cold restart: full process restart without preserved execution snapshots; requires warmup/synthetic traffic to reach steady-state.
- snapshot: a serialized representation of runtime-required state for resumability (KV cache segments, session token pointers, decoder cache offsets, plugin state checksums).
- resume window: the time window after restart during which resumed requests must meet latency/fidelity guarantees.

Latency Contracts (SLOs)

These contracts are the SLA-facing expectations that the runtime operator must meet under planned maintenance (rolling upgrades) and common unplanned restarts (OOMs, node reboots, kernel panics). Targets are expressed as 30-day rolling metrics and validated through synthetic traffic during canaries.

Primary SLOs

- Controlled rolling restart (per-pool, single-tenant): resumed-request p95 <= 120 ms, p99 <= 400 ms within 30s of restart completion.
- Kernel module swap (same-node live-replace): resumed-request p95 <= 60 ms, p99 <= 200 ms within 10s.
- Cold restart recovery (node-rejoin): median request latency to baseline <= 2x baseline after warmup period of 120s; p99 should not exceed 4x baseline.
- Snapshot application time: time to apply a snapshot and be ready for resume <= 250 ms for per-session snapshots, <= 2s for full shard snapshots.

Measuring and Attribution

- Signals used: request timeline (trace start -> resume/serve), kernel handoff events, snapshot-save and snapshot-apply events, kvcache warmness score, model load digest.
- Primary metric names: runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms (histogram), runtime.resume.success_rate (ratio), runtime.snapshot.serialize_ms, runtime.snapshot.size_bytes.
- Attribution: resumed vs. newly routed requests are tagged with resume=true/false. SLOs are measured only on resumed=true requests for restart contracts and only on resumed=false requests for cold-start recovery windows.

Snapshot Synthesis: What We Capture

Snapshots must be compact, deterministic, and verifiable. They are intended to let a worker resume decoding/generation for an in-flight request or restore a warm execution state for a session cohort. The synthesis procedure produces a snapshot manifest and a binary blob.

Essential snapshot components (per-entity):

- header: snapshot_version, producer_node_id, sequence_id, timestamp (ISO8601).
- kvcache segments: list of (prefix_hash, ttl_ms, compressed_bytes_base64, checksum_sha256).
- decoder cache: token_index_offset, kv_positions, compressed_kv_blob (lz4), kernel_hint_id.
- active invocation metadata: request_id, model_id, config_hash (quantization/profile), prompt_prefix_fingerprint.
- plugin state checksums: list of plugin_name -> checksum, and optional plugin blob pointer.

Format and versioning

- snapshot manifest is JSON (text) with fixed keys; blobs are base64 strings or object-store pointers.
- versioning: snapshot_version follows semver-like format v<major>.<minor>.<build>. Consumers must reject unknown major versions.

Consistency and Safety Checks

Before accepting a snapshot to resume, the worker applies these gates in order:

1) model_id and config_hash must match the local loaded model. If mismatch, reject (fail-open routing to fallback model).
2) checksum verification for all blobs. Any mismatch -> reject.
3) ttl_ms on kvcache segments must not be expired; expired segments are pruned.

If any gate fails during a planned maintenance canary, the runtime must route the in-flight request to a compatible fallback variant and mark the snapshot as incompatible in telemetry.

Operational Protocols

Planned rolling upgrade checklist (per-pool):

1) Mark pool as draining in control plane for 30s.
2) For each worker being rotated: capture snapshot for active invocations and attempt to handoff to hot neighbor using snapshot RPC.
3) Wait for runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms p95 < 120 ms for 5 consecutive seconds on the neighborhood (canary sample).
4) If passes, decommission old worker; if fails, rollback routing and escalate to oncall.

Emergency restart runbook (short):

1) Detect restart cause and capture node-level signals: OOM, kernel-panic, watchdog.
2) If snapshot exists and passes safety checks, apply snapshot and mark resumed=true for requests attached to snapshot.
3) If snapshot rejected or unavailable, use fallback routing to sibling pool and trigger warmup traffic with weight 0.1 -> 1 over 60s.
4) Post-incident: run snapshot validation test suite (see Testing and Canary section) and file incident with execution fingerprint.

Canary and Synthetic Tests

We validate restart contracts using two classes of synthetic tests executed in CI and production canary environments:

- per-invocation resume test: inject 1–3 active invocations, force a swap of kernel module and assert runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms p95/p99.
- shard snapshot roundtrip: serialize a full shard snapshot, write to object store, read back on a separate node, apply, and run e2e generation verification (exact-match on deterministic seeds).

Testing matrix (example):

| Test type | Snapshot size | Assertion | Run cadence | Owner |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| per-invocation resume | small (<=128KB) | p95<=120ms, success_rate>=99% | CI + nightly canary | runtime-team |
| kernel swap live-replace | n/a | p95<=60ms across node | weekly canary | kernel-team |
| shard roundtrip | large (<=50MB) | deterministic output match | nightly | infra-team |

Implementation notes and sample commands

- Capture snapshot (CLI):

  curl -X POST https://control-plane/_internal/snapshot/capture 
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' 
    -d '{"worker_id":"worker-42","mode":"per-invocation"}'

  Response: {"snapshot_manifest_url":"s3://redwood-snapshots/poolA/worker-42/snap-v1.2.3-1234.json"}

- Apply snapshot (worker):

  rw-runtime apply-snapshot --manifest s3://.../snap.json --strict-checks=true

YAML example: runtime resume gating policy

  resume_policy:
    allow_if:
      - model_match: true
      - checksum_ok: true
      - kvcache_warmness_threshold: 0.6
    fallback_strategy: route_to_fallback_pool
    apply_timeout_ms: 250

Observability and Dashboards

- Dashboard panels to create/update: Resume latency heatmap (by pool, model), Snapshot success rate (by node), Snapshot serialization size histogram, Resume/error timeline waterfall.
- Alerts: snapshot.apply_failure_rate > 1% for 5m; resume.apply_latency.p99 > 500ms for 3m -> page oncall.

Ownership and Roles

- Runtime team: implement snapshot APIs, maintain resume metrics and canary infra.
- Kernel team: ensure live-replace path for kernel modules and provide kernel_hint_id semantics.
- SRE: own alerting thresholds, incident runbooks, and warmup traffic orchestration.

Release and Rollout Guidance

- Feature gate: snapshot major version rollouts must be staged behind a feature flag.
- Canary plan: enable on 5% of traffic in poolA for 24h, monitor resume.apply_latency_ms p95/p99 and snapshot.success_rate.

Appendix: Troubleshooting checklist (fast reference)

1) resume.apply_latency samples high: check snapshot.apply_latency_ms, worker CPU contention, and kvcache hit rates.
2) snapshot checksums failing: verify object-store replication and manifest integrity (run crc32 on blob).
3) incompatible model/config: confirm model version pinning and rollout policy, escalate to release engineering if rollout unintended.

References

- See runbooks: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-deployment-circuit-breakers-and-sla-fallback-playbook-2026
- See observability contract: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/team-wiki/runtime-observability-data-model-and-tracebook-2025

Contact

For questions or extensions to this contract, open an RFC in the runtime-team backlog and cc runtime@redwood.internal.

- Signals used: request timeline (trace start -> resume/serve), kernel handoff events, snapshot-save and snapshot-apply events, kvcache warmness score, model load digest.
- Primary metric names: runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms (histogram), runtime.resume.success_rate (ratio), runtime.snapshot.serialize_ms, runtime.snapshot.size_bytes.
- Attribution: resumed vs. newly routed requests are tagged with resume=true/false. SLOs are measured only on resumed=true requests for restart contracts and only on resumed=false requests for cold-start recovery windows.

Snapshot Synthesis: What We Capture

Snapshots must be compact, deterministic, and verifiable. They are intended to let a worker resume decoding/generation for an in-flight request or restore a warm execution state for a session cohort. The synthesis procedure produces a snapshot manifest and a binary blob.

Essential snapshot components (per-entity):

- header: snapshot_version, producer_node_id, sequence_id, timestamp (ISO8601).
- kvcache segments: list of (prefix_hash, ttl_ms, compressed_bytes_base64, checksum_sha256).
- decoder cache: token_index_offset, kv_positions, compressed_kv_blob (lz4), kernel_hint_id.
- active invocation metadata: request_id, model_id, config_hash (quantization/profile), prompt_prefix_fingerprint.
- plugin state checksums: list of plugin_name -> checksum, and optional plugin blob pointer.

Format and versioning

- snapshot manifest is JSON (text) with fixed keys; blobs are base64 strings or object-store pointers.
- versioning: snapshot_version follows semver-like format v<major>.<minor>.<build>. Consumers must reject unknown major versions.

Consistency and Safety Checks

Before accepting a snapshot to resume, the worker applies these gates in order:

1) model_id and config_hash must match the local loaded model. If mismatch, reject (fail-open routing to fallback model).
2) checksum verification for all blobs. Any mismatch -> reject.
3) ttl_ms on kvcache segments must not be expired; expired segments are pruned.

If any gate fails during a planned maintenance canary, the runtime must route the in-flight request to a compatible fallback variant and mark the snapshot as incompatible in telemetry.

Operational Protocols

Planned rolling upgrade checklist (per-pool):

1) Mark pool as draining in control plane for 30s.
2) For each worker being rotated: capture snapshot for active invocations and attempt to handoff to hot neighbor using snapshot RPC.
3) Wait for runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms p95 < 120 ms for 5 consecutive seconds on the neighborhood (canary sample).
4) If passes, decommission old worker; if fails, rollback routing and escalate to oncall.

Emergency restart runbook (short):

1) Detect restart cause and capture node-level signals: OOM, kernel-panic, watchdog.
2) If snapshot exists and passes safety checks, apply snapshot and mark resumed=true for requests attached to snapshot.
3) If snapshot rejected or unavailable, use fallback routing to sibling pool and trigger warmup traffic with weight 0.1 -> 1 over 60s.
4) Post-incident: run snapshot validation test suite (see Testing and Canary section) and file incident with execution fingerprint.

Canary and Synthetic Tests

We validate restart contracts using two classes of synthetic tests executed in CI and production canary environments:

- per-invocation resume test: inject 1–3 active invocations, force a swap of kernel module and assert runtime.resume.apply_latency_ms p95/p99.
- shard snapshot roundtrip: serialize a full shard snapshot, write to object store, read back on a separate node, apply, and run e2e generation verification (exact-match on deterministic seeds).

Testing matrix (example):

| Test type | Snapshot size | Assertion | Run cadence | Owner |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| per-invocation resume | small (<=128KB) | p95<=120ms, success_rate>=99% | CI + nightly canary | runtime-team |
| kernel swap live-replace | n/a | p95<=60ms across node | weekly canary | kernel-team |
| shard roundtrip | large (<=50MB) | deterministic output match | nightly | infra-team |

Implementation notes and sample commands

- Capture snapshot (CLI):

  curl -X POST https://control-plane/_internal/snapshot/capture 
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' 
    -d '{"worker_id":"worker-42","mode":"per-invocation"}'

  Response: {"snapshot_manifest_url":"s3://redwood-snapshots/poolA/worker-42/snap-v1.2.3-1234.json"}

- Apply snapshot (worker):

  rw-runtime apply-snapshot --manifest s3://.../snap.json --strict-checks=true

YAML example: runtime resume gating policy

  resume_policy:
    allow_if:
      - model_match: true
      - checksum_ok: true
      - kvcache_warmness_threshold: 0.6
    fallback_strategy: route_to_fallback_pool
    apply_timeout_ms: 250

Observability and Dashboards

- Dashboard panels to create/update: Resume latency heatmap (by pool, model), Snapshot success rate (by node), Snapshot serialization size histogram, Resume/error timeline waterfall.
- Alerts: snapshot.apply_failure_rate > 1% for 5m; resume.apply_latency.p99 > 500ms for 3m -> page oncall.

Ownership and Roles

- Runtime team: implement snapshot APIs, maintain resume metrics and canary infra.
- Kernel team: ensure live-replace path for kernel modules and provide kernel_hint_id semantics.
- SRE: own alerting thresholds, incident runbooks, and warmup traffic orchestration.

Release and Rollout Guidance

- Feature gate: snapshot major version rollouts must be staged behind a feature flag.
- Canary plan: enable on 5% of traffic in poolA for 24h, monitor resume.apply_latency_ms p95/p99 and snapshot.success_rate.

Appendix: Troubleshooting checklist (fast reference)

1) resume.apply_latency samples high: check snapshot.apply_latency_ms, worker CPU contention, and kvcache hit rates.
2) snapshot checksums failing: verify object-store replication and manifest integrity (run crc32 on blob).
3) incompatible model/config: confirm model version pinning and rollout policy, escalate to release engineering if rollout unintended.

References

- See runbooks: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-deployment-circuit-breakers-and-sla-fallback-playbook-2026
- See observability contract: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/team-wiki/runtime-observability-data-model-and-tracebook-2025

Contact

For questions or extensions to this contract, open an RFC in the runtime-team backlog and cc runtime@redwood.internal.

source.title: Runtime safety: telemetry contracts and chaos-embargo plan
source.author: Maya Chen
source.content:
Summary:
This document defines a compact, operational telemetry contract for the serving runtime and the engineering policy we call the "chaos embargo"—time windows and gating rules that restrict disruptive experiments (kernel rollouts, hardware rebalancing, large-scale quantization flips, and canary expansion) unless safety conditions are met. The goal is to reduce blast radius during peak business hours and give SRE/Owners deterministic signal windows for fast assessment.

1) Why this exists
- Serving runtime changes interact with low-level GPU kernels and KV caching; small regressions can cause correlated tail-latency spikes and cross-tenant noise.
- We must guarantee experiments only proceed when essential health signals are stable, and we must have a fast path to freeze/rollback if we observe early regressions.

2) Scope and applicability
- Applies to all changes that can affect runtime execution fidelity, scheduling, or memory (examples: kernel binary changes, scheduler policy updates, eviction heuristics, major quantization profile flips, large-scale model swaps).
- Does not apply to pure documentation or client-side SDK changes.

3) Telemetry contract (signals & canonical definitions)
- Source: Prometheus metrics, per-request traces, aggregated token histograms, runtime kernel telemetry, and error logs.
- Canonical signals (names used in alerts/queries):
  - runtime_request_p95_latency_ms (histogram p95; measured at ingress service boundary)
  - runtime_request_p99_latency_ms
  - kernel_execution_tail_ratio (fraction of kernel calls > 2x median kernel latency)
  - kvcache_miss_rate (per-shard)
  - kvcache_warmup_time_seconds (median warmup per session)
  - gpu_memory_pressure_pct (95th percentile across nodes)
  - grpc_connection_error_rate (errors / minute per node)
  - fidelity_degradation_score (synthetic eval online regression score, see section 6)
  - tenant_error_spike_ratio (ratio of tenant error rate to baseline over last 15m)

- Contract guarantees:
  - All experiments must publish a 48h baseline for the signals above before rollout starts. Baseline must include at least one high-traffic weekday peak if available.
  - Signal tags: every metric must be tagged with release_id, kernel_variant, region, and cohort.

4) Safety thresholds (gating rules for experiments)
- Absolute thresholds (fail immediately):
  - runtime_request_p99_latency_ms > 1200ms (global) OR > 2x baseline p99 for the cohort -> immediate abort/rollback.
  - kernel_execution_tail_ratio increases by > 3x relative to baseline -> pause rollout and investigate.
  - gpu_memory_pressure_pct > 93% sustained for 5 minutes -> reject new allocations and drain optional cohorts.
  - tenant_error_spike_ratio > 4 -> trigger page and throttle new canary traffic.

- Degradation windows (require human review if crossed):
  - runtime_request_p95_latency_ms increases by 20-40% for an hour -> require PM + SRE review before further rollout.
  - fidelity_degradation_score drop >= 5 points on production eval set -> require product signoff prior to further expansion.

5) Chaos embargo policy (calendar and exception flow)
- Default embargo windows (no disruptive experiments allowed):
  - Monday–Friday 07:00–20:00 UTC (company peak coverage hours)
  - Any documented customer QBR windows or enterprise go-live days (tagged in the calendar)
- Allowed during embargo: monitoring-only tests, small non-disruptive config changes (no kernel swaps, no canary expansion > 2% traffic).
- Exceptions: emergency fixes or mitigations may be executed inside embargo with the following steps:
  1. Pager duty owner (SRE lead) triages and documents the risk.
  2. Run the preflight signal checklist (section 7) and gain approval from on-call and release owner.
  3. If approval granted, a narrow mitigation can be executed with real-time monitoring and a rollback playbook on standby.

6) Fidelity evaluation (online synthetic checks)
- Short synthetic eval harness runs on canary cohort traffic to compute fidelity_degradation_score. The harness compares a small set of golden prompts and checks:
  - Semantic divergence (embedding cosine distance threshold)
  - Token-level generation anomalies (sudden repetition or cutoffs)
  - Safety classification regressions (NLP safety binary classifier rate)
- Scoring is normalized to a 0–100 scale with historical baselining. A drop of 2–5 points is a yellow flag; >5 points is a hard stop for canary expansion.

7) Preflight checklist (must be completed before non-trivial rollout)
- Automated checks (pass/fail):
  - Baseline metrics uploaded for 48h and validated by the release pipeline.
  - Smoke test suite green on canary (latency p95 within 15% of baseline; error rate <= 2x baseline).
  - KVCache checksum/consistency check for canary shards.
  - Canary synthetic fidelity harness run with result >= 95% of baseline score.
- Manual approvals (must be recorded in rollout ticket):
  - Release owner (engineer) confirms risk and rollback plan.
  - On-call SRE approves time window and monitoring readiness.
  - PM or product owner acknowledges potential user-visible impact for fidelity-sensitive releases.

8) Operational procedures and runbook snippets
- Immediate abort (scripted):
  1. Run the control API to set canary weight to 0 for release_id = <id>. Example curl (replace tokens):
     curl -X POST "https://controlplane.internal/release/adjust" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" 
     -d '{"release_id":"RELEASE-1234","canary_weight":0}'
  2. Trigger automatic rollback job: POST /release/rollback with same auth header.
  3. Open a critical incident with tag runtime-regression and notify product and infra channels.

- Emergency GPU memory pressure mitigation:
  1. Set admission controller to "restrict" for affected pool: kubectl -n runtime patch configmap admission --patch '{"data":{"mode":"restrict"}}'
  2. Evict non-critical cohorts via the operator: runtimectl evict --pool=persistent --cohort=best-effort
  3. If pressure persists, initiate cross-region spill policy: runtimectl spill --target-region=us-west-2

9) Example Prometheus rule (text form):
- ALERT RuntimeP99Spike
  IF increase(runtime_request_p99_latency_ms[5m]) > (2 * baseline_p99_ratio)
  FOR 2m
  LABELS {severity="page", team="runtime"}
  ANNOTATIONS {summary="Runtime p99 latency doubled for cohort {{ $labels.cohort }}"}

10) Dashboard and runbook links (actionable views)
- Canary health dashboard: /dashboards/runtime/canary-health (contains p50/p95/p99, kernel tail heatmap, synthetic fidelity, kvcache per-shard heatmap)
- Rollout inspector (time-travel query): /dashboards/runtime/rollout-inspector?release_id={{release_id}}
- Incident runbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-incident-orchestration-and-capacity-decision-matrix-2026

11) Ownership, cadence, and review
- Owners:
  - Telemetry contract steward: Priya Natarajan (observability team)
  - Chaos embargo steward: Diego Alvarez (platform SRE)
  - Fidelity harness owner: Samir Patel (applied ML)
- Cadence: Monthly safety review meeting to audit baselines, embargo calendar exceptions, and recent near-misses (chair rotates between owners).

12) Onboarding notes for experimenters
- Minimum artifact checklist for an experiment ticket:
  - Baseline data export (48h) attached as CSV.
  - Short summary of expected impact and rollback steps.
  - Preflight results attached (smoke tests and synthetic harness).
- Slack template for announcing a canary kick-off in #infra-experiments and #sre-oncall (copy into ticket).

13) Postmortem and learning loop
- Every aborted or failed rollout under this policy must produce a short postmortem within 72 hours, using the template: summary, what happened, detection signals, root cause, actions (owner + due date). Attach telemetry snapshots and kernel traces to the postmortem.

Appendix A — Suggested PromQL queries (text):
- p99 over 15m: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_request_latency_bucket[15m])) by (le, cohort))
- kernel tail ratio: sum(rate(kernel_call_duration_seconds_bucket{le=">0.02"}[5m])) / sum(rate(kernel_call_duration_seconds_count[5m]))

Appendix B — Synthetic harness golden prompt examples (text):
- Prompt 1 (instruction): "Summarize the following paragraph in one sentence: ..."
- Prompt 2 (dialogue): short multi-turn assistant/user interaction used to detect unexpected repetition or cutoff.

If there are questions or suggested edits, ping the telemetry steward or open a PR against this page.

- Cadence: Monthly safety review meeting to audit baselines, embargo calendar exceptions, and recent near-misses (chair rotates between owners).

12) Onboarding notes for experimenters
- Minimum artifact checklist for an experiment ticket:
  - Baseline data export (48h) attached as CSV.
  - Short summary of expected impact and rollback steps.
  - Preflight results attached (smoke tests and synthetic harness).
- Slack template for announcing a canary kick-off in #infra-experiments and #sre-oncall (copy into ticket).

13) Postmortem and learning loop
- Every aborted or failed rollout under this policy must produce a short postmortem within 72 hours, using the template: summary, what happened, detection signals, root cause, actions (owner + due date). Attach telemetry snapshots and kernel traces to the postmortem.

Appendix A — Suggested PromQL queries (text):
- p99 over 15m: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_request_latency_bucket[15m])) by (le, cohort))
- kernel tail ratio: sum(rate(kernel_call_duration_seconds_bucket{le=">0.02"}[5m])) / sum(rate(kernel_call_duration_seconds_count[5m]))

Appendix B — Synthetic harness golden prompt examples (text):
- Prompt 1 (instruction): "Summarize the following paragraph in one sentence: ..."
- Prompt 2 (dialogue): short multi-turn assistant/user interaction used to detect unexpected repetition or cutoff.

If there are questions or suggested edits, ping the telemetry steward or open a PR against this page.

source.title: Asynchronous Kernel Handoff Simulation and Safety Playbook
source.author: Sofia Patel
source.content:
Summary

This playbook describes the simulation-driven, safety-first approach for introducing and validating asynchronous kernel handoffs in the runtime scheduler. It complements the scheduler contract and kernel handoff design docs by providing step-by-step simulation recipes, invariant checks, telemetry baselines, and an incident recovery checklist for live rollouts.

Intended audience
- Runtime engineers implementing scheduler/handoff changes
- SREs validating rollouts and runbooks
- Performance engineers and profiling owners

Scope and definitions
- Asynchronous kernel handoff: transferring control of a kernel or execution slot from one worker / kernel instance to another without blocking incoming requests synchronously; may include prefetching of KV cache and lazy state reconciliation.
- Simulation harness: an isolated environment that replays production-like traces and injects failure modes deterministically.
- Safety invariants: conditions that must hold before, during, and after handoffs (listed below).

Goals
1. Validate correctness: ensure no token duplication, KV cache divergence, or session corruption occurs across handoffs.
2. Preserve SLOs: keep p50 and p99 latency within the rollback guard rails during canonical handoff scenarios.
3. Enable deterministic root cause: simulation traces must reproduce handoff failures with >=95% fidelity to production traces.
4. Provide a concise checklist for safe rollout and emergency rollback.

Safety invariants (explicit)
- Invariant A: No request may be acknowledged to the client more than once.
- Invariant B: KV cache consistency: for any session id, post-handoff output must be deterministic given the same prompt and pre-handoff KV state.
- Invariant C: Memory safety: no kernel may hold references to evicted memory regions after handoff.
- Invariant D: Backpressure preservation: handoff must not exceed a 10% transient increase in queuing for any request class.

Simulation harness overview
- Components:
  - Trace replayer: injects request timeline events (arrival times, priorities, session ids, token budgets).
  - Fault injector: simulates partial failures (GPU preemption signals, process SIGTERM, nvlink transient errors, slow KV prefetch).
  - Kernel proxy: mediates handoff by providing deterministic serialization/deserialization of kernel runtime state.
  - Metrics collector: exports the same telemetry schema we use in staging (request_timeline, kernel_events, kvcache_diff_ratio, handoff_duration_ms).

Quick start (local staging)
1. Provision the harness cluster: ./infra/scripts/provision-sim-cluster.sh --type=staging-small 
2. Load trace corpus: ./tools/trace_importer --source s3://redwood-prod-traces/2025-11 --filter "route:chat, seq_len>64, entropy>0.2"
3. Start trace replayer: ./sim/replayer --traces ./corpus --concurrency 200 --compress-traffic
4. Start fault injector (dry-run): ./sim/fault_injector --profile handoff-basic --mode dry
5. Run baseline: ./sim/run_experiment --scenario baseline --duration 10m --collect prometheus,otel
6. Run handoff scenario: ./sim/run_experiment --scenario async-handoff --handoff-window 200ms --faults "gpu-preempt:50ms"

Suggested simulation parameter table
| parameter | example | purpose | acceptance threshold |
|---|---:|---|---:|
| concurrency | 200 | concurrent in-flight requests to simulate mixed traffic | p99 latency <= baseline * 1.10 |
| handoff-window | 200ms | allowed window where both origin and target may race | no token duplication, kvcache_diff_ratio < 0.001 |
| fault-injection-rate | 5% | fraction of handoffs where a fault is injected | recovery completes or rollback triggers 100% |
| replay-fidelity | 0.95 | percentage match of timeline events to production trace | >= 0.90 for acceptance |

Key telemetry and alerts to monitor during simulation
- Metrics to chart live:
  - handoff_attempts_total (by scenario, kernel_variant)
  - handoff_success_total / handoff_failure_total
  - handoff_duration_ms (p50/p90/p99)
  - kvcache_diff_ratio (per-session rolling 1m)
  - duplicate_response_detected_total
  - request_retries_total (automated retry policy triggered)
  - downstream_slo_violation_count (per-service)
- Alerts to configure for canary/staging:
  - P1: duplicate_response_detected_total > 0 in 5m window -> stop rollout
  - P1: handoff_failure_total / handoff_attempts_total > 0.01 over 10m -> investigate
  - P2: p99 end-to-end latency > baseline p99 * 1.2 for 5m -> throttling rule

Acceptance criteria for simulation runs
- Functional: duplicate_response_detected_total == 0 and kvcache_diff_ratio < 0.001 across all traces.
- Performance: p99 latency <= baseline p99 * 1.10 and CPU/GPU utilization delta <= 15% compared to baseline.
- Observability: full trace sampling with trace-id correlation for at least 80% of simulated handoffs.

Experiment scenarios (examples)
1. Graceful prefetch handoff: origin prefetches KV to target before releasing slot (no faults).
2. Interrupted migration: origin receives SIGTERM during prefetch and target must reconcile partial KV state.
3. Transient interconnect loss: nvlink jitter causes target to receive delayed KV chunks; simulate reordering.
4. Priority inversion under load: high-priority requests arrive during handoff causing preemption to requeue.

Step-by-step simulation validation checklist
1. Start baseline run and capture baseline snapshots for 10m.
2. Run scenario with handoff enabled and no faults. Validate functional invariants, then capture telemetry.
3. Introduce single-fault injections and assert invariants still hold or run compensating recovery.
4. Scale fault-injection to 5% and validate rollback triggers and operator automation.
5. Run full mixed-workload run and validate acceptance criteria.
6. Produce a short experiment report and attach trace bundles for reviewers.

Runtime-safe rollout procedure (canary -> ramp)
1. Canary: enable async handoff for 1% of traffic routed to a dedicated pool for 2 hours.
2. Verify: run the simulation acceptance suite against canary traces and monitor P1 alerts for 30m.
3. Ramp: 1% -> 5% -> 15% -> 50% over successive deployments, each step with a 2h monitoring window and automatic rollback on P1 alerts.
4. Full rollout guard: require sign-off from owner (runtime scheduler) and observability owner before >50%.

Emergency rollback checklist
1. Trigger: P1 alert firing (duplicate_response_detected_total>0 or handoff_failure ratio > threshold).
2. Actions:
   a. Flip feature flag: infra/flags set async_handoff=false for affected pool.
   b. Drain: start draining canary pool (no new handoff attempts) using /ops/drain-pool --pool <id> --mode gentle
   c. Rehydrate: ensure in-flight requests are allowed to complete under origin kernels.
   d. Postmortem: collect handoff trace bundles, kernel logs, and the fault injector script used.
3. Communicate: status-page update and paging to runtime-oncall channel (#runtime-oncall).

Runbook: triage recipe for a single handoff failure
1. Identify trace-id(s) from handoff_failure events. 2. Pull the correlated kernel_event timeline and kvcache snapshots. 3. Re-run the small-trace repro in the harness with identical fault injection. 4. If repro succeeds locally, create a hotfix branch to add additional guards (e.g., stricter prefetchcks, idempotency tokens). 5. If repro fails to reproduce, escalate to scheduler + kernel owners with a seeded simulation trace and full telemetry bundle.

Developer guidance: safe coding patterns for handoff logic
- Always attach monotonic idempotency tokens to outbound token-write ops.
- Serialize kernel runtime state with a compact checksum field to quickly validate deserialized state.
- Prefer append-only handoff logs for state replay to avoid in-place mutation issues.
- Add defensive backoff and re-check logic for KV prefetch acknowledgements.

SLOs and impact budget for rollout
- Target: end-to-end p99 latency regression <= +10% during steady-state handoffs.
- Error budget: no more than 0.1% of requests may be retried due to handoff (counted toward service SLO).

Ownership and contact list
- Scheduler owner: Daniel Kim (daniel.k@redwood.ai)
- Kernel runtime owner: Priya Natarajan (priya.n@redwood.ai)
- Profiling & telemetry: Evan Martinez (evan.m@redwood.ai)
- SRE oncall: see rotation in /ops/oncall-schedule

Artifacts to produce after an experiment
- Experiment report (one-page): hypothesis, configuration, graphs, acceptance pass/fail, decision.
- Trace bundle: named <scenario>-<date>-<gid>.tar.gz uploaded to s3://redwood-sim-results/<team>/
- Hotfix or roll-forward plan if acceptance failed.

References and related material
- Kernel handoff decision tracing manual (link above)
- Compact trace annotation and causal latency map (profiling-playbook)
- Runtime decision graph and observed behaviour catalog

Appendix: example commands and JSON config
```
# simulate a graceful prefetch handoff scenario
./sim/run_experiment --scenario async-handoff --handoff-window 200ms --concurrency 200 --faults "none" --output s3://redwood-sim-results/handsoff-2026-03-02

# sample experiment config (JSON stored in /infra/experiments)
{
  "name": "async-handoff-graceful-200ms",
  "scenario": "async-handoff",
  "concurrency": 200,
  "handoff_window_ms": 200,
  "faults": [],
  "sampling": {
    "trace_sample_rate": 0.8
  }
}
```

Notes and future work
- Next iteration: integrate kernel-level snapshot diffs to reduce trace bundle sizes and speed up repro.
- Long term: bake this harness into CI gating for scheduler changes that touch handoff paths.

Change log
- 2026-01-12: Initial draft by Sofia Patel.
- 2026-02-05: Added acceptance table and telemetry alerts (reviewers Daniel, Priya).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized rollout procedure and emergency rollback steps (published).

End of document

  "scenario": "async-handoff",

  "faults": [],

Notes and future work
- Next iteration: integrate kernel-level snapshot diffs to reduce trace bundle sizes and speed up repro.
- Long term: bake this harness into CI gating for scheduler changes that touch handoff paths.

source.title: Runtime deployment circuit breakers and SLA fallback playbook
source.author: Maya Torres
source.content:
Purpose and scope:

This playbook documents the design and operational procedures for runtime deployment circuit breakers and SLA fallback strategies used by Redwood’s serving runtime. It standardizes automatic and manual interventions when serving infrastructure or model variants threaten customer SLOs. It is intended for oncall SREs, runtime, and platform engineers who operate rollouts, incident response, and automated safety systems.

Background and goals:

- Circuit breakers protect global and per-tenant SLAs by detecting overload or degraded-quality conditions and triggering well-defined fallback actions.
- Fallbacks preserve availability first (serve simpler/cheaper model variant), then correctness and cost.
- Safety goals: minimize user-visible errors, keep p99 under SLO where possible, and bound cost/regressions from automatic remediation.

Key concepts and signals:

- Primary signals: request_queue_depth, kernel_queue_time_ms (per-GPU dispatcher), per_route_p99_latency_ms, per_route_error_rate, gpu_memory_pressure_pct, token_generation_rate_rps, kvcache_miss_rate.
- Derived signals: fast_sla_breach (p99 > SLA threshold for 3 consecutive minutes), slow_tail_trend (p99 rising > 2x baseline over 30m), model_degradation_signal (quality regression from eval jobs or production shadowing).
- Circuit breaker states: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (blocking new traffic for offending route/model), HALF_OPEN (trial traffic allowed), FORCED_OPEN (manual lock until human intervention).

Default thresholds and recommended baseline configuration:

| scope | metric | threshold (default) | window | action priority |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| per-route | per_route_p99_latency_ms | 1200 ms | 3m | 1 (fast degrade) |
| per-route | error_rate | 5% | 3m | 2 (fallback variant) |
| node | gpu_memory_pressure_pct | 92% | 1m | 1 (evict/buffer) |
| cluster | request_queue_depth | > 50 per-gpu | 30s | 1 (shed/balance) |
| global | token_generation_rate_rps | > reserved_capacity * 1.15 | 1m | 3 (route to overflow pool) |

Notes: teams owning high‑latency workloads should lower p99 thresholds. Dedicated customers may have separate circuit-breaker profiles with stricter fail-open criteria.

Automatic remediation actions (ordered by invasiveness):

1) Fast degrade: reduce max_output_tokens or streaming chunk size to cut token generation tail.
2) Soft fallback: switch to a functionally compatible but cheaper/less-accurate model variant (e.g., gpt-like-7b -> gpt-like-3b-quant).
3) Rate shaping: apply per-token rate limit and backpressure responses (HTTP 429 with Retry-After).
4) Queue-shed and rebalance: evict low-priority requests and route new traffic to other regions or overflow pools.
5) Scale up: trigger autoscaling policies (burst capacity) if capacity remains available and within cost guardrails.
6) Circuit open: block new sessions for the offending route for the configured timeout and return degraded responses or explicit error codes.

Example circuit-breaker configuration (JSON) - what the runtime accepts:

{ "route": "text-generation/v1/compose", "conditions": [ { "metric": "per_route_p99_latency_ms", "operator": ">", "value": 1200, "window_s": 180 }, { "metric": "request_queue_depth", "operator": ">", "value": 50, "window_s": 30 } ], "actions": [ { "type": "fast_degrade", "params": { "max_output_tokens": 64 } }, { "type": "soft_fallback", "params": { "target_variant": "compose-v2-lite" } }, { "type": "open_circuit", "params": { "timeout_s": 300 } } ] }

(Operational note: the runtime merges route configs with customer and global policies. Use the CLI to preview effective config before activating.)

Safe rollout and canary guidance:

- Always deploy changes to circuit-breaker rules using a two-stage rollout: canary (1–3% traffic, 10–30m) -> progressive rollout (10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%) with automated health checks at each step.
- Canary health gates: p50/p95/p99 for the route, error rate, queue_depth, and kernel_cpu_util. Stop if fast_sla_breach or error_rate > threshold.
- Use the 'trial' HALF_OPEN mode for new rules where the breaker will only elevate to OPEN if a second independent signal (e.g., error_rate) also trips.

Canary checklist (quick):

1) Create canary config with explicit traffic weights in rollout plan.
2) Enable verbose tracing for the route for the canary window.
3) Schedule a platform observer (engineer oncall) and set PagerDuty suppression for noisy alerts.
4) Monitor canary for 30m; if stable, progress according to rollout plan.

Manual intervention and escalation steps (incident playbook):

1) Detect: Alerts from metric rules (see monitoring section). Pull the route dashboard and the kernel dispatcher view.
2) Triage: Identify whether the primary signal is resource (GPU/memory/queue) or model-quality (sudden quality regression).
3) Immediate mitigations (ordered):
   a) Enable fast_degrade for the route (CLI: redwoodctl runtime action apply --route <route> --action fast_degrade --params '{"max_output_tokens":64}').
   b) If resource pressure, trigger rebalance: redwoodctl cluster rebalance --scope route --target "overflow-pool".
   c) If degradation persists, enact soft_fallback: redwoodctl policy set --route <route> --fallback compose-v2-lite.
4) Open an incident and assign owner (runtime-sre). If customer is affected, notify Customer Success via the standard incident communication template.

Post-incident steps and diagnostics:

- Preserve traces and kernel profiles for the incident window (retain sampling at 100% for 30m).
- Run the regression triage checklist: correlate p99 spike with kernel scheduling delays, KV cache miss-rate, recent kernel rollouts, or model onboarding.
- If the circuit opened incorrectly (false positive), lower sensitivity and add a correlated-signal requirement to prevent recurrence.

Monitoring, dashboards and alerts (recommended rules):

- Dashboards: per-route health (p50/p95/p99 latencies, error rate, throughput), node resource view (GPU mem, utilization), kernel dispatcher queue depth, per-tenant ingress/egress.
- Alerts (examples):
   * ALERT: route-fast-sla-breach -> condition: per_route_p99 > SLA for 3m -> severity: P1 -> notify: runtime-oncall.
   * ALERT: high-queue-depth -> condition: request_queue_depth > 50 per-gpu for 30s -> severity P2 -> suppress if autoscale running.

Interpreting false positives and tuning knobs:

- Common false positive causes: short lived bursty workloads, improperly configured burst-capacity, and rapid client retries.
- Tuning knobs: window size, required correlated signals, sensitivity multiplier, allow-list of IPs/customers exempt from soft-open.

Automation safety guards and policy controls:

- All automated circuit-breaker actions are logged to the audit log with before/after config and stack traces of triggering events.
- Safety policy: automated open_circuit can only trigger if at least one non-human safety guard (e.g., memory_pressure OR kernel_queue_time) is present. Pure quality regressions must be validated by model-quality signals before automated circuit opening unless the error_rate is above 20%.
- Enterprise customers may opt-out of automated open_circuit actions; their contracts fall back to not-automatically-block but to soft_fallback and human review only.

Example incident runbook (step-by-step):

1) Pager fires: route-fast-sla-breach for text-generation/v1/compose.
2) Oncall: open route dashboard, confirm p99 > 1200ms for last 5m and queue_depth > 50.
3) Execute fast_degrade: redwoodctl runtime action apply --route text-generation/v1/compose --action fast_degrade --params '{"max_output_tokens":64}'
4) If < 2 minutes to improvement -> monitor 10m. If no improvement, enable soft_fallback to compose-v2-lite.
5) If still failing, open circuit as temporary measure (document reasoning in incident): redwoodctl circuit open --route text-generation/v1/compose --timeout 300 --reason "auto-incident mitigation"
6) Ensure customer comms and run postmortem after recovery.

Ownership and contact matrix:

- Primary owner: runtime-sre (pager: #runtime-oncall, lead: Ethan Park).
- Secondary: serving-runtime team (owner: Maya Torres).
- Escalation: platform-ops -> production-sre -> CTO on persistent outages > 3h.

Change control and review process:

- Any durable change to breaker rules must be submitted as a config PR, reviewed by runtime-sre and platform security, and staged through canary rollout.
- For high-impact routes (customer-facing or enterprise SLAs), require sign-off from product and CS before strong-open thresholds are lowered.

Appendix: useful CLI snippets and queries:

- Preview effective circuit config: redwoodctl runtime config preview --route text-generation/v1/compose
- Force a HALF_OPEN test: redwoodctl circuit test --route <route> --mode half_open --duration 60
- Query recent triggers (last 60m): redwoodctl events list --filter "event_type:circuit_triggered AND route:text-generation/v1/compose" --since 60m

References and related pages:

- See rollout tuning checklist for rollout-specific guidance: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/production-rollout-tuning-checklist-2026
- Tiered fallback architecture: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/tiered-fallback-routing-and-eviction-architecture-2026

Revision history:

- 2026-01-15: Created by Maya Torres (runtime safety draft).
- 2026-02-05: Added automated policy guards and enterprise opt-out notes (reviewers: Ethan Park).
- 2026-03-02: Finalized runbook steps, added CLI snippets and contact matrix.

source.title: Cooperative Preemption and Warm-Start Playbook for Latency-SLO Workloads
source.author: Ava Martinez
source.body:
Overview:
This playbook defines a cooperative preemption model and warm-start procedures for the Redwood serving runtime to preserve customer latency SLOs while maximizing GPU utilization. It consolidates scheduler signals, kernel boundary preemption, and KV/prefix cache warm-start techniques into an operationally safe procedure that on-call and platform engineers can follow during scale events, upgrades, and transient overloads.

Scope:
- Applies to GPU-backed serving nodes running the Redwood inference runtime (all major variants: hosted, dedicated, private).
- Focused on interactive text-generation and chat endpoints with strict P50/P95 latency SLOs (<= 50ms P50, <= 200ms P95 typical).
- Excludes bulk embedding/long-batch offline workloads except where co-located on shared pools (see co-location section).

Goals:
1. Maintain customer-visible latency SLOs during transient overloads and rolling upgrades.
2. Avoid noisy eviction cascades that cause repeated cold-starts and SLA regressions.
3. Provide deterministic, observable preemption behavior that is easy to reason about in postmortems.

Key concepts and components:
- Cooperative preemption: scheduler and runtime negotiate preemption at well-defined kernel boundaries instead of forced GPU context kills. This preserves partial KV state and allows graceful request migration.
- Preemption tokens: lightweight reservation tokens issued by the scheduling coordinator that carry preemption priority (urgent, standard, background) and preemption TTL.
- Warm-start checkpoints: serialized KV/prefix cache snapshots and minimal model context capture stored locally (ephemeral SSD) for fast rehydration after eviction.
- Eviction fencing: short quiesce windows (typically 50-200ms) to allow kernels to finish completions before eviction.

Architecture overview (short):
1) Request arrives at fronting gateway with priority header (derived from customer tier and endpoint config).
2) Scheduler maps request to a shard and provides a preemption token if eviction is possible.
3) Runtime accepts token and places request into a guarded execution lane that checks for preemption signals at kernel boundaries (attention-block, layer normalization boundary, kv-flush points).
4) If preemption is required, runtime quiesces new work, finishes the current micro-kernel, checkpoints minimal KV state, and returns mutable fallback or routes to warm node.

Decision table: when to preempt vs. when to deny new requests
| Condition | Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Node overloaded (GPU util > 95% for 5s) and incoming request priority = background | Reject with 429/backoff header | Background work should not destabilize interactive SLOs |
| Node overloaded and incoming request priority = standard | Try cooperative preemption: identify background lanes to evict | Preserves mid-tier throughput while protecting high-priority clients |
| Node overload + rolling upgrade drain | Graceful preempt with warm-start enabled | Avoids cold-cache penalties at the cost of short quiesce windows |

Operational parameters (recommended defaults):
- Kernel preemption check interval: 1-3 kernel boundaries per request (~every 8-16 transformer layers or 50-200ms for long decodes).
- Preemption fencing timeout: 200ms (must be < endpoint P95 target).
- KV warm-start snapshot cadence: on eviction and every 30s for long-lived sessions > 60s.
- Max concurrent warm-start restores: 4 per node to limit SSD contention.

Runbook: normal-scale preemption flow (engineer-facing)
1) Detect: alert fires when cluster-wide overload fraction > 10% and observed P95 > target for 2 minutes.
2) Evaluate: check agent dashboard: preemptible-lanes metric, current kernel preemption events, and SSD write queue depth.
3) Trigger soft-throttle: increase rejection probability for background priority by 20% via gateway feature flag.
4) If SLOs do not recover in 2 minutes, enable cooperative eviction on suspect nodes: set scheduler flag "enable_coop_preempt=true" for affected pool.
5) Monitor: watch metrics (listed below). If eviction errors or excessive cold-starts occur, roll back the flag immediately and open an incident.

Emergency eviction flow (when nodes must be drained for hardware or security patch):
1) Set graceful-drain=60s for maintenance windows, enable warm-start checkpoints.
2) Scheduler marks node as draining; new high-priority requests are migrated immediately, background lanes are queued for eviction.
3) Runtime performs ordered checkpointing of KV caches for active sessions and flushes ephemeral artifacts to local SSD with immediate replication to a standby within the AZ.
4) After checkpoint success or fencing timeout, scheduler migrates in-flight sessions to target nodes and resumes traffic.

KV cache warm-start details:
- What we save: minimal per-session KV ranges (start/end positions), per-segment attention state, and a 64-byte session manifest. Avoid saving full activations.
- Format: LZ4-compressed protobuf manifest + per-shard segment files. Example path: /var/lib/redwood/warmcheckpoints/<node>/<session-id>.lz4
- Restore time target: <= 150ms for a typical 1–3-layer prefix recomposition on NVMe SSDs. Test with the included microbenchmark (see commands).

Observability checklist (metrics & traces to inspect):
- preemption.events.count (by reason: load, drain, upgrade)
- preemption.latency-ms (time from preempt-signal to kernel quiesce)
- kv.checkpoint.size-bytes and kv.restore.latency-ms
- runtime.fallback-ratio (requests routed to fallback model or node)
- gateway.429-by-priority (measure rejecting behaviour)
- per-node ssd.write-ms and ssd.iops
- trace: follow a preemption trace (trace span name: runtime.preempt.sequence)

Table: Key thresholds (suggested)
| Metric | Warning | Critical |
|---|---:|---:|
| GPU util (5m avg) | 85% | 95% |
| preemption.latency-ms | 100ms | 300ms |
| kv.restore.latency-ms | 100ms | 400ms |
| ssd.write-ms | 10ms | 50ms |

Example CLI snippets and configs:
- Enable cooperative preemption for pool 'interactive-pool':
  $ rctl scheduler set-pool-config interactive-pool --enable-coop-preempt=true --preempt-fence-ms=200

- Trigger a manual warm checkpoint for node 'node-17':
  $ rctl runtime checkpoint node-17 --session all --force --dest /var/lib/redwood/warmcheckpoints

- YAML fragment (runtime config):
  runtime:
    preemption:
      enabled: true
      fence_ms: 200
      kernel_checkpoints_per_request: 2
    kv_cache:
      checkpoint_dir: "/var/lib/redwood/warmcheckpoints"
      snapshot_interval_s: 30
      max_simultaneous_restores: 4

Safety and failure modes to watch for:
- SSD saturation: frequent checkpoints can queue writes and slow restores, increasing cold-starts. Mitigation: reduce snapshot cadence or increase node-level restore concurrency cap.
- Eviction storm: misconfigured scheduler flags may evict too many sessions at once. Use staggered eviction windows and a global circuit breaker.
- Incomplete checkpoints: detect via checksum mismatches and abort migration; revert to fallback routing and alert SRE.

Testing and validation guidance:
- Microbenchmark to validate restore latency (run on target instance type):
  $ ./bench/warm-restore-bench --sessions 100 --concurrency 8 --checkpoint-dir /var/lib/redwood/warmcheckpoints
  Expected: 95th percentile restore latency < 150ms on NVMe-backed nodes.
- Chaos tests: simulate scheduler preemption toggles and measure SLO drift for 1h windows.
- A/B rollout: start with 5% of interactive-pool then 25% before global rollout. Watch runtime.fallback-ratio and preemption.latency histograms.

Operational play examples (short):
- Rolling upgrade with minimal SLO impact: enable coop preempt, drain old nodes with checkpointing, warm restore on new nodes, monitor P95.
- Sudden traffic spike: raise background rejection at gateway, enable coop preempt on overflow pool, activate autoscale burst if available.

Ownership and run-once checklist:
- Owner (primary): runtime-scheduling (pager: @runtime-scheduling)
- Secondary support: sres-serving, infra-storage for NVMe health
- Post-incident: complete Eviction & Warm-Start incident checklist and link to postmortem ID.

Appendix: quick reference links and artifacts:
- Runtime preemption sequence diagram (diagram id: diag-preempt-2026)
- Notebook: warm-restore-bench-readme.md (internal repo: infra/benchmarks/warm-restore)
- Related runbooks: numa-aware-shard-placement-eviction-playbook-2026 (for co-location policies)

If you are on-call and uncertain which path to take: prioritize keeping P95 within SLO. If cooperative preemption introduces regressions, disable it for the pool and revert to conservative rejection until a postmortem has been completed.
