|---|---:|---|---|
| reserve_slot | model_id, seq_len_est, ttl | Reserve a logical GPU slot for expected new sequence starts. Not a physical allocation. | Reject if host soft-oom or thermal throttling active. |
| prefetch_token | kv_key, priority, expected_live_ms | Start asynchronous KV prefetch and stage to warm cache. | Limit bytes prefetched per-second by host budget. |
| soft_evict | kv_key, reason | Mark entry for eviction; kernel may defer until safe window. | Respect in-flight references; must not kick out active session entries. |
| priority_boost | tenant_id, boost_level | Raise kernel scheduling priority for specific tenant's packets. | Must honor fairness quotas and not exceed token budget. |

Integration notes:
- Use request timeline annotations (trace_id + event_ids) when translating prefetch into kernel operations to permit traceability.
- All kernel APIs return an op_result and telemetry tag; Operators should emit both as structured logs.

Profiling & observability guidance

Required telemetry flows (minimum):
- Hint lifecycle traces: RIG:hint:generate -> Operator:hint:apply -> Kernel:hint:ack.
- Prefetch stats: bytes_initiated, bytes_completed, success_rate, tail_latency_p95/p99.
- Eviction surface: evict_attempts, evict_success, delayed_eviction_count, in_flight_blocker_reasons.

Sampling policy:
- 100% of hint lifecycle events must be annotated and sampled at 10% for full trace capture.
- Stratified sampling: always capture 100% when SLO > breach_threshold or when evictions coincide with model-swaps.

Profiling playbook excerpt (investigation steps):
1) Identify symptom: increased generation tail latency or token error rate.
2) Correlate with hint application windows (RIG/Operator traces).
3) If evictions increased: pull kvcache-backed references and examine in-flight reference counts.
4) If prefetch failures: check network I/O metric (prefetch throughput) and per-host prefetch budget exhaustion.
5) Deploy a controlled canary: disable prefetch for a single tenant cohort and compare SLA slices; report delta to RIG.

Benchmarking protocol (how to validate changes safely)

- Objective: measure delta in per-token cost, tail latency, and cache hit ratio after any change to hint heuristics or Operator enforcement.
- Minimum benchmark matrix:
  - Workloads: short-chat (mean seq_len 32), long-chat (mean seq_len 512), embedding-bulk (batch-size 128), mixed-burst (poisson arrivals lambda=200).
  - Metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens-per-second, cache_hit_rate, kernel_preemption_count, host_cpu_percent, GPU_util.
  - Duration: each profile run = 2h steady-state after 20m warmup. Repeat 3x and report median.

Bench commands (example):
- Synthesize traffic: ./benchctl --profile mixed-burst --tenant test-tenant --duration 02:00 --seed 42
- Collect traces: benchctl collect --trace-filter "RIG:hint* OR Operator:hint*" --out bench-2026-02-collect.json

Release & rollout guidance

- All Operator changes must pass a green gate on the benchmark matrix above with no >5% regression in p95 for the short-chat workload across three replicate runs.
- Rollout strategy: staged canary by affinity cohort (10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100%) with automated rollback on >3s sustained p95 regression or 2x increase in eviction-induced request failures.
- Release note template: include exact benchmark artifact links, canary cohort ids, and throttling parameters.

Known issues (current summary)

| ID | Symptom | Impact | Workaround | Owner |
| KVO-2025-11 | Prefetch storms on model swap causing network saturation | Elevated tail latency for embedding workloads | Stagger prefetch by 2s buckets via RIG hint windowing | eng-serving-runtime |
| OP-2025-07 | Operator sometimes double-applies soft_evict after transient disconnect | Elevated evict_attempts and unnecessary cache churn | Blacklist consecutive soft_evicts within 500ms per-kv | kernel-team |
| THERM-2025-02 | Thermal-induced GPU throttles reject reserve_slot calls | Rejection leads to rapid fallback to lower-capacity kernels | Preemptively lower prefetch budget when thermal_throttle > 0 | infra-sre |

Eviction & prefetch runbook (operational playbook)

When customers report latency regressions possibly tied to prefetch/eviction:
1) Triage: capture traces across RIG, Operator, and kernel for the suspect timeframe.
2) Check host budgets: run `rigctl host-status --host <node>` and inspect prefetch_budget and eviction_backlog.
3) If prefetch_budget_pressure > 80%: temporarily reduce RIG prefetch window for the affected cohort: `rigctl hint-update --cohort <id> --prefetch-window-ms 200`
4) If eviction churn > 100/min: enable soft-eviction-mitigation mode on Operator: `operatorctl set soft-evict-delay 1200` (ms).
5) If step 3–4 restore SLOs: schedule a follow-up bench run and root-cause analysis. If not, escalate to oncall kernel-team.

Escalation matrix

- Oncall runtime (pager): Triage and apply immediate mitigations.
- Kernel team (pager): For kernel API misbehavior or invariant violations.
- Infra SRE: For network, thermal, or host-level resource saturation.

Ownership and maintenance

- RIG control plane: eng-serving-runtime/core-routing (primary).
- Operator agent: eng-serving-runtime/operator (primary); kernel-team (secondary for API changes).
- Bench harness and artifact storage: benchmarking-team.

Appendix: sample Operator config (yaml)

```yaml
operator:
  prefetch:
    enabled: true
    max_bytes_per_sec: 250000000 # 250MB/s
    per_tenant_limit: 50MB
  eviction:
    soft_evict_delay_ms: 1000
    max_concurrent_evictions: 16
  safety:
    slo_throttle_threshold_pct: 10
    telemetry_sampling_pct: 10
```

FAQ and change log

- Q: Why are hints advisory not mandatory?
  A: Kernels have richer context about in-flight requests and memory graphs; advisory model preserves safety and prevents hard failures.

Change log (high level):
- 2024-11-12 initial draft and prototype Operator integration.
- 2025-04-03 added prefetch budget enforcement and hint versioning.
- 2026-02-18 added canary gating rules and expanded benchmark matrix.

Related reading

- See runtime-execution-safety-matrix-2026 for safety invariants.
- See kvcache-epoch-synchronization-and-repair-procedures-2026 for epoch reconciliation procedures.

If you implement or modify the Operator or RIG, update this playbook and attach benchmark artifacts to the release notes before rolling past 30% canary.
text:
Summary

This page documents the design, operational contract, and runbooks for the Runtime Integration Gateway (RIG) and the Operator component used by Redwood’s serving runtime. The RIG is the control plane facade that mediates decisions between the API surface, routing layer, and the kernel scheduler. The Operator is the local agent that implements prefetch/eviction hints, capacity decisions, and safety checks on a per-host basis. Together they enable consistent fallback behavior, cache coordination, and safe kernel handoffs across deployments (hosted, dedicated, private).

Scope

- Cross-cutting: runtime architecture, kernel scheduling hints, profiling instrumentation, benchmark protocols, evacuation/eviction runbooks, and release guidance.
- Intended audience: runtime engineers, SREs, oncall, platform/ops teams, and partners owning Private deployments.

Design overview

Key goals:
- Preserve latency SLOs while maximizing throughput and cache hit ratios.
- Provide safe, observable prefetch and eviction signals to kernels without exposing internal kernel details.
- Offer a deterministic handshake for model swap, graceful degradation, and fast rollback.

Core components:
1) Runtime Integration Gateway (RIG): global control plane component that synthesizes routing policy, historical telemetry, and fidelity budgets to produce per-tenant hints (prefetch score, target warmup priority, eviction budget).
2) Operator agent: host-local agent that receives hints and enforces them via kernel scheduler APIs and kvcache controls.
3) Kernel Scheduler: GPU-local execution layer that exposes a narrow set of safe primitives (reserve_slot, prefetch_token, soft_evict, priority_boost).

Behavioral contract (high level)

- Hints are advisory: Operator must never trigger kernel state transitions that break in-flight requests. All transitions are atomic and checkpointed.
- Backpressure loop: if Operator observes a >10% SLO degradation coincident with hint enforcement, it must throttle hint application and report a delta to RIG.
- Revisioning: every hint includes a version token; kernels reject stale tokens.

Kernel scheduling integration patterns

Recommended primitive semantics (stable API):

| Primitive | Inputs | Expected behavior | Safety checks |
|---|---:|---|---|
| reserve_slot | model_id, seq_len_est, ttl | Reserve a logical GPU slot for expected new sequence starts. Not a physical allocation. | Reject if host soft-oom or thermal throttling active. |
| prefetch_token | kv_key, priority, expected_live_ms | Start asynchronous KV prefetch and stage to warm cache. | Limit bytes prefetched per-second by host budget. |
| soft_evict | kv_key, reason | Mark entry for eviction; kernel may defer until safe window. | Respect in-flight references; must not kick out active session entries. |
| priority_boost | tenant_id, boost_level | Raise kernel scheduling priority for specific tenant's packets. | Must honor fairness quotas and not exceed token budget. |

Integration notes:
- Use request timeline annotations (trace_id + event_ids) when translating prefetch into kernel operations to permit traceability.
- All kernel APIs return an op_result and telemetry tag; Operators should emit both as structured logs.

Profiling & observability guidance

Required telemetry flows (minimum):
- Hint lifecycle traces: RIG:hint:generate -> Operator:hint:apply -> Kernel:hint:ack.
- Prefetch stats: bytes_initiated, bytes_completed, success_rate, tail_latency_p95/p99.
- Eviction surface: evict_attempts, evict_success, delayed_eviction_count, in_flight_blocker_reasons.

Sampling policy:
- 100% of hint lifecycle events must be annotated and sampled at 10% for full trace capture.
- Stratified sampling: always capture 100% when SLO > breach_threshold or when evictions coincide with model-swaps.

Profiling playbook excerpt (investigation steps):
1) Identify symptom: increased generation tail latency or token error rate.
2) Correlate with hint application windows (RIG/Operator traces).
3) If evictions increased: pull kvcache-backed references and examine in-flight reference counts.
4) If prefetch failures: check network I/O metric (prefetch throughput) and per-host prefetch budget exhaustion.
5) Deploy a controlled canary: disable prefetch for a single tenant cohort and compare SLA slices; report delta to RIG.

Benchmarking protocol (how to validate changes safely)

- Objective: measure delta in per-token cost, tail latency, and cache hit ratio after any change to hint heuristics or Operator enforcement.
- Minimum benchmark matrix:
  - Workloads: short-chat (mean seq_len 32), long-chat (mean seq_len 512), embedding-bulk (batch-size 128), mixed-burst (poisson arrivals lambda=200).
  - Metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens-per-second, cache_hit_rate, kernel_preemption_count, host_cpu_percent, GPU_util.
  - Duration: each profile run = 2h steady-state after 20m warmup. Repeat 3x and report median.

Bench commands (example):
- Synthesize traffic: ./benchctl --profile mixed-burst --tenant test-tenant --duration 02:00 --seed 42
- Collect traces: benchctl collect --trace-filter "RIG:hint* OR Operator:hint*" --out bench-2026-02-collect.json

Release & rollout guidance

- All Operator changes must pass a green gate on the benchmark matrix above with no >5% regression in p95 for the short-chat workload across three replicate runs.
- Rollout strategy: staged canary by affinity cohort (10% -> 30% -> 60% -> 100%) with automated rollback on >3s sustained p95 regression or 2x increase in eviction-induced request failures.
- Release note template: include exact benchmark artifact links, canary cohort ids, and throttling parameters.

Known issues (current summary)

| ID | Symptom | Impact | Workaround | Owner |
| KVO-2025-11 | Prefetch storms on model swap causing network saturation | Elevated tail latency for embedding workloads | Stagger prefetch by 2s buckets via RIG hint windowing | eng-serving-runtime |
| OP-2025-07 | Operator sometimes double-applies soft_evict after transient disconnect | Elevated evict_attempts and unnecessary cache churn | Blacklist consecutive soft_evicts within 500ms per-kv | kernel-team |
| THERM-2025-02 | Thermal-induced GPU throttles reject reserve_slot calls | Rejection leads to rapid fallback to lower-capacity kernels | Preemptively lower prefetch budget when thermal_throttle > 0 | infra-sre |

Eviction & prefetch runbook (operational playbook)

When customers report latency regressions possibly tied to prefetch/eviction:
1) Triage: capture traces across RIG, Operator, and kernel for the suspect timeframe.
2) Check host budgets: run `rigctl host-status --host <node>` and inspect prefetch_budget and eviction_backlog.
3) If prefetch_budget_pressure > 80%: temporarily reduce RIG prefetch window for the affected cohort: `rigctl hint-update --cohort <id> --prefetch-window-ms 200`
4) If eviction churn > 100/min: enable soft-eviction-mitigation mode on Operator: `operatorctl set soft-evict-delay 1200` (ms).
5) If step 3–4 restore SLOs: schedule a follow-up bench run and root-cause analysis. If not, escalate to oncall kernel-team.

Escalation matrix

- Oncall runtime (pager): Triage and apply immediate mitigations.
- Kernel team (pager): For kernel API misbehavior or invariant violations.
- Infra SRE: For network, thermal, or host-level resource saturation.

Ownership and maintenance

- RIG control plane: eng-serving-runtime/core-routing (primary).
- Operator agent: eng-serving-runtime/operator (primary); kernel-team (secondary for API changes).
- Bench harness and artifact storage: benchmarking-team.

Appendix: sample Operator config (yaml)

```yaml
operator:
  prefetch:
    enabled: true
    max_bytes_per_sec: 250000000 # 250MB/s
    per_tenant_limit: 50MB
  eviction:
    soft_evict_delay_ms: 1000
    max_concurrent_evictions: 16
  safety:
    slo_throttle_threshold_pct: 10
    telemetry_sampling_pct: 10
```

FAQ and change log

- Q: Why are hints advisory not mandatory?
  A: Kernels have richer context about in-flight requests and memory graphs; advisory model preserves safety and prevents hard failures.

Change log (high level):
- 2024-11-12 initial draft and prototype Operator integration.
- 2025-04-03 added prefetch budget enforcement and hint versioning.
- 2026-02-18 added canary gating rules and expanded benchmark matrix.

Related reading

- See runtime-execution-safety-matrix-2026 for safety invariants.
- See kvcache-epoch-synchronization-and-repair-procedures-2026 for epoch reconciliation procedures.

If you implement or modify the Operator or RIG, update this playbook and attach benchmark artifacts to the release notes before rolling past 30% canary.

source.title: Runtime change impact matrix & experimentation cookbook
source.author: Alyssa Chen
source.body:
Overview:

This document defines a single, consistent process for assessing the operational impact of changes to the serving runtime and for running controlled experiments (feature gates, kernel rollouts, scheduler tuning, quantization profiles, and routing policies). It combines a lightweight impact matrix with an experimentation cookbook and the minimal runbook snippets required to triage regressions introduced by change. The goal is to reduce blast radius, speed detection, and make rollbacks predictable.

Scope:
- Applies to changes that modify: runtime binary (C++/CUDA), kernel selection mapping, scheduler policy, KV cache behavior, batching heuristics, and routing/fallback rules.
- Excludes purely client-side SDK changes or docs-only updates (those follow a different lightweight process).

Who should use this:
- Release engineers and runtime owners for planned rollouts
- SREs on-call during canaries and business hours
- Performance engineers running microbenchmarks and cost simulations

Owners and Contacts:
- Runtime owner: Alyssa Chen (runtime@redwood.example.com)
- Scheduler lead: Diego Morales
- SRE oncall escalation: pager RUNTIME_PGM

Change impact matrix (summary):

| Change class                         | Primary subsystems affected                 | Risk vector(s)                                     | SLOs to watch                         | Suggested canary scope                  | Rollback threshold (alert-driven)        |
|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Kernel mapping or new kernel binary  | kernel scheduler, GPU dispatcher, telemetry | increased tail latency, OOM, kernel hangs         | P99 latency < 250ms; error rate <0.1% | 1% traffic, single region, dev tenants  | P99 > 350ms for 5m OR error% > 0.5%      |
| Scheduler policy change              | request router, admission control, metrics  | priority inversion, head-of-line blocking         | P95 latency < 150ms; queue depth SLAs | 5% traffic, shadowing only               | sustained queue growth > 30%            |
| KV cache eviction/format change      | kvcache, model swap, warm start             | cold-start regressions, model consistency issues  | cold-start P90 < 400ms                 | internal tenants only, off-peak hours    | cold-start P90 > 800ms                  |
| Quantization profile (exec change)   | kernel execution paths, memory footprint     | accuracy regressions, per-token latency increase  | embedding similarity drift < 0.02      | batch replay + 0.5% production traffic   | quality alert (eval) OR latency > 2x    |
| Routing/fallback policy              | routing, model catalog, tracing             | oscillation, request misrouted, capacity overrun  | availability 99.9%                     | canary on non-critical routes            | availability < 99.7% OR oscillation >3x |

Notes:
- SLOs here are global defaults. Product teams may define tighter per-route objectives; include those in the rollout ticket.
- Canary scope should prefer low-risk tenants (internal or dev).

Experimentation cookbook (step-by-step):

1) Plan & impact assessment
   - Create a runtime change card in Jira with: change summary, binary/flag, owner, rollback plan, required observability, required benchmark and eval suites to run.
   - Fill the light-weight impact matrix (use the table above as a template).
   - Required artifacts: perf microbenchmark results, unit tests, regression eval with golden prompts (for quantization or model changes).

2) Lab validation (developer sandbox)
   - Run isolation microbenchmarks from the perf-suite (see section "Benchmarks to run").
   - Run deterministic unit-level CUDA reproducer if applicable.
   - Run the policy through synthetic load generator using the "operational-workload-simulator" harness.

3) Canary (staged)
   - Canary configs: 1% of traffic, single region, low-variance time window (preferably off-peak).
   - Enable verbose telemetry at canary endpoints: kernel traces, kvcache hit rates, scheduler queue depth, per-request decision trace id.
   - Monitor these dashboards: Runtime-P99-Latency, Kernel-Error-Rate, Scheduler-Queue-Depth, Canary-Health.
   - Run automated smoke checks every 30s (example PromQL provided below).

4) Evaluate canary
   - Wait for at least 30 minutes of steady traffic or a minimum of 10k requests (whichever is larger).
   - Evaluate: latency P50/P95/P99, error rate, queue depth percentile, memory growth, GPU SM utilization, and regression eval if applicable.
   - If any rollback threshold breached (see matrix), execute rollback immediately and file an incident.

5) Rollout to broader population
   - If canary passes, increase scope to 10% for one hour, then 30% for two hours, then full rollout.
   - At each step, confirm no regressions and that rollbacks are wired and tested.

6) Post-rollout verification
   - Run the full benchmark suite and a dedicated quality eval within 24h.
   - Update release notes template (see below) and mark the change as stable.

Benchmarks to run (minimum set) :
- Microbench: per-kernel latency (single-threaded) across sequence lengths [8,64,256,1024] on target instance types.
- Mixed workload SLO simulation: run the mixed-workload simulator with tenant mix matching last 7d production sample.
- Burst SLO test: synthetic bursts to validate admission control and backpressure.
- Token-cost regression: end-to-end token cost comparison vs baseline.

Example PromQL smoke checks (copy into canary job):
- Kernel error rate (30s): sum(rate(runtime_kernel_errors_total[30s])) / sum(rate(runtime_requests_total[30s]))
- P99 latency (1m): histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket[1m])) by (le))
- Scheduler queue depth (1m avg): avg(runtime_scheduler_queue_depth)

Profiling and telemetry checklist during rollout:
- Enable sampling rate to at least 10% for compact traces; ramp to 30% if small regressions are suspected.
- Attach kernel-level timestamps and cuda-kernel ids to traces.
- Collect a small set of full-trace captures (5–10 requests) for failed or slow requests.
- Ensure perf counters (SM utilization, memory bw) and NVLink error counters are surfaced to dashboards.

Release notes template (to be included in the change ticket):
- Title: short summary (single sentence)
- Change id: runtime/commit/hash or flag name
- Jenkins/CI build: link + artifact hash
- Canaries: date/time, scope, passing checks
- Benchmarks: key numbers (P95/P99 vs baseline, token cost delta)
- Known limitations: any outstanding caveats and owners

Runbook snippets (quick triage):

Symptom A: sudden P99 spike (>100% increase) shortly after enabling change
1) Look up canary decision trace IDs and correlate kernel ids.
2) Check kernel error rate query (above) and GPU watchdog alerts.
3) Capture 5 slow traces and compare kernel execution time per segment.
4) If cause is kernel hang or long SM stall, trigger rollback and file incident.

Symptom B: queue depth steadily grows while throughput flatlines
1) Check scheduler policy change flag and admission control metrics.
2) Inspect top-N slow requests by route and trace decision path.
3) If root cause is priority inversion, roll back scheduler change; otherwise tune admission settings.

Symptom C: spike in cold-start latency after KV cache change
1) Validate kvcache hit ratio and warmup hooks.
2) Run canary replay for warmstart paths and compare warmup traces to baseline.
3) If eviction policy regressions found, revert to previous retention and run a targeted warmup job.

Known issues and detection signatures (current):
- Intermittent kernel-side deadlock on Ampere+ 80GB nodes when executing fused attention path under large batch shapes. Signature: occasional 100% SM utilization but zero progress at host level; kernel_timeouts increased. Workaround: disable fused attention for affected kernel patch; fix scheduled for next kernel release.
- KVcache shard skew when tenant affinity mixing changes: detection signature is per-tenant warmup time drift > 3x baseline and increased model-swap events. Workaround: enforce soft-affinity during canaries.

Telemetry queries and dashboards (examples):
- Basic canary dashboard widgets:
  - Request P50/P95/P99 over time (per model variant)
  - Kernel error rate and error types (OOM, kernel-crash, watchdog)
  - Scheduler queue depth histogram (per priority class)
  - KVcache hit rate and average warmup time
- Suggested alerting thresholds:
  - Canary-P99>350ms sustained 5m -> Sev2
  - Kernel error rate >0.5% for 5m -> Sev1
  - Queue growth > 30% for 10m -> Sev2
Example canary CLI steps (for runtime binary + flag rollouts):
```
# build and upload runtime artifact
bazel build //runtime:server --config=release
artifacts upload ./bazel-bin/runtime/server.tar.gz --tag runtime/2026-02-18

# create canary rollout with 1% traffic
redwoodctl rollout create --service runtime --artifact runtime/2026-02-18 --scope canary --percent 1 --region us-west-2 --flag enable-new-sched=true

# watch logs and metrics
redwoodctl rollout watch --id <rollout-id>
```

Quality eval example (for quantization changes):
- Run golden prompt set (1000 prompts) and compute: exact-match, BLEU if applicable, and embedding drift (cosine) with baseline. Alert if embedding mean drift > 0.02 or exact-match drops > 1.5%.

Postmortem and learning loop:
- If a rollback occurs, create an incident review within 72h describing: what changed, what triggered detection, timeline, root cause, and at least two action items (one for technical fix, one for process improvement).

Appendix A — Small impact matrix (CSV for ticket copy-paste):
ChangeClass,Subsystems,PrimaryRisk,SLOToWatch,CanaryScope,RollbackCondition
KernelBinary,kernel-scheduler;gpu-dispatcher,tail-latency;oom,P99<250ms,1%/dev,P99>350ms or error%>0.5%
SchedulerPolicy,router;admission-control,queue-growth,P95<150ms,5%/shadow,queue_growth>30%
KVCache,kvcache;model-swap,cold-starts,coldstart_P90<400ms,internal-only,coldstart_P90>800ms
QuantProfile,kernel-exec;memory,quality-drift;latency,embedding_drift<0.02,batch-replay,eval_alert_or_latency>2x
Routing,router;catalog,availability;oscillation,availability>99.9%,non-critical_routes,availability<99.7%

Appendix B — Links and references:
- Runtime decision graph (internal): /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Continuous profiling strategy: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/continuous-runtime-profiling-strategy-2026
- Mixed-workload simulator: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/mixed-workload-slo-simulation-suite-2026

If you have feedback on this cookbook or find missing telemetry hooks, please tag the runtime team and create an improvement ticket (label: runtime-rollout-improvement).

6) Post-rollout verification
   - Run the full benchmark suite and a dedicated quality eval within 24h.
   - Update release notes template (see below) and mark the change as stable.

Benchmarks to run (minimum set) :
- Microbench: per-kernel latency (single-threaded) across sequence lengths [8,64,256,1024] on target instance types.
- Mixed workload SLO simulation: run the mixed-workload simulator with tenant mix matching last 7d production sample.
- Burst SLO test: synthetic bursts to validate admission control and backpressure.
- Token-cost regression: end-to-end token cost comparison vs baseline.

Example PromQL smoke checks (copy into canary job):
- Kernel error rate (30s): sum(rate(runtime_kernel_errors_total[30s])) / sum(rate(runtime_requests_total[30s]))
- P99 latency (1m): histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket[1m])) by (le))
- Scheduler queue depth (1m avg): avg(runtime_scheduler_queue_depth)

Profiling and telemetry checklist during rollout:
- Enable sampling rate to at least 10% for compact traces; ramp to 30% if small regressions are suspected.
- Attach kernel-level timestamps and cuda-kernel ids to traces.
- Collect a small set of full-trace captures (5–10 requests) for failed or slow requests.
- Ensure perf counters (SM utilization, memory bw) and NVLink error counters are surfaced to dashboards.

Release notes template (to be included in the change ticket):
- Title: short summary (single sentence)
- Change id: runtime/commit/hash or flag name
- Jenkins/CI build: link + artifact hash
- Canaries: date/time, scope, passing checks
- Benchmarks: key numbers (P95/P99 vs baseline, token cost delta)
- Known limitations: any outstanding caveats and owners

Runbook snippets (quick triage):

Symptom A: sudden P99 spike (>100% increase) shortly after enabling change
1) Look up canary decision trace IDs and correlate kernel ids.
2) Check kernel error rate query (above) and GPU watchdog alerts.
3) Capture 5 slow traces and compare kernel execution time per segment.
4) If cause is kernel hang or long SM stall, trigger rollback and file incident.

Symptom B: queue depth steadily grows while throughput flatlines
1) Check scheduler policy change flag and admission control metrics.
2) Inspect top-N slow requests by route and trace decision path.
3) If root cause is priority inversion, roll back scheduler change; otherwise tune admission settings.

Symptom C: spike in cold-start latency after KV cache change
1) Validate kvcache hit ratio and warmup hooks.
2) Run canary replay for warmstart paths and compare warmup traces to baseline.
3) If eviction policy regressions found, revert to previous retention and run a targeted warmup job.

Known issues and detection signatures (current):
- Intermittent kernel-side deadlock on Ampere+ 80GB nodes when executing fused attention path under large batch shapes. Signature: occasional 100% SM utilization but zero progress at host level; kernel_timeouts increased. Workaround: disable fused attention for affected kernel patch; fix scheduled for next kernel release.
- KVcache shard skew when tenant affinity mixing changes: detection signature is per-tenant warmup time drift > 3x baseline and increased model-swap events. Workaround: enforce soft-affinity during canaries.

Telemetry queries and dashboards (examples):
- Basic canary dashboard widgets:
  - Request P50/P95/P99 over time (per model variant)
  - Kernel error rate and error types (OOM, kernel-crash, watchdog)
  - Scheduler queue depth histogram (per priority class)
  - KVcache hit rate and average warmup time
- Suggested alerting thresholds:
  - Canary-P99>350ms sustained 5m -> Sev2
  - Kernel error rate >0.5% for 5m -> Sev1
  - Queue growth > 30% for 10m -> Sev2
Example canary CLI steps (for runtime binary + flag rollouts):
```
# build and upload runtime artifact
bazel build //runtime:server --config=release
artifacts upload ./bazel-bin/runtime/server.tar.gz --tag runtime/2026-02-18

# create canary rollout with 1% traffic
redwoodctl rollout create --service runtime --artifact runtime/2026-02-18 --scope canary --percent 1 --region us-west-2 --flag enable-new-sched=true

# watch logs and metrics
redwoodctl rollout watch --id <rollout-id>
```

Quality eval example (for quantization changes):
- Run golden prompt set (1000 prompts) and compute: exact-match, BLEU if applicable, and embedding drift (cosine) with baseline. Alert if embedding mean drift > 0.02 or exact-match drops > 1.5%.

Postmortem and learning loop:
- If a rollback occurs, create an incident review within 72h describing: what changed, what triggered detection, timeline, root cause, and at least two action items (one for technical fix, one for process improvement).

Appendix A — Small impact matrix (CSV for ticket copy-paste):
ChangeClass,Subsystems,PrimaryRisk,SLOToWatch,CanaryScope,RollbackCondition
KernelBinary,kernel-scheduler;gpu-dispatcher,tail-latency;oom,P99<250ms,1%/dev,P99>350ms or error%>0.5%
SchedulerPolicy,router;admission-control,queue-growth,P95<150ms,5%/shadow,queue_growth>30%
KVCache,kvcache;model-swap,cold-starts,coldstart_P90<400ms,internal-only,coldstart_P90>800ms
QuantProfile,kernel-exec;memory,quality-drift;latency,embedding_drift<0.02,batch-replay,eval_alert_or_latency>2x
Routing,router;catalog,availability;oscillation,availability>99.9%,non-critical_routes,availability<99.7%

Appendix B — Links and references:
- Runtime decision graph (internal): /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
- Continuous profiling strategy: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/profiling-playbook/continuous-runtime-profiling-strategy-2026
- Mixed-workload simulator: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/mixed-workload-slo-simulation-suite-2026

If you have feedback on this cookbook or find missing telemetry hooks, please tag the runtime team and create an improvement ticket (label: runtime-rollout-improvement).
text:
Overview:

This document defines a single, consistent process for assessing the operational impact of changes to the serving runtime and for running controlled experiments (feature gates, kernel rollouts, scheduler tuning, quantization profiles, and routing policies). It combines a lightweight impact matrix with an experimentation cookbook and the minimal runbook snippets required to triage regressions introduced by change. The goal is to reduce blast radius, speed detection, and make rollbacks predictable.

Scope:
- Applies to changes that modify: runtime binary (C++/CUDA), kernel selection mapping, scheduler policy, KV cache behavior, batching heuristics, and routing/fallback rules.
- Excludes purely client-side SDK changes or docs-only updates (those follow a different lightweight process).

Who should use this:
- Release engineers and runtime owners for planned rollouts
- SREs on-call during canaries and business hours
- Performance engineers running microbenchmarks and cost simulations

Owners and Contacts:
- Runtime owner: Alyssa Chen (runtime@redwood.example.com)
- Scheduler lead: Diego Morales
- SRE oncall escalation: pager RUNTIME_PGM

Change impact matrix (summary):

| Change class                         | Primary subsystems affected                 | Risk vector(s)                                     | SLOs to watch                         | Suggested canary scope                  | Rollback threshold (alert-driven)        |
|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Kernel mapping or new kernel binary  | kernel scheduler, GPU dispatcher, telemetry | increased tail latency, OOM, kernel hangs         | P99 latency < 250ms; error rate <0.1% | 1% traffic, single region, dev tenants  | P99 > 350ms for 5m OR error% > 0.5%      |
| Scheduler policy change              | request router, admission control, metrics  | priority inversion, head-of-line blocking         | P95 latency < 150ms; queue depth SLAs | 5% traffic, shadowing only               | sustained queue growth > 30%            |
| KV cache eviction/format change      | kvcache, model swap, warm start             | cold-start regressions, model consistency issues  | cold-start P90 < 400ms                 | internal tenants only, off-peak hours    | cold-start P90 > 800ms                  |
| Quantization profile (exec change)   | kernel execution paths, memory footprint     | accuracy regressions, per-token latency increase  | embedding similarity drift < 0.02      | batch replay + 0.5% production traffic   | quality alert (eval) OR latency > 2x    |
| Routing/fallback policy              | routing, model catalog, tracing             | oscillation, request misrouted, capacity overrun  | availability 99.9%                     | canary on non-critical routes            | availability < 99.7% OR oscillation >3x |

Notes:
- SLOs here are global defaults. Product teams may define tighter per-route objectives; include those in the rollout ticket.
- Canary scope should prefer low-risk tenants (internal or dev).

Experimentation cookbook (step-by-step):

1) Plan & impact assessment
   - Create a runtime change card in Jira with: change summary, binary/flag, owner, rollback plan, required observability, required benchmark and eval suites to run.
   - Fill the light-weight impact matrix (use the table above as a template).
   - Required artifacts: perf microbenchmark results, unit tests, regression eval with golden prompts (for quantization or model changes).

2) Lab validation (developer sandbox)
   - Run isolation microbenchmarks from the perf-suite (see section \"Benchmarks to run\").
   - Run deterministic unit-level CUDA reproducer if applicable.
   - Run the policy through synthetic load generator using the \"operational-workload-simulator\" harness.

3) Canary (staged)
   - Canary configs: 1% of traffic, single region, low-variance time window (preferably off-peak).
   - Enable verbose telemetry at canary endpoints: kernel traces, kvcache hit rates, scheduler queue depth, per-request decision trace id.
   - Monitor these dashboards: Runtime-P99-Latency, Kernel-Error-Rate, Scheduler-Queue-Depth, Canary-Health.
   - Run automated smoke checks every 30s (example PromQL provided below).

4) Evaluate canary
   - Wait for at least 30 minutes of steady traffic or a minimum of 10k requests (whichever is larger).
   - Evaluate: latency P50/P95/P99, error rate, queue depth percentile, memory growth, GPU SM utilization, and regression eval if applicable.
   - If any rollback threshold breached (see matrix), execute rollback immediately and file an incident.

5) Rollout to broader population
   - If canary passes, increase scope to 10% for one hour, then 30% for two hours, then full rollout.
   - At each step, confirm no regressions and that rollbacks are wired and tested.

6) Post-rollout verification
   - Run the full benchmark suite and a dedicated quality eval within 24h.
   - Update release notes template (see below) and mark the change as stable.

Benchmarks to run (minimum set) :
- Microbench: per-kernel latency (single-threaded) across sequence lengths [8,64,256,1024] on target instance types.
- Mixed workload SLO simulation: run the mixed-workload simulator with tenant mix matching last 7d production sample.
- Burst SLO test: synthetic bursts to validate admission control and backpressure.
- Token-cost regression: end-to-end token cost comparison vs baseline.

Example PromQL smoke checks (copy into canary job):
- Kernel error rate (30s): sum(rate(runtime_kernel_errors_total[30s])) / sum(rate(runtime_requests_total[30s]))
- P99 latency (1m): histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(runtime_request_duration_seconds_bucket[1m])) by (le))
- Scheduler queue depth (1m avg): avg(runtime_scheduler_queue_depth)

Profiling and telemetry checklist during rollout:
- Enable sampling rate to at least 10% for compact traces; ramp to 30% if small regressions are suspected.
- Attach kernel-level timestamps and cuda-kernel ids to traces.
- Collect a small set of full-trace captures (5–10 requests) for failed or slow requests.
- Ensure perf counters (SM utilization, memory bw) and NVLink error counters are surfaced to dashboards.

Release notes template (to be included in the change ticket):
- Title: short summary (single sentence)
- Change id: runtime/commit/hash or flag name
- Jenkins/CI build: link + artifact hash
- Canaries: date/time, scope, passing checks
- Benchmarks: key numbers (P95/P99 vs baseline, token cost delta)
- Known limitations: any outstanding caveats and owners

Runbook snippets (quick triage):

Symptom A: sudden P99 spike (>100% increase) shortly after enabling change
1) Look up canary decision trace IDs and correlate kernel ids.
2) Check kernel error rate query (above) and GPU watchdog alerts.
3) Capture 5 slow traces and compare kernel execution time per segment.
4) If cause is kernel hang or long SM stall, trigger rollback and file incident.

Symptom B: queue depth steadily grows while throughput flatlines
1) Check scheduler policy change flag and admission control metrics.
2) Inspect top-N slow requests by route and trace decision path.
3) If root cause is priority inversion, roll back scheduler change; otherwise tune admission settings.

Symptom C: spike in cold-start latency after KV cache change
1) Validate kvcache hit ratio and warmup hooks.
2) Run canary replay for warmstart paths and compare warmup traces to baseline.
3) If eviction policy regressions found, revert to previous retention and run a targeted warmup job.

Known issues and detection signatures (current):
- Intermittent kernel-side deadlock on Ampere+ 80GB nodes when executing fused attention path under large batch shapes. Signature: occasional 100% SM utilization but zero progress at host level; kernel_timeouts increased. Workaround: disable fused attention for affected kernel patch; fix scheduled for next kernel release.
- KVcache shard skew when tenant affinity mixing changes: detection signature is per-tenant warmup time drift > 3x baseline and increased model-swap events. Workaround: enforce soft-affinity during canaries.

Telemetry queries and dashboards (examples):
- Basic canary dashboard widgets:
  - Request P50/P95/P99 over time (per model variant)
  - Kernel error rate and error types (OOM, kernel-crash, watchdog)
  - Scheduler queue depth histogram (per priority class)
  - KVcache hit rate and average warmup time
- Suggested alerting thresholds:
  - Canary-P99>350ms sustained 5m -> Sev2
  - Kernel error rate >0.5% for 5m -> Sev1
  - Queue growth > 30% for 10m -> Sev2
Example canary CLI steps (for runtime binary + flag rollouts):
```
# build and upload runtime artifact
bazel build //runtime:server --config=release
artifacts upload ./bazel-bin/runtime/server.tar.gz --tag runtime/2026-02-18

# create canary rollout with 1% traffic
redwoodctl rollout create --service runtime --artifact runtime/2026-02-18 --scope canary --percent 1 --region us-west-2 --flag enable-new-sched=true
```

ChangeClass,Subsystems,PrimaryRisk,SLOToWatch,CanaryScope,RollbackCondition
KernelBinary,kernel-scheduler;gpu-dispatcher,tail-latency;oom,P99<250ms,1%/dev,P99>350ms or error%>0.5%
SchedulerPolicy,router;admission-control,queue-growth,P95<150ms,5%/shadow,queue_growth>30%

Appendix B — Links and references:
- Runtime decision graph (internal): /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-decision-graph-and-observed-behaviour-catalog-2026
